topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT863 - COGNITIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus for **PERUT863 – Cognitive Robotics**, **Module 2** builds upon the foundational concepts of embodiment, agent architecture, and the sense–think–act loop introduced in Module 1. This module specifically focuses on the **perceptual, cognitive, and learning machinery** that allows a robot to *interpret*, *reason about*, and *adapt to* its environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cognitive Robotics – Module 2 Core Theme:**
> The module is centred on **how a robot perceives the world, represents knowledge, reasons under uncertainty, and learns from experience** — collectively termed the **Perception–Cognition–Learning (PCL) Stack**.

Formally, **Module 2 – Overview** can be defined as:

> **Definition (KTU 2024):** A structured study of the computational and mathematical frameworks that enable a robotic agent to acquire multimodal sensory data, transform it into symbolic or subsymbolic representations, perform reasoning and planning under uncertainty, and continuously improve its behaviour through learning.

The scope of the module spans the following **four pillars**:

| Pillar # | Domain | Core Question Answered |
|----------|--------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Robot Perception** | "What is around the robot?" |
| 2 | **Knowledge Representation** | "What does the robot know and how is it stored?" |
| 3 | **Reasoning & Planning** | "What should the robot do next?" |
| 4 | **Learning & Adaptation** | "How does the robot improve over time?" |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Robot as a Curious Child

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine teaching a 3-year-old child to navigate a busy kitchen. The child uses eyes (vision), ears (sound), and touch (force feedback) to *perceive*. The child builds an internal mental map of cupboards, fridge, and stove (knowledge). When asked to fetch a glass, the child thinks, "I have seen glasses on the third shelf before, so I will move there" (reasoning). After bumping into a chair, the child remembers to avoid that spot next time (learning). A cognitive robot does **exactly the same thing**, but with cameras, LIDAR, knowledge bases, probabilistic reasoning engines, and neural networks.

This **child-in-kitchen** analogy maps directly onto the four pillars of Module 2:

- **Perception** → Eyes, ears, skin of the child → Cameras, microphones, tactile sensors of the robot.
- **Knowledge Representation** → Mental map in the brain → Semantic maps, ontologies, neural embeddings.
- **Reasoning** → "Third shelf had glasses" → Probabilistic inference, symbolic logic, planning algorithms.
- **Learning** → Avoid the chair next time → Reinforcement learning, supervised/unsupervised training, online adaptation.

## 1.3 Why Module 2 Matters — The Industrial & Research Motivation

The shift from **pre-programmed industrial robots** (repetitive tasks in cages) to **cognitive robots** (collaborative, mobile, adaptive) is one of the defining transitions of **Industry 4.0 / 5.0**. Module 2 equips the B.Tech engineer with the conceptual and mathematical tools to design this new generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Industry Highlights:**
> - The global **cognitive robotics market** is projected to exceed **USD 18 billion by 2030** with a CAGR > **35 %**.
> - Core enabling technologies — **sensor fusion, SLAM, Bayesian inference, deep learning, and reinforcement learning** — all sit inside Module 2.
> - Reference architectures such as **ROS 2**, **NVIDIA Isaac**, and **Google RT-2** are direct industrial implementations of the PCL stack.

## 1.4 The Sense–Think–Learn–Act Loop (Recap with Module 2 Emphasis)

Building on Module 1's control loop, Module 2 injects **cognition** between *Sense* and *Act*:

```
Sense → [Perception] → [Knowledge] → [Reasoning/Planning] → [Learning] → Act
```

The **Learning** block (highlighted in Module 2) closes the loop over time, making the agent **adaptive** rather than purely reactive.

## 1.5 Module 2 Learning Outcomes (Mapped to KTU Course Outcomes)

Upon completing this module, the student should be able to:

- **CO2.1** — Identify and classify the principal sensors used in cognitive robots and articulate their operating principles.
- **CO2.2** — Construct formal knowledge representations (logical, probabilistic, and neural) for robotic domains.
- **CO2.3** — Apply Bayesian reasoning and decision-theoretic planning under uncertainty.
- **CO2.4** — Differentiate between supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning paradigms in robotics.
- **CO2.5** — Evaluate the performance of perception and learning pipelines using standard metrics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars — Operational Breakdown

### Pillar 1 — Robot Perception (Sub-modules 2.1 & 2.2)

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Signal Acquisition:** Raw analogue/digital signals are captured from **proprioceptive sensors** (encoders, IMUs, force/torque sensors) and **exteroceptive sensors** (cameras, LIDAR, ultrasonic, depth sensors).
- **Step 2 — Pre-processing:** Noise filtering, calibration, and synchronisation across modalities. Standard operations include Gaussian smoothing, edge detection (Canny, Sobel), and outlier rejection (RANSAC).
- **Step 3 — Feature Extraction:** Salient descriptors (SIFT, SURF, ORB for vision; FPFH for point clouds) are computed to compactly represent the data.
- **Step 4 — Sensor Fusion:** Multi-modal data is combined using techniques ranging from the **Kalman Filter** (linear Gaussian) to the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** and **Particle Filter** for non-linear/non-Gaussian systems.
- **Step 5 — World Model Update:** The fused representation is registered into the robot's internal map (occupancy grid, semantic map, or neural latent space).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why perception matters:** Without accurate perception, downstream reasoning and planning operate on **garbage data**. A 1° error in camera calibration can translate to a **30 cm positional error** at 20 m distance.

### Pillar 2 — Knowledge Representation (Sub-module 2.3)

Three dominant paradigms are studied:

1. **Symbolic (Logic-based):** First-Order Logic (FOL), Description Logics (DL), ontologies (OWL/RDF). Example: $\text{Cup}(x) \land \text{OnShelf}(x, k) \Rightarrow \text{Fragile}(x)$.
2. **Probabilistic (Graphical Models):** Bayesian Networks, Markov Random Fields, Dynamic Bayesian Networks (DBNs).
3. **Subsymbolic (Neural/Embedding):** Vector embeddings, knowledge graphs, large language model (LLM) representations.

### Pillar 3 — Reasoning & Planning Under Uncertainty (Sub-module 2.4)

- **Symbolic Reasoning:** Forward/backward chaining, theorem proving, SAT solvers.
- **Probabilistic Reasoning:** Bayesian inference, Maximum A Posteriori (MAP), Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE).
- **Planning:** Classical (A*, D*), decision-theoretic (MDP, POMDP), and sampling-based (RRT, PRM).

### Pillar 4 — Learning & Adaptation (Sub-module 2.5)

- **Supervised Learning:** Regression, classification (e.g., object recognition).
- **Unsupervised Learning:** Clustering, dimensionality reduction, autoencoders.
- **Reinforcement Learning (RL):** Q-learning, Deep Q-Networks (DQN), Policy Gradient, PPO.
- **Imitation & Few-shot Learning:** Learning from demonstrations, meta-learning.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for Module 2. Memorise the **forms** of each equation, the **boundary conditions**, and the **typical units**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Meaning | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---------|--------------------|---------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem (posterior update) | $P(H \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(D)}$ | $P(H \mid D)$: posterior, $P(D \mid H)$: likelihood, $P(H)$: prior, $P(D)$: evidence | Probabilities are unitless in $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | Total Probability | $P(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(D \mid H_i) P(H_i)$ | Sum of mutually exclusive hypotheses $H_i$ | Unitless, $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | Gaussian / Normal PDF | $\mathcal{N}(x;\mu,\sigma^2) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\mu$: mean, $\sigma^2$: variance, $\sigma$: std. dev. | $x \in \mathbb{R}$, $\sigma > 0$ |
| 4 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | $p(x_i)$: probability mass function | Unit: **bits** (or **nats** if $\ln$) |
| 5 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L_{CE} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | $y_i$: true label (one-hot), $\hat{y}_i$: predicted prob. | Unitless, $\geq 0$ |
| 6 | Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $N$: number of samples | Squared units of target |
| 7 | KL Divergence | $D_{KL}(P \,\Vert\, Q) = \sum_{i} P(i)\log\dfrac{P(i)}{Q(i)}$ | Measures difference between distributions $P$ and $Q$ | $\geq 0$, unitless |
| 8 | Kalman Filter Prediction | $\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = A\hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + Bu_k$ | $A$: state transition, $B$: control input matrix | State dimension dependent |
| 9 | Kalman Filter Covariance Update | $P_{k \mid k-1} = A P_{k-1 \mid k-1} A^{T} + Q$ | $Q$: process noise covariance | Positive semi-definite matrix |
| 10 | Kalman Gain | $K_k = P_{k \mid k-1} H^{T} (H P_{k \mid k-1} H^{T} + R)^{-1}$ | $H$: observation matrix, $R$: measurement noise cov. | Matrix, dimensionless |
| 11 | State Update | $\hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k(z_k - H\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1})$ | $z_k$: measurement vector | State dimension dependent |
| 12 | PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $e(t)$: error, $K_p,K_i,K_d$: gains | $K_p$: V/rad, $K_i$: V/(rad·s), $K_d$: V·s/rad |
| 13 | Camera Pinhole Projection | $x = f \dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $f$: focal length, $(X,Y,Z)$: world point | $x,y$ in pixels; $f$ in mm or px |
| 14 | LIDAR Range Equation | $R_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 P_{min}}}$ | $P_t$: transmit power, $\sigma$: target cross-section | Distance in metres |
| 15 | Bellman Optimality (RL) | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a}\left[R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) V^{*}(s')\right]$ | $R$: reward, $\gamma$: discount $\in [0,1)$ | $V^{*}$: value, units of reward |
| 16 | Q-Learning Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[r + \gamma\max_{a'}Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)\right]$ | $\alpha$: learning rate $\in (0,1]$, $r$: reward | $Q$: action-value function |
| 17 | Softmax (Action Selection) | $P(a_i \mid s) = \dfrac{e^{Q(s,a_i)/\tau}}{\sum_j e^{Q(s,a_j)/\tau}}$ | $\tau$: temperature parameter | Probabilities $\in [0,1]$ |
| 18 | Occupancy Grid Update | $l_{t}(m) = l_{t-1}(m) + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(m, z_t) - l_0$ | $l$: log-odds, $z_t$: measurement | Log-odds $\in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 19 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\Vert\mathbf{u}\Vert \Vert\mathbf{v}\Vert}$ | $\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}$: embedding vectors | Range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 20 | F1 Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Combines precision and recall | Range $[0, 1]$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** In the table above, $\Vert \mathbf{u}\Vert$ represents the **norm** (vertical bars), not the markdown table pipe. LaTeX commands `\Vert` are used to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Pillar | Industrial Application |
|--------|------------------------|
| Perception | Autonomous vehicles (Tesla, Waymo), surgical robots (da Vinci), drone navigation |
| Knowledge Representation | Digital twins in manufacturing, warehouse robotics (Amazon Kiva), medical diagnosis |
| Reasoning & Planning | Space exploration rovers (NASA Perseverance), disaster-response robots |
| Learning & Adaptation | Adaptive grasping (Google DeepMind), locomotion (ANYbotics), human–robot collaboration |

## 2.4 Mathematical Foundations Recap

The mathematical bedrock of Module 2 rests on **three pillars of mathematics**:

1. **Probability & Statistics** (Bayesian inference, stochastic processes)
2. **Linear Algebra** (vector spaces, matrix decompositions, SVD, PCA)
3. **Calculus & Optimisation** (gradients, Lagrange multipliers, convex optimisation)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Approximately **70 % of Module 2 numerical problems** involve either (a) a Bayes' update, (b) a Kalman filter step, or (c) a Bellman equation evaluation. Master these three patterns first.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

### Goal
Derive Bayes' theorem from the definition of **conditional probability**.

### Starting Definition

The **conditional probability** of event $A$ given event $B$ is:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

where $P(B) > 0$.

### Step 1 — Express Joint Probability Two Ways

By symmetry of the intersection operator:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(B \cap A)
$$

Applying the conditional probability definition to both:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)
$$

$$
P(B \cap A) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

### Step 2 — Equate the Two Expressions

$$
P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Desired Posterior

Dividing both sides by $P(B)$:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Standard Bayesian Terminology

Renaming $A \to H$ (hypothesis) and $B \to D$ (data):

$$
P(H \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(D)}
$$

### Step 5 — Expand the Evidence via Total Probability

If $\{H_1, H_2, \dots, H_n\}$ is a partition of the sample space:

$$
P(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(D \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)
$$

Hence the **expanded form**:

$$
P(H_j \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(D \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions:** Posterior $\in [0, 1]$; the denominator normalises over all hypotheses; if $P(D) = 0$, the posterior is undefined.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Kalman Filter Recursion

### Goal
Derive the iterative Kalman filter update equations for linear Gaussian systems.

### State and Measurement Models

$$
x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)
$$

$$
z_k = H x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)
$$

where $x_k$ is the state vector, $z_k$ the measurement, $w_k, v_k$ are zero-mean Gaussian noise.

### Step 1 — A Priori State Estimate (Prediction)

Taking expectations of the state model:

$$
\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + B u_k
$$

### Step 2 — A Priori Error Covariance (Prediction)

The prediction error $e_{k \mid k-1} = x_k - \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}$ propagates as:

$$
P_{k \mid k-1} = A P_{k-1 \mid k-1} A^{T} + Q
$$

### Step 3 — Innovation (Measurement Residual)

$$
\tilde{y}_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}
$$

This represents the **difference between the actual measurement and what the model predicted**.

### Step 4 — Innovation Covariance

$$
S_k = H P_{k \mid k-1} H^{T} + R
$$

### Step 5 — Optimal Kalman Gain

Minimise the posterior error covariance $\text{tr}(P_{k \mid k})$. Setting the derivative to zero:

$$
K_k = P_{k \mid k-1} H^{T} S_k^{-1} = P_{k \mid k-1} H^{T} (H P_{k \mid k-1} H^{T} + R)^{-1}
$$

### Step 6 — Posterior State Update (Correction)

$$
\hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k \tilde{y}_k
$$

### Step 7 — Posterior Covariance Update (Joseph form for numerical stability)

$$
P_{k \mid k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k \mid k-1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** The Kalman Filter is **optimal only for linear Gaussian systems**. For non-linear systems, use the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** with linearisation via Jacobians, or the **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** which uses the unscented transform.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bellman Optimality Equation

### Goal
Derive the recursive Bellman equation that underlies all of reinforcement learning.

### Markov Decision Process (MDP) Definition

An MDP is a tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ where:

- $\mathcal{S}$: set of states.
- $\mathcal{A}$: set of actions.
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$: transition probability.
- $R(s, a)$: reward function.
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$: discount factor.

### Step 1 — Define the Return

The discounted return starting from time $t$ is:

$$
G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k})
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Value Function

The state-value function under policy $\pi$ is the expected return:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[G_t \mid s_t = s\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Expectation One Step

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[R(s, a) + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Law of Iterated Expectations

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[R(s, a) + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1}=s']\right]
$$

### Step 5 — Recognise the Recursive Structure

Since $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid s_{t+1}=s'] = V^{\pi}(s')$:

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\left[R(s, a) + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')\right]
$$

This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation**.

### Step 6 — Optimise Over Policies

The optimal value function takes the maximum over actions:

$$
V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[R(s, a) + \gamma V^{*}(s')\right]
$$

This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation**.

### Step 7 — Define the Q-Function

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\left[R(s, a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions:** If $\gamma = 0$, the agent is myopic (greedy). If $\gamma \to 1$, the agent values future rewards nearly equally. For any finite MDP with bounded rewards and $\gamma < 1$, $V^{*}$ exists and is unique.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Concepts to Equations

| Concept | Foundational Equation | Application in Robotics |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Sensor Uncertainty | $P(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(z; Hx, R)$ | LIDAR / camera noise modelling |
| State Estimation | Kalman filter recursion | SLAM, drone localisation |
| Decision Making | $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)$ | Behaviour selection, grasping |
| Policy Improvement | $\pi' = \arg\max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}[Q^{\pi}(s, a)]$ | RL training loop |
| Map Update | $l_t(m) = l_{t-1}(m) + \text{inv\_model}(m, z_t) - l_0$ | Occupancy grid SLAM |
| Visual Feature Match | $\mathbf{d}_{ij} = \Vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mathbf{f}_j \Vert_2$ | Loop closure in V-SLAM |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Bayesian Update for a Robot)

> **Problem:** A warehouse robot uses a vision module to identify boxes. The prior probability that a detected object is a *fragile* box is $P(F) = 0.2$. The vision sensor has a true positive rate $P(+\mid F) = 0.95$ and a false positive rate $P(+\mid \neg F) = 0.10$. The sensor reports **positive**. Compute $P(F \mid +)$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given values:**
$P(F) = 0.2$, $P(+\mid F) = 0.95$, $P(+\mid \neg F) = 0.10$.

**Step 2 — Compute the complement of the prior:**
$P(\neg F) = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8$.

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' theorem:**

$$
P(F \mid +) = \dfrac{P(+\mid F) \cdot P(F)}{P(+\mid F) P(F) + P(+\mid \neg F) P(\neg F)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
P(F \mid +) = \dfrac{(0.95)(0.2)}{(0.95)(0.2) + (0.10)(0.8)}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
(0.95)(0.2) = 0.190
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
(0.95)(0.2) + (0.10)(0.8) = 0.190 + 0.080 = 0.270
$$

**Step 7 — Final posterior:**

$$
P(F \mid +) = \dfrac{0.190}{0.270} = 0.7037 \approx 0.704
$$

**Interpretation:** Upon receiving a *positive* sensor reading, the robot's belief that the object is fragile rises from **20 % (prior)** to **70.4 % (posterior)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Module 2 — Perception–Cognition–Learning (PCL) Stack

The diagram below illustrates the **end-to-end PCL architecture** that defines the scope of Module 2. It traces data flow from raw sensor acquisition through to adaptive action selection.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Physical World Environment"] --> nodeB["Sensor Suite"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Signal Preprocessing and Calibration"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Feature Extraction Layer"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Sensor Fusion and State Estimation"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["World Model and Knowledge Base"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Probabilistic and Symbolic Reasoning Engine"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Planner and Decision Module"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Action Selection and Motor Control"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Actuators and Robot Body"]
    nodeJ --> nodeA
    nodeF --> nodeK["Learning Module"]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeG
    nodeK --> nodeF
    nodeK --> nodeH

    classDef sensor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef perception fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef cognition fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef learning fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeJ sensor
    class nodeC,nodeD,nodeE perception
    class nodeF,nodeG,nodeH cognition
    class nodeI action
    class nodeK learning
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Top-down arrow flow (clockwise):** Physical world → sensors → perception → cognition → action → physical world (the primary control loop).
- **Bottom feedback arrows:** The **Learning Module** observes the outputs of the *Knowledge Base*, the *Planner*, and the *Reasoning Engine*, then updates them in turn — closing the **meta-cognitive loop**.

## 4.2 Subsystem Interaction Map — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following block diagram details the **functional interactions** between the four pillars, which is a typical KTU-style architecture question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PerceptionPillar["Pillar 1 - Perception"]
        nodeP1["Proprioceptive Sensors"]
        nodeP2["Exteroceptive Sensors"]
        nodeP3["Multi Modal Fusion"]
    end

    subgraph KnowledgePillar["Pillar 2 - Knowledge"]
        nodeK1["Symbolic Layer OWL and FOL"]
        nodeK2["Probabilistic Layer Bayesian Net"]
        nodeK3["Subsymbolic Layer Neural Embeddings"]
    end

    subgraph ReasoningPillar["Pillar 3 - Reasoning and Planning"]
        nodeR1["Bayesian Inference Engine"]
        nodeR2["Symbolic Logic Solver"]
        nodeR3["Planner MDP and RRT"]
    end

    subgraph LearningPillar["Pillar 4 - Learning"]
        nodeL1["Supervised Module"]
        nodeL2["Reinforcement Learner"]
        nodeL3["Self Supervised Adapter"]
    end

    nodeP3 --> nodeK1
    nodeP3 --> nodeK2
    nodeP3 --> nodeK3
    nodeK1 --> nodeR2
    nodeK2 --> nodeR1
    nodeK3 --> nodeL1
    nodeR1 --> nodeR3
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3
    nodeR3 --> nodeL2
    nodeL2 --> nodeK2
    nodeL1 --> nodeK3
    nodeL3 --> nodeP3

    classDef pFill fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef kFill fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef rFill fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef lFill fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class nodeP1,nodeP2,nodeP3 pFill
    class nodeK1,nodeK2,nodeK3 kFill
    class nodeR1,nodeR2,nodeR3 rFill
    class nodeL1,nodeL2,nodeL3 lFill
```

## 4.3 Learning Taxonomy for Cognitive Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeM["Machine Learning for Robots"] --> nodeS["Supervised Learning"]
    nodeM --> nodeU["Unsupervised Learning"]
    nodeM --> nodeR["Reinforcement Learning"]
    nodeM --> nodeI["Imitation and Meta Learning"]

    nodeS --> nodeS1["Object Classification"]
    nodeS --> nodeS2["Pose Estimation"]
    nodeS --> nodeS3["Grasp Success Prediction"]

    nodeU --> nodeU1["Scene Segmentation"]
    nodeU --> nodeU2["Anomaly Detection"]
    nodeU --> nodeU3["Representation Learning"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** — *"Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors in a cognitive robot. Give two examples of each."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition of proprioceptive sensors (1 mark):** Sensors that measure the **internal state** of the robot itself — joint angles, wheel velocities, battery voltage, motor current. Examples: rotary encoders, inertial measurement units (IMUs).
- **Definition of exteroceptive sensors (1 mark):** Sensors that measure the **external environment** relative to the robot — distance to obstacles, ambient light, sound, temperature. Examples: cameras, LIDAR, ultrasonic rangefinders, GPS.
- **Significance for cognition (1 mark):** Cognitive robots require *both* — proprioception enables self-monitoring and closed-loop control, while exteroception enables environmental awareness, mapping, and human–robot interaction.

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** — *"State and explain Bayes' theorem. Why is it central to cognitive robotics?"* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Statement of Bayes' theorem (1 mark):** $P(H \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H) \, P(H)}{P(D)}$, where $H$ is the hypothesis, $D$ is the observed data, $P(H \mid D)$ is the posterior, $P(D \mid H)$ the likelihood, $P(H)$ the prior, and $P(D)$ the evidence.
- **Explanation (1 mark):** It provides a principled mathematical framework for **updating beliefs** about the state of the world (e.g., object identity, robot pose) in light of new sensor evidence.
- **Why central to cognitive robotics (1 mark):** Robot sensors are noisy and incomplete. Bayes' theorem allows the robot to **fuse prior knowledge with sensor measurements** in a mathematically optimal way, forming the foundation of SLAM, object recognition, and decision-making under uncertainty.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Module 2, Q1(a)(b) Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Perception–Cognition–Learning (PCL) architecture** of a cognitive robot. Explain the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Kalman filter recursive update equations** from the linear Gaussian state and measurement models. Clearly state the prediction and correction steps. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

- **Block diagram of the PCL stack (2 marks):** Draw a labelled block diagram showing (i) Sensor Suite → (ii) Pre-processing → (iii) Feature Extraction → (iv) Sensor Fusion → (v) Knowledge Base → (vi) Reasoning Engine → (vii) Planner → (viii) Action Module → (ix) Actuators, with a feedback path labelled *Learning Module* returning to (v)–(vii).
- **Explanation of Sensor Suite (1 mark):** Combines proprioceptive (encoders, IMU) and exteroceptive (camera, LIDAR, ultrasonic) sensors to capture multimodal data.
- **Explanation of Pre-processing and Feature Extraction (1 mark):** Noise filtering, calibration, and salient descriptor computation (e.g., SIFT, ORB).
- **Explanation of Sensor Fusion and State Estimation (1 mark):** Combines multiple sensor streams into a single consistent estimate of robot and world state (e.g., via Kalman filter).
- **Explanation of Knowledge Base (1 mark):** Stores symbolic, probabilistic, and subsymbolic representations for reasoning.
- **Explanation of Reasoning, Planning, and Learning loop (1 mark):** Reasoning selects inferences, planning generates action sequences, and the learning module updates the knowledge base and policies based on experience.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

- **State the linear Gaussian models (1 mark):**

$$
x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)
$$

$$
z_k = H x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)
$$

- **Prediction step — state estimate (1 mark):**

$$
\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + B u_k
$$

- **Prediction step — covariance (1 mark):**

$$
P_{k \mid k-1} = A P_{k-1 \mid k-1} A^{T} + Q
$$

- **Innovation and innovation covariance (1 mark):**

$$
\tilde{y}_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}
$$

$$
S_k = H P_{k \mid k-1} H^{T} + R
$$

- **Optimal Kalman gain derivation (2 marks):** Minimise $\text{tr}(P_{k \mid k})$ by setting $\partial / \partial K = 0$:

$$
K_k = P_{k \mid k-1} H^{T} (H P_{k \mid k-1} H^{T} + R)^{-1}
$$

- **Correction step — state and covariance (1 mark):**

$$
\hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k \tilde{y}_k
$$

$$
P_{k \mid k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k \mid k-1}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (b):**
> - Students commonly **lose 1–2 marks** by omitting the **noise covariance terms** $Q$ and $R$ in the model equations.
> - A second frequent error: writing $H P H^T + R$ instead of the full inverse form $K = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$.
> - Always specify that the Kalman filter is **optimal only under linear Gaussian assumptions** — failure to state this can cost 1 mark.
> - A fully labelled diagram (prediction/correction loop) earns **1 additional clarity mark** in the KTU answer script.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Module 2, Q2(a)(b) Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain **three paradigms of knowledge representation** in cognitive robotics with suitable examples. Compare their strengths and weaknesses. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A service robot must decide whether to **approach a human** or **wait** at a junction. Formulate this as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** and derive the **Bellman optimality equation** for the optimal policy. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

- **Symbolic (logic-based) representation (2 marks):** Uses First-Order Logic (FOL), Description Logics, and ontologies (OWL/RDF). Example: $\text{Robot}(x) \land \text{At}(x, \text{kitchen}) \Rightarrow \text{CanGrasp}(x, \text{cup})$. **Strength:** interpretable, verifiable, formal reasoning. **Weakness:** brittle to noise, hard to encode uncertainty.
- **Probabilistic (graphical models) representation (2 marks):** Uses Bayesian networks, Markov random fields, hidden Markov models. Example: a network encoding $P(\text{intent} \mid \text{gaze}, \text{gesture}, \text{context})$. **Strength:** handles uncertainty elegantly. **Weakness:** computationally expensive for large state spaces.
- **Subsymbolic (neural) representation (2 marks):** Uses vector embeddings produced by deep networks. Example: CLIP embeddings of objects, LLM embeddings of natural-language commands. **Strength:** scalable, handles raw high-dimensional data. **Weakness:** opaque (black box), hard to verify.
- **Comparison summary (1 mark):** Symbolic methods offer interpretability, probabilistic methods handle uncertainty, and subsymbolic methods scale to high-dimensional perception. Modern cognitive robots often use **hybrid neurosymbolic architectures** combining all three.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

- **MDP formulation — tuple definition (1 mark):** $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ with:
  - States: $s \in \{\text{safe\_junction}, \text{human\_near}, \text{obstacle\_detected}\}$.
  - Actions: $a \in \{\text{approach}, \text{wait}\}$.
  - Transition probabilities: $P(s' \mid s, a)$.
  - Reward: $R(s, a) = +10$ for safe approach, $-20$ for collision, $-1$ for each wait step.
  - Discount factor: $\gamma \in [0, 1)$.
- **Define return and value function (1 mark):**

$$
G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R(s_{t+k}, a_{t+k}), \quad V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid s_t = s]
$$

- **Expand expectation using law of total probability (1 mark):**

$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) [R(s, a) + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]
$$

- **Take the maximum over actions (1 mark):**

$$
V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) [R(s, a) + \gamma V^{*}(s')]
$$

- **Define Q-function and write Bellman optimality in Q-form (1 mark):**

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) [R(s, a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')]
$$

- **Derive optimal policy (1 mark):**

$$
\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^{*}(s, a)
$$

- **Boundary condition and interpretation (1 mark):** For $\gamma < 1$ and bounded rewards, $V^{*}$ exists uniquely. The optimal policy tells the robot to *wait* when the human is close and uncertainty is high, and to *approach* only when the safety constraints are satisfied with high confidence.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (b):**
> - Students frequently **omit the discount factor** $\gamma$ or treat it as $1$ — this loses 1 mark.
> - A common error is writing $\max$ *inside* the expectation rather than *outside* — the correct position is **outside** the sum over $s'$ as shown.
> - Not stating the **boundary condition** ($\gamma < 1$, bounded rewards) costs a half-mark.
> - The MDP tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ must be explicitly listed; partial formulation loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Overview**

### The Four Pillars
- **Perception:** proprioceptive + exteroceptive sensors → preprocessing → feature extraction → sensor fusion (Kalman / Particle) → world model.
- **Knowledge Representation:** symbolic (FOL/OWL) · probabilistic (Bayesian nets) · subsymbolic (neural embeddings).
- **Reasoning & Planning:** Bayesian inference, symbolic logic, MDPs/POMDPs, RRT/PRM.
- **Learning & Adaptation:** supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement, imitation/meta.

### Top 10 Equations (Must Memorise)
1. Bayes' theorem: $P(H \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H) P(H)}{P(D)}$.
2. Gaussian PDF: $\mathcal{N}(x;\mu,\sigma^2) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$.
3. Shannon entropy: $H(X) = -\sum_i p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$.
4. Cross-entropy loss: $L_{CE} = -\sum_i y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$.
5. Kalman state prediction: $\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1} + B u_k$.
6. Kalman gain: $K = P H^{T}(H P H^{T} + R)^{-1}$.
7. Camera pinhole: $x = f \cdot X / Z$, $y = f \cdot Y / Z$.
8. Bellman optimality: $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a}\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma V^{*}(s')]$.
9. Q-learning: $Q \leftarrow Q + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'}Q' - Q]$.
10. F1 score: $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$.

### Key Conceptual Differentiations
- **Proprioceptive vs. Exteroceptive** sensors.
- **Symbolic vs. Subsymbolic** knowledge representation.
- **Bayesian vs. Frequentist** inference.
- **Supervised vs. Reinforcement** learning (labelled data vs. reward signals).
- **Model-based vs. Model-free** RL.
- **MDP vs. POMDP** (full vs. partial observability).

### Common Pitfalls
- Confusing **prior** and **posterior** in Bayes' theorem.
- Applying the Kalman filter to **non-linear systems without linearisation**.
- Forgetting the **$\gamma < 1$** assumption in Bellman equations.
- Writing `|` (markdown table pipe) inside LaTeX expressions (use `\vert` or `\mid` instead).
- Omitting the noise covariance matrices $Q$ and $R$ in Kalman filter derivations.

### Module 2 High-Yield Industrial Buzzwords
- SLAM (Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping).
- Sensor fusion.
- Neurosymbolic AI.
- Digital twins.
- Foundation models for robotics (e.g., RT-2, PaLM-E).
- Human–Robot Collaboration (HRC).
- Imitation learning and inverse RL.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always (i) **define** the concept in one sentence, (ii) **state** the governing equation, (iii) **derive or apply** with explicit steps, (iv) **interpret** the result in the robotic context, and (v) add a **neat labelled diagram** wherever possible. KTU examiners reward *clarity, structure, and diagrams* as much as mathematical correctness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Desirable Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT863 - COGNITIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Desirable Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cognitive Robotics**, the term **"Desirable Features"** refers to the foundational behavioural, architectural, and computational characteristics that a robotic agent must possess in order to be classified as a *cognitive robot* rather than a purely reactive, pre-programmed automaton. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for PERUT863, these features define the boundary between conventional industrial robotics and the next generation of intelligent, embodied autonomous agents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Desirable features of a cognitive robot are those essential properties such as perception, reasoning, learning, memory, autonomy, attention, natural interaction, and goal-directed behaviour that enable a robot to operate intelligently in unstructured, dynamic, and human-shared environments.*

These features collectively enable a robot to perform the **sense → think → act** cycle in a continuous, adaptive loop without requiring explicit human intervention for every decision.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a traditional **automatic washing machine** versus a **human housekeeper**:

- The **washing machine** follows a fixed programme. It does not perceive the type of cloth, the level of dirt, or whether a pet is sitting inside. It is **reactive**.
- The **housekeeper** looks at the clothes, smells them, decides the detergent, remembers past stains, plans the order of work, and learns over time. She is **cognitive**.

A cognitive robot must behave more like the **housekeeper** than the washing machine. The "desirable features" are precisely the qualities that make this leap from automation to cognition possible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 tests whether the student can *list, explain, and justify* the desirable features, and critically, *map* each feature to a real robotic capability (e.g., SLAM → Spatial Memory, Deep Nets → Learning).

## 1.3 The Eight Pillars of Cognitive Robotics

The eight universally accepted desirable features, as per KTU Module 2, are:

1. **Perception** – interpreting multi-modal sensor data.
2. **Attention** – selectively focusing cognitive resources.
3. **Memory** – short-term (working), long-term (episodic/semantic), and procedural storage.
4. **Learning** – improving performance from experience.
5. **Reasoning & Planning** – logical inference and sequence generation.
6. **Goal-Directed Behaviour** – pursuing objectives autonomously.
7. **Natural Interaction** – communicating with humans intuitively.
8. **Self-Awareness / Situatedness** – knowing its own state and environment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Desirable Feature

### Pillar 1 — Perception
- **What:** The ability to acquire and interpret data from heterogeneous sensors (cameras, LiDAR, microphones, tactile arrays, IMUs).
- **How:** Sensor fusion algorithms combine raw signals into a coherent internal world model.
- **Why:** Without perception, a robot cannot distinguish a wall from a doorway or recognise a human voice command.
- **Mathematical representation:** Sensor observation is modelled as a probability distribution $P(z_t \mid x_t)$ where $z_t$ is the observation and $x_t$ is the true state at time $t$.

### Pillar 2 — Attention
- **What:** A selective filtering mechanism that prioritises salient stimuli.
- **How:** Implemented via saliency maps, top-down (goal-driven) and bottom-up (stimulus-driven) weighting.
- **Why:** Computational resources are finite; a robot cannot process every pixel of a 4K camera at 60 fps with full semantic depth.
- **Mathematical model:** A saliency map $S(x,y)$ is computed as a weighted sum:
$$
S(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot F_i(x,y)
$$
where $F_i(x,y)$ are feature channels (colour, motion, depth) and $w_i$ are adaptive weights.

### Pillar 3 — Memory
- **What:** Persistent storage of past experiences, maps, skills, and facts.
- **How:** Tripartite memory model — **Sensory buffer → Working memory → Long-term memory**.
- **Why:** Enables temporal continuity; allows the robot to recall "I have been here before" or "this object is fragile".
- **Capacity analogy (Atkinson–Shiffrin model):** Sensory memory $\approx$ **7 ± 2** items, working memory $\approx$ **4 ± 1** chunks for humans; robots typically store gigabytes but retrieval is the bottleneck.

### Pillar 4 — Learning
- **What:** Modifying internal parameters/policies from experience to improve future performance.
- **How:** Supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement, and imitation learning paradigms.
- **Why:** Hand-engineering every behaviour is infeasible in open environments.
- **Update rule (generic gradient descent):**
$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_t)
$$
where $\theta$ are model parameters, $\eta$ is the learning rate, and $L$ is the loss function.

### Pillar 5 — Reasoning & Planning
- **What:** Inferring new facts and generating action sequences to achieve goals.
- **How:** Symbolic reasoning (logic), probabilistic inference (Bayesian networks), or hybrid neuro-symbolic systems.
- **Why:** Enables long-horizon decision-making beyond reactive stimulus-response.
- **Planning as search:** The cost-to-go function $f(n)$ is given by:
$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$
where $g(n)$ is the actual cost from start to node $n$ and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate from $n$ to the goal (used in **A\*** search).

### Pillar 6 — Goal-Directed Behaviour
- **What:** Actions are driven by explicit internal objectives rather than mere reflexes.
- **How:** A behaviour selection layer (e.g., subsumption architecture, BDI model — Belief-Desire-Intention).
- **Why:** Distinguishes purposive agents from reactive ones.
- **BDI formalisation:** At any moment, the agent holds a belief set $\mathcal{B}$, a desire set $\mathcal{D}$, and commits to an intention set $\mathcal{I} \subseteq \mathcal{D}$.

### Pillar 7 — Natural Interaction
- **What:** Communicating with humans via speech, gesture, gaze, and emotion.
- **How:** ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition), NLU (Natural Language Understanding), gesture recognition, affective computing.
- **Why:** Robots deployed in homes, hospitals, and offices must collaborate with non-technical users.

### Pillar 8 — Self-Awareness & Situatedness
- **What:** The robot knows its own internal state (battery, joint limits, confidence) and is aware that it is embedded in an environment that changes due to its own actions.
- **How:** Internal state estimation, introspection modules, embodied cognition frameworks.
- **Why:** Enables safe operation, graceful degradation, and meta-cognition ("I do not know" → ask for help).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Feature | Core Equation / Metric | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Perception | $P(z_t \mid x_t)$ observation model | SLAM, object detection |
| 2 | Attention | $S(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i F_i(x,y)$ | Visual saliency, foveation |
| 3 | Memory | Atkinson–Shiffrin tripartite model | Episodic recall, skill retention |
| 4 | Learning | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_t)$ | Neural network training, RL |
| 5 | Reasoning | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | A\* path planning, STRIPS |
| 6 | Goal-Directed | $(\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{I})$ BDI triplet | Autonomous mission planning |
| 7 | Interaction | Word Error Rate (WER), latency | HRI, social robotics |
| 8 | Self-Awareness | Confidence $c \in [0,1]$ on outputs | Safe failure, trust calibration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU frequently asks students to "**list any six desirable features and explain any two**." Memorise all eight, but be prepared to elaborate on Perception + Learning OR Memory + Reasoning as the most commonly chosen pair.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Saliency-Based Attention Map

We derive the bottom-up saliency map that allows a cognitive robot to focus on the most important region of its visual field.

### Step 1 — Define the Feature Channels
For a given image $I$ of resolution $H \times W$, we extract $n$ feature maps $\{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n\}$. Common channels include:
- Intensity $F_{\text{int}}$
- Colour opponency $F_{\text{RG}}, F_{\text{BY}}$
- Orientation $F_{\theta}$ for $\theta \in \{0^\circ, 45^\circ, 90^\circ, 135^\circ\}$
- Motion $F_{\text{mot}}$ (optical flow magnitude)

### Step 2 — Compute Centre-Surround Differences
For each channel $F_i$, compute the difference between a centre scale $c$ and a surround scale $s$:
$$
F_i^{cs}(x,y) = F_i^{c}(x,y) - F_i^{s}(x,y)
$$
This step highlights regions that stand out from their local context.

### Step 3 — Normalise and Combine
Apply across-scale combination and normalisation operator $\mathcal{N}(\cdot)$:
$$
S(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \mathcal{N}\!\left(F_i^{cs}(x,y)\right)
$$
The weights $w_i$ may be static (hand-tuned) or dynamic (learned via reinforcement).

### Step 4 — Winner-Take-All and Inhibition-of-Return
The peak of $S(x,y)$ indicates the next fixation point. To scan the entire scene, the algorithm applies **Inhibition-of-Return (IOR)**:
$$
S^{(k+1)}(x,y) = S^{(k)}(x,y) \cdot M^{(k)}(x,y)
$$
where $M^{(k)}(x,y)$ is a mask that zeros out the previously attended location.

> **Conversion Logic (textual):** The saliency map converts raw pixel intensities into a priority-ordered spatial attention landscape, enabling the robot's gaze, manipulator, or computational resources to focus on the most informative region first.

## 3.2 Derivation: BDI Goal Selection for Autonomous Navigation

### Step 1 — Belief Formation
At time $t$, the robot maintains a belief set:
$$
\mathcal{B}_t = \{b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_m\}
$$
Example belief: $b_3$: "Door A is 2.4 m ahead, **70%** probability of being open."

### Step 2 — Desire Generation
From beliefs, a desire set is generated via utility estimation:
$$
\mathcal{D}_t = \{d \mid U(d \mid \mathcal{B}_t) > \tau\}
$$
where $U(d \mid \mathcal{B}_t)$ is the expected utility of desire $d$ and $\tau$ is the threshold.

### Step 3 — Intention Commitment
The agent commits to a subset of desires that are mutually consistent and feasible:
$$
\mathcal{I}_t = \mathrm{Consistent}\!\left(\{d \in \mathcal{D}_t \mid \mathrm{Feasible}(d)\}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Action Execution
The selected intention $i^* \in \mathcal{I}_t$ is translated into a low-level action via motion planning:
$$
a_t = \pi(s_t, i^*)
$$
where $\pi$ is the policy and $s_t$ is the current world state.

> **Conversion Logic (textual):** This pipeline transforms ambiguous environmental beliefs into committed, executable intentions — the mathematical essence of **goal-directed behaviour** in cognitive robotics.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Features → Robotic Implementations

| Desirable Feature | Classical Algorithm / Model | Modern AI Equivalent | Typical Sensor / Hardware |
|-------------------|-----------------------------|----------------------|-----------------------------|
| Perception | Kalman Filter | CNN-based vision transformers | Camera, LiDAR, IMU |
| Attention | Itti–Koch saliency model | Transformer self-attention | GPU-accelerated vision pipeline |
| Memory | SLAM graph | Neural episodic memory | SSD, RAM maps |
| Learning | Q-learning | Deep Q-Networks (DQN), PPO | GPU/TPU cluster |
| Reasoning | STRIPS planner | Large Language Model planners | Symbolic solver / LLM |
| Goal-Directed | Subsumption architecture | BDI agents, hierarchical RL | Behaviour tree engine |
| Natural Interaction | Finite-state grammar | Whisper, GPT-based dialogue | Microphone array, display |
| Self-Awareness | Health monitoring | Uncertainty quantification (Bayesian DL) | Battery sensors, joint encoders |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Cognitive Robot

The diagram below maps how the eight desirable features interact within a single cognitive robotic architecture. The arrows denote information flow (read/write) between modules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    env[Environment World] -->|Raw Stimuli| perc[Perception Module]
    perc -->|Filtered Observations| att[Attention Module]
    att -->|Salient Snapshot| mem[Memory Store]
    mem -->|Context Recall| reas[Reasoning and Planning]
    reas -->|Action Plan| goal[Goal Directed Behaviour]
    goal -->|Motor Commands| act[Actuator Layer]
    act -->|Physical Effect| env
    learn[Learning Module] -.->|Policy Update| reas
    learn -.->|Weight Update| perc
    intmod[Natural Interaction Module] -->|Human Input| reas
    selfmod[Self Awareness Module] -.->|Confidence Signals| reas
    selfmod -.->|Health Status| goal
    mem -->|Episodic Recall| intmod
```

### Architectural Reading Guide
- **Solid arrows** = primary data flow (sense-think-act loop).
- **Dashed arrows** = meta-cognitive / learning feedback that tunes the primary loop.
- **Self-Awareness** and **Learning** are cross-cutting modules — they do not own a stage in the loop but modulate every other stage.
- **Natural Interaction** is bidirectional with Reasoning because human utterances can both inject new goals and request explanations.

## 4.2 Hierarchical Layer Diagram of Memory Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sm[Sensory Memory Buffer] -->|Attention Gate| wm[Working Memory Active]
    wm -->|Consolidation Encoding| ltm[Long Term Memory Store]
    ltm -->|Retrieval Cue| wm
    ltm -->|Procedural Recall| sm
    subgraph LTM_TYPES[Long Term Categories]
        sem[Semantic Memory Facts]
        epi[Episodic Memory Events]
        pro[Procedural Memory Skills]
    end
    LTM_TYPES --> ltm
```

### Reading Guide
- **Sensory memory** holds raw data for **300 ms** (mimicking human cognition).
- **Working memory** is the active scratchpad used by the reasoning module.
- **Long-term memory** is partitioned into semantic (facts), episodic (events), and procedural (skills) — directly inspired by the Tulving model.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six desirable features of a cognitive robot.**

**Model Answer:**
The six desirable features are:
1. **Perception** – interpreting multi-modal sensor data.
2. **Attention** – selectively focusing on salient stimuli.
3. **Memory** – retaining and recalling past experiences.
4. **Learning** – improving behaviour from experience.
5. **Reasoning and Planning** – inferring and generating action sequences.
6. **Goal-Directed Behaviour** – acting purposefully towards objectives.

*(Any six from the eight pillars listed in Section 1.3 are acceptable.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between reactive robots and cognitive robots.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Reactive Robot | Cognitive Robot |
|--------|----------------|------------------|
| Decision-making | Stimulus–response (if-then rules) | Internal world model + reasoning |
| Learning | None or minimal | Continuous from experience |
| Memory | Stateless or short buffer | Multi-tier (sensory, working, long-term) |
| Adaptability | Pre-programmed behaviours only | Adapts to novel situations |
| Example | Industrial pick-and-place arm | Autonomous humanoid in a hospital |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the desirable features of **Perception** and **Attention** in a cognitive robot. Discuss how these two features together enable a robot to operate in a cluttered indoor environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable diagrams, describe the **BDI (Belief-Desire-Intention)** architecture and show how it implements **goal-directed behaviour** in an autonomous mobile robot. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Perception:**
Perception is the robot's ability to acquire, fuse, and interpret data from heterogeneous sensors. In a cluttered indoor environment, the robot fuses RGB-D camera input, LiDAR point clouds, and ultrasonic readings. Mathematically, perception is the computation of:
$$
\hat{x}_t = \arg\max_{x_t} P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})
$$
where $\hat{x}_t$ is the most likely current state estimate.

**Attention:**
Attention filters the perceived data so that the robot does not waste computation on irrelevant regions. A saliency map $S(x,y)$ prioritises the doorway over a static bookshelf.

**Combined working:**
In a cluttered room, perception first constructs a 360° semantic map (chair, table, person). Attention then redirects the robot's pan-tilt camera and processing resources towards the moving person and the open doorway. The robot allocates **80%** of its inference budget to these two regions and **20%** to peripheral monitoring.

#### Model Solution for (b)

The BDI architecture models an agent using three logical structures:

- **Beliefs ($\mathcal{B}$):** the robot's current knowledge (e.g., "battery at 35%", "kitchen is east of current position").
- **Desires ($\mathcal{D}$):** the goals the robot would like to achieve (e.g., "deliver medicine to patient in Room 204").
- **Intentions ($\mathcal{I}$):** the committed subset of desires currently being executed.

**Flow:**
1. Belief Revision function updates $\mathcal{B}$ from perception.
2. Option Generation produces a candidate desire set $\mathcal{D}'$ from $\mathcal{B}$.
3. Deliberation filters $\mathcal{D}'$ via utility threshold $\tau$ to produce $\mathcal{D}$.
4. Means-End Reasoning selects feasible intentions $\mathcal{I} \subseteq \mathcal{D}$.
5. Execution translates $\mathcal{I}$ into actuator commands.

A block-level flow (mermaid):
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B[Belief Revision] --> O[Option Generation]
    O --> D[Deliberation Filter]
    D --> M[Means End Reasoning]
    M --> E[Execution Engine]
    E -->|Actuators| env2[Environment]
    env2 -->|Sensors| B
```

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

**(a)** Discuss the **memory subsystem** of a cognitive robot. Differentiate between sensory, working, and long-term memory with one robotic example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of **Learning** and **Self-Awareness** in cognitive robotics. Describe one modern machine learning algorithm that enables a robot to learn manipulation skills from human demonstrations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The cognitive robot's memory is tripartite, mirroring the **Atkinson–Shiffrin** model:

| Memory Type | Capacity | Duration | Robotic Example |
|-------------|----------|----------|------------------|
| Sensory Memory | Very large (raw bitstream) | **300 ms** | Last camera frame buffer |
| Working Memory | Limited (RAM-bound) | Active task duration | Current SLAM sub-map |
| Long-Term Memory | Effectively unlimited | Persistent | Stored semantic map of building |

- **Sensory memory** holds raw sensor data briefly so that attention can select salient features.
- **Working memory** integrates the currently attended items with relevant retrieved knowledge to support reasoning.
- **Long-term memory** is partitioned into semantic (object labels), episodic ("I visited Room 204 yesterday"), and procedural (how to grasp a cup) sub-stores.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Learning** enables the robot to refine its policy $\pi$ from experience using:
$$
\pi_{t+1} = \pi_t + \alpha \left(r_t + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t)\right) \nabla_{\theta} Q(s_t, a_t)
$$
where $r_t$ is reward, $\gamma$ is discount, and $\alpha$ is learning rate.

**Self-Awareness** monitors internal state (battery, joint torques, model confidence $c$) and triggers safe behaviours when $c < 0.5$.

**Imitation Learning Example:** Behavioural Cloning trains a neural network $\pi_{\theta}(a \mid s)$ on tuples $(s_i, a_i)$ collected from human teleoperation. The loss minimised is:
$$
L(\theta) = -\sum_{i} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_i \mid s_i)
$$
This allows a robot to acquire a "pick-and-place" skill in **20–50** demonstrations without hand-coded programming.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often **list features without explaining** them. In a 14-mark question, each sub-part requires **at least 4–5 lines of explanation** plus a diagram.
> - Confusing **working memory** with **RAM** is a common error. RAM is hardware; working memory is a *functional* concept.
> - When asked about BDI, do not write only the acronym — define Belief, Desire, AND Intention explicitly.
> - For learning questions, the **update equation** is mandatory. A diagram without an equation loses up to **3 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **eight desirable features** are: Perception, Attention, Memory, Learning, Reasoning & Planning, Goal-Directed Behaviour, Natural Interaction, Self-Awareness.
- **Perception** uses sensor fusion; mathematically modelled as $P(z_t \mid x_t)$.
- **Attention** uses saliency maps $S(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i F_i(x,y)$.
- **Memory** is tripartite: sensory $\to$ working $\to$ long-term (semantic / episodic / procedural).
- **Learning** update rule: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_t)$.
- **Planning** uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ (A\* heuristic).
- **BDI architecture**: Beliefs, Desires, Intentions.
- **Self-Awareness** provides confidence signals for safe degradation.
- **Natural Interaction** uses ASR, NLU, and affective computing.
- **Cognitive ≠ Reactive**: cognitive robots have internal models, learning, and long-term memory; reactive robots do not.
- Always **combine diagrams + equations + examples** for full KTU marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing a Cognitive Architecture: Introduction to the Model of Cognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT863 - COGNITIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Designing a Cognitive Architecture: Introduction to the Model of Cognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Cognitive Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cognitive Architecture (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **Cognitive Architecture** is a comprehensive, domain-general computational framework that specifies the fixed underlying mechanisms — perception, attention, memory, learning, reasoning, decision-making, and motor control — required to produce intelligent behaviour in an agent (human, animal, or robot). It defines *how* the mind is organised, *what* the structural components are, and *how* information flows among them.

In the context of **Cognitive Robotics**, the cognitive architecture acts as the *software/hardware blueprint* of the robot's "mind." Just as a building cannot stand without an architect's blueprint, a cognitive robot cannot exhibit intelligent behaviour without an underlying cognitive architecture that organises its sensors, actuators, knowledge base, and reasoning modules.

### 1.2 The Model of Cognition

> [!NOTE]
> **Model of Cognition (MOC):**
> A *Model of Cognition* is the formal, computational specification of the mental processes inside the architecture. It represents **what** an agent knows, **how** it represents that knowledge, and **how** it transforms inputs (sensory data) into outputs (motor actions) through cognitive operations.

The Model of Cognition is, therefore, the **theoretical heart** of any cognitive architecture. It typically addresses:

- **Representation** — How knowledge is encoded (symbols, vectors, probabilities, graphs).
- **Reasoning** — How new conclusions are drawn (logic, inference, search).
- **Memory** — How information is stored, retrieved, and forgotten.
- **Learning** — How the system updates itself from experience.
- **Control** — Which process executes when, and how conflicts are resolved.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Cognitive Robot as an Office

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Robot as a Smart Office"**
> Imagine a high-tech office building:
> - The **reception desk** receives visitors (sensors/perception).
> - The **filing cabinets** store documents (long-term memory).
> - The **whiteboard** holds current tasks (working memory).
> - The **manager** decides what to do next (reasoning/decision module).
> - The **employees** carry out the orders (motor/action system).
> - The **building's wiring and layout** itself is the **cognitive architecture**.
> - The **rules of how work flows** through the office is the **Model of Cognition**.

The architecture is *static structure*; the model of cognition is the *dynamic workflow*. Together, they transform raw inputs into intelligent, goal-directed actions.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Parameters in Cognitive Architectures

| Metric | Symbol/Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Cognitive Cycle Time** | $T_c \approx 50$–$200\,\text{ms}$ | Time per perception–cognition–action loop |
| **Working Memory Capacity** | $C_{wm} \approx 7 \pm 2$ items | Miller's Law — short-term storage limit |
| **Decay Constant** | $\lambda$ | Rate of memory decay in working memory |
| **Activation Threshold** | $\theta_a$ | Minimum activation for memory retrieval |
| **Response Time (Hick's Law)** | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | Choice reaction time vs. number of alternatives |

> [!NOTE]
> These values are derived from classical human cognition studies and are commonly used as **design benchmarks** when validating bio-inspired cognitive robots.

### 1.5 Why Cognitive Architecture Matters for Robotics

In Cognitive Robotics, a well-designed architecture allows the robot to:

1. **Perceive** uncertain, noisy environments.
2. **Reason** about goals, plans, and alternatives.
3. **Learn** from mistakes and novel situations.
4. **Adapt** without reprogramming.
5. **Explain** its own decisions (XAI — Explainable AI).

This is the central theme of Module 2 — designing the *mind* that will sit inside the *body* of a robot.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Historical Evolution of Cognitive Architectures

The field traces its roots to the **Physical Symbol System Hypothesis (Newell & Simon, 1976)**:

> *"A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action."*

From this hypothesis, three major paradigms emerged:

| Paradigm | Era | Core Idea | Example Architectures |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Symbolic (Cognitivist)** | 1960s–1990s | Mind = manipulation of symbols | SOAR, ACT-R, EPIC |
| **Emergent (Connectionist)** | 1980s–2000s | Mind = patterns of activation in networks | Subsumption (Brooks), Neural architectures |
| **Hybrid** | 1990s–Present | Combines symbols + connectionist units | CLARION, LIDA, ACT-R (modern) |

### 2.2 Core Components of a Cognitive Architecture

A complete cognitive architecture is composed of **five inter-locking subsystems**:

1. **Perception Module** — Converts raw sensor data (vision, sound, touch, proprioception) into internal symbolic/vector representations.
2. **Memory System** — Composed of:
   - *Sensory Memory* ($\approx 0.5\,$s)
   - *Working / Short-Term Memory* (capacity $7 \pm 2$)
   - *Long-Term Memory* (declarative + procedural)
3. **Reasoning / Decision Module** — Performs inference, planning, and problem solving.
4. **Learning Module** — Updates knowledge from experience (supervised, reinforcement, unsupervised).
5. **Action / Motor Module** — Translates decisions into motor commands and controls effectors.

### 2.3 The Model of Cognition — Operational Breakdown

The Model of Cognition can be expressed as a closed-loop transformation:

$$
C: \mathcal{P} \times \mathcal{M} \times \mathcal{G} \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{A}
$$

where:

- $\mathcal{P}$ = Perceptual state space
- $\mathcal{M}$ = Memory state space
- $\mathcal{G}$ = Goal / motivation set
- $\mathcal{A}$ = Action space
- $C$ = Cognitive function mapping

**Operational Steps inside the Model of Cognition:**

1. **Sense:** Acquire multimodal sensory data.
2. **Encode:** Convert raw data into internal representations.
3. **Attend:** Filter salient information (attention mechanism).
4. **Retrieve:** Pull relevant knowledge from long-term memory.
5. **Reason:** Apply inference/planning over representations.
6. **Decide:** Select the best action (decision policy $\pi$).
7. **Act:** Execute motor commands.
8. **Learn:** Update memory/parameters based on outcome feedback.
9. **Repeat:** Close the perception–cognition–action loop.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **must-know equations** for KTU ESE questions on cognitive architecture and the model of cognition.

| # | Formula | Name / Concept | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | **Hick's Law** | Reaction time vs. number of choices |
| 2 | $S = k \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | **Miller's Law** | Working memory capacity |
| 3 | $A_i(t) = B_i + \sum_j W_j S_{ji} - \lambda \cdot t$ | **ACT-R Activation Equation** | Memory retrieval probability |
| 4 | $P(\text{retrieval} \mid i) = \frac{e^{A_i/\tau}}{\sum_j e^{A_j/\tau}}$ | **Softmax Retrieval** | Probabilistic memory access |
| 5 | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'}Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | **Q-Learning Update** | Reinforcement learning in cognitive agents |
| 6 | $\pi^*(a \mid s) = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ | **Optimal Policy** | Decision-making in cognition |
| 7 | $T_c = T_p + T_r + T_d + T_m$ | **Cognitive Cycle Time** | Latency budget for robot cognition |
| 8 | $U(s) = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r_t \mid s_0 = s\right]$ | **Utility Function** | Goal evaluation in decision theory |
| 9 | $H(X) = -\sum_i p(x_i)\log p(x_i)$ | **Shannon Entropy** | Uncertainty in perceptual input |
| 10 | $w_{ij}^{(t+1)} = w_{ij}^{(t)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}}$ | **Gradient Descent** | Learning rule in neural cognitive modules |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table formatting rule:** All absolute-value and conditional separators inside the formulas above use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never the raw pipe character — to preserve KTU-compatible markdown table rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Cognitive architectures like hierarchical task networks drive self-driving decision loops.
- **Service Robots:** Pepper, Nao and other social robots use LIDA-inspired architectures for human–robot interaction.
- **Industrial Cobots:** Hybrid architectures combine symbolic task planning with reactive control.
- **Assistive & Medical Robotics:** Cognitive models help robots infer user intent and adapt to disability.
- **Space Exploration (NASA):** SOAR-based agents have been used for autonomous spacecraft fault diagnosis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the ACT-R Activation Equation

The ACT-R (Adaptive Control of Thought — Rational) architecture is the most widely taught cognitive architecture in academic curricula. We will derive its **memory activation equation** step by step.

**Step 1 — Start with the Base-Level Activation**

Each declarative memory chunk $i$ in ACT-R has a **base-level activation** $B_i$, representing how often it has been used in the past:

$$
B_i = \ln\left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} t_k^{-d}\right)
$$

where:

- $t_k$ = time since the $k^{\text{th}}$ occurrence of memory $i$
- $d$ = decay parameter (typically $d = 0.5$)
- $n$ = total number of past occurrences

> *Logic:* Frequently accessed memories accumulate higher base-level activation. The logarithmic form matches empirical human recall curves.

**Step 2 — Add the Spreading Activation from Context**

When a cue $j$ is present in working memory, it spreads activation $W_j S_{ji}$ to memory chunk $i$:

$$
W_j S_{ji} = W_j \cdot S_{ji}
$$

where $W_j$ is the *activation weight* of source element $j$, and $S_{ji}$ is the *strength of association* between $j$ and $i$ (typically $S_{ji} \in [-1, 1]$).

**Step 3 — Incorporate Decay Over Time**

Memory strength decays exponentially as time $t$ passes. Adding a decay term with rate $\lambda$:

$$
A_i(t) = B_i + \sum_j W_j S_{ji} - \lambda \cdot t
$$

> *Logic:* The negative $\lambda t$ term ensures that even highly relevant memories fade if not reinforced — modelling human forgetting.

**Step 4 — Convert Activation into Retrieval Probability**

Using the **softmax (Boltzmann) distribution** with temperature $\tau$:

$$
P(i \mid \text{cue}) = \frac{e^{A_i/\tau}}{\sum_{m=1}^{M} e^{A_m/\tau}}
$$

> *Logic:* The most highly activated memory is most likely to be retrieved, but lower-activated memories still have non-zero probability — modelling memory confusion and partial recall.

**Step 5 — Decision Threshold**

A memory $i$ is retrieved if and only if:

$$
A_i \geq \theta_a
$$

where $\theta_a$ is the retrieval threshold (default $\theta_a \approx -1.0$ in ACT-R). Otherwise a *retrieval failure* is reported.

**Final Form of the ACT-R Activation Equation:**

$$
\boxed{A_i(t) = \ln\!\left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} t_k^{-d}\right) + \sum_{j} W_j S_{ji} - \lambda t}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cognitive Cycle Time Budget

A real-time cognitive robot must complete one full perception–cognition–action loop within $T_c$. Let us derive the cycle time:

$$
T_c = T_p + T_a + T_r + T_d + T_m
$$

where:

- $T_p$ = **Perception time** (sensor acquisition + preprocessing)
- $T_a$ = **Attention time** (saliency computation)
- $T_r$ = **Reasoning time** (inference, planning)
- $T_d$ = **Decision time** (action selection)
- $T_m$ = **Motor time** (actuator command execution)

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
T_p = \frac{1}{f_s} \cdot N_s
$$

(where $f_s$ = sensor sampling rate, $N_s$ = number of samples per cycle)

$$
T_r = \frac{N_{\text{rules}} \cdot c_r}{P_{\text{CPU}}}
$$

(where $N_{\text{rules}}$ = number of inference rules fired, $c_r$ = cycles per rule, $P_{\text{CPU}}$ = CPU clock rate)

$$
T_d = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1) \quad \text{(Hick's Law)}
$$

(where $n$ = number of candidate actions)

**Substituting back:**

$$
T_c = \frac{N_s}{f_s} + \frac{N_{\text{rules}} \cdot c_r}{P_{\text{CPU}}} + \left[a + b \log_2(n+1)\right] + T_m
$$

> *Logic:* This derivation shows that real-time cognitive robotics requires **engineering trade-offs** — fewer sensors, faster CPUs, or simpler rule sets reduce $T_c$ to meet real-time deadlines.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Cognitive Architectures

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is a **high-frequency KTU comparison topic** — students are commonly asked to contrast two architectures in 7–14 mark questions.

| Property | **SOAR** | **ACT-R** | **CLARION** | **LIDA** | **Subsumption (Brooks)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Symbolic | Symbolic-Hybrid | Hybrid | Hybrid | Reactive / Emergent |
| **Memory** | Working + SMem | Declarative + Procedural | Explicit + Implicit | Multiple cycles | None central |
| **Learning** | Chunking | Bayesian update | Dual-process | Attention codelets | Fixed |
| **Decision Rule** | Production rules | Activation matching | Bottom-up + top-down | Global Workspace | Layered FSM |
| **Cycle Time** | Variable | $\sim 50\,\text{ms}$ | Variable | $\sim 200\,\text{ms}$ | $\sim 10\,\text{ms}$ |
| **Robotics Use** | UAV, planning | HCI, tutoring | Industrial agents | Consciousness modelling | Roomba, insects |
| **Strength** | Goal-driven | Cognitive accuracy | Learning richness | Consciousness realism | Speed & simplicity |
| **Weakness** | Slow learning | Tuning overhead | Complexity | Computationally heavy | No long-term planning |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A cognitive robot must choose between **$n = 7$** candidate actions. Using Hick's Law with constants $a = 100\,\text{ms}$ and $b = 150\,\text{ms}$, compute the decision time $T_d$. Given the remaining subsystems take $T_p = 30\,\text{ms}$, $T_a = 10\,\text{ms}$, $T_r = 80\,\text{ms}$, $T_m = 50\,\text{ms}$, find the total cognitive cycle time $T_c$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Apply Hick's Law for decision time:

$$
T_d = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1) = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_2(8)
$$

$$
\log_2(8) = 3
$$

$$
T_d = 100 + 150 \cdot 3 = 100 + 450 = 550\,\text{ms}
$$

Step 2: Sum all subsystem times:

$$
T_c = T_p + T_a + T_r + T_d + T_m
$$

$$
T_c = 30 + 10 + 80 + 550 + 50 = 720\,\text{ms}
$$

Step 3: Convert to seconds:

$$
T_c = 0.72\,\text{s} \approx 1.39\,\text{Hz}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The robot can make **approximately 1.4 cognitive decisions per second**, suitable for slow-paced assistive tasks but inadequate for high-speed dynamic environments (which require $T_c < 100\,\text{ms}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Cognitive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Perception Module] --> B[Attention Filter]
    B --> C[Working Memory]
    C --> D[Long Term Memory]
    C --> E[Reasoning Engine]
    E --> F[Decision Module]
    F --> G[Action Motor System]
    G --> H[Environment]
    H --> A
    D -.retrieval.-> E
    E -.learning feedback.-> D
    F -.error signal.-> I[Learning Module]
    I -.update weights.-> D
    I -.policy update.-> F
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows represent the **real-time perception–cognition–action loop**, while the dotted arrows represent **slower learning feedback channels** that update the system based on experience.

---

### 4.2 Model of Cognition — Detailed Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSORY_LAYER [SENSORY INPUT LAYER]
        S1[Vision Sensor]
        S2[Audio Sensor]
        S3[Tactile Sensor]
    end
    subgraph PERCEPTION [PERCEPTION LAYER]
        P1[Feature Extraction]
        P2[Object Recognition]
        P3[Speech to Text]
    end
    subgraph COGNITION [COGNITION LAYER]
        W1[Working Memory Buffer]
        W2[Episodic Memory Store]
        W3[Semantic Memory Store]
        R1[Planner]
        R2[Reasoner]
    end
    subgraph DECISION [DECISION LAYER]
        D1[Action Selector]
        D2[Policy Module]
    end
    subgraph ACTION [ACTION LAYER]
        M1[Robot Arm]
        M2[Mobile Base]
        M3[Speech Output]
    end
    S1 --> P1 --> P2
    S2 --> P3
    S3 --> P1
    P2 --> W1
    P3 --> W1
    W1 <--> W2
    W1 <--> W3
    W1 --> R1
    W1 --> R2
    R1 --> D1
    R2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> M1
    D2 --> M2
    D2 --> M3
```

---

### 4.3 Cognitive Cycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sense
    Sense --> Encode: raw data acquired
    Encode --> Attend: symbolic form
    Attend --> Retrieve: salient features selected
    Retrieve --> Reason: memory activated
    Reason --> Decide: plans generated
    Decide --> Act: action selected
    Act --> Learn: outcome observed
    Learn --> Sense: parameters updated
    Act --> [*]: task complete
```

---

### 4.4 Comparison Flow — Symbolic vs Emergent vs Hybrid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cognitive Architecture Paradigms] --> B[Symbolic]
    A --> C[Emergent]
    A --> D[Hybrid]
    B --> B1[SOAR]
    B --> B2[ACT-R]
    B --> B3[EPIC]
    C --> C1[Subsumption Brooks]
    C --> C2[Neural Networks]
    C --> C3[Self Organizing Maps]
    D --> D1[CLARION]
    D --> D2[LIDA]
    D --> D3[ACT-R Modern]
```

> [!TIP]
> When asked in exams to draw a cognitive architecture diagram, **always include both the perception–action loop (solid arrows) and the learning feedback path (dotted arrows)** — this distinction is worth marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Define a cognitive architecture. List any four major components of a typical cognitive architecture used in robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** A cognitive architecture is a domain-general computational framework that defines the fixed structural mechanisms — perception, memory, reasoning, learning, and action — that together produce intelligent behaviour in an agent.

> **Four Major Components (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Perception Module** — converts raw sensory data into internal representations.
> 2. **Memory System** — short-term (working) and long-term (declarative + procedural) memory.
> 3. **Reasoning / Planning Module** — performs inference and goal-directed planning.
> 4. **Action / Motor Module** — executes decisions via actuators.

*(Optional 5th: Learning Module — updates the system from experience.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Explain the term "Model of Cognition" with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Model of Cognition (MOC)** is the formal specification of the mental operations that occur inside a cognitive architecture. It defines *what* knowledge an agent holds, *how* it is represented, and *how* it is processed to produce behaviour. *(1 Mark)*

> **Example (2 Marks):** In a robot waiter, the Model of Cognition specifies that when a customer orders *"table 5, coffee"*:
> - The speech is encoded into symbols.
> - Relevant knowledge (menu, table layout) is retrieved from semantic memory.
> - A plan is generated: *navigate → pick cup → pour coffee → deliver*.
> - The plan is executed and the memory is updated with the success or failure.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a) Discuss in detail the major components of a cognitive architecture with a neat block diagram. Explain how the perception–cognition–action loop enables intelligent behaviour in a robot.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A cognitive architecture is the computational blueprint that organises the robot's perception, memory, reasoning, learning, and action into a unified intelligent system. It is the *engine* of cognitive robotics.

**Major Components (4 Marks):**

1. **Perception Module:** Acquires multimodal sensory data (vision, audio, tactile) and transforms it into internal symbolic or vector representations. Uses techniques like CNN-based vision, MFCC-based audio, and Kalman filters for proprioception.

2. **Memory System:** Has three tiers:
   - *Sensory Memory* ($\approx 0.5$ s buffer).
   - *Working Memory* (capacity $7 \pm 2$, Miller's Law).
   - *Long-Term Memory* (declarative = facts, procedural = skills).

3. **Reasoning Module:** Performs logical inference, planning, and problem solving. Examples include production-rule systems, probabilistic graphical models, and hierarchical task networks.

4. **Learning Module:** Updates internal parameters via supervised, unsupervised, or reinforcement learning. Updates both declarative memory content and procedural skills.
