| 6 | Clamping (saturation) | $l \in [l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$, typically $[-2, 2]$ | Prevents over-confident fusion | nats |
| 7 | Octree node count | $N_{\text{octree}} \le (8^k - 1)/7$ | $k$=max depth | For fully occupied tree |
| 8 | Octree memory (sparse) | $M_{\text{oct}} = N_{\text{occupied}} \cdot (3 \cdot 3 + 1) \cdot b$ | Stores 3D coord + occupancy flag | Bytes |
| 9 | kd-tree query | $T_q = O(\log n)$ avg., $O(n)$ worst | $n$=number of points | n=points |
| 10 | Quadtree memory | $M_q = N_{\text{leaf}} \cdot (c_x + c_y + v)$ | Stores centroid + value | Bytes |
| 11 | Voronoi edge length | $E_v \approx 2\sqrt{A \cdot N_s}$ | $A$=area, $N_s$=sample points | m |
| 12 | Point cloud size | $M_{pc} = N_p \cdot (3 \cdot f + 1 \cdot b)$ | $f$=float size (4 B), $b$=intensity bits | Bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX rule for KTU exam scripts:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside tables. (Already followed in this sheet — see column for $l \in [l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$.)

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Real Robots

- **Autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Tesla)**: hybrid metric-topological; occupancy grid is downsampled into a **multi-resolution voxel grid** for the planning stack.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva)**: pure 2D occupancy grid, $\Delta = 0.05$ m, stored as a `uint8` log-odds map.
- **Drones (DJI, Skydio)**: **octree + ESDF** (Euclidean Signed Distance Field) for real-time collision avoidance.
- **Humanoid robots (ASIMO, Atlas)**: 3D **voxel grid + semantic labels** for stair/door detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Log-Odds Occupancy Update

The standard probabilistic formulation (Moravec & Elfes, 1985; accepted KTU reference) is:

> **Goal:** Show that Bayesian inversion of the sensor model reduces to a *linear additive* log-odds update.

**Step 1 — Define the prior occupancy probability.**
Let $m_i \in \{0, 1\}$ be the occupancy of cell $i$ (1 = occupied, 0 = free). Prior:
$$P(m_i = 1) = p_0$$
$$P(m_i = 0) = 1 - p_0$$

**Step 2 — Define the sensor measurement model.**
The sensor returns measurement $z_t$ at time $t$. By Bayes' rule:
$$P(m_i \vert z_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \vert m_i) \cdot P(m_i \vert z_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \vert z_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 3 — Express the odds ratio.**
Define odds $O(x) = p / (1 - p)$. Then:
$$\frac{P(m_i = 1 \vert z_{1:t})}{P(m_i = 0 \vert z_{1:t})} = \frac{P(z_t \vert m_i = 1)}{P(z_t \vert m_i = 0)} \cdot \frac{P(m_i = 1 \vert z_{1:t-1})}{P(m_i = 0 \vert z_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 4 — Take the logarithm (log-odds).**
Let $l = \log O(x)$. Applying $\log(\cdot)$ to both sides:
$$l_t(m_i) = \underbrace{\log \frac{P(z_t \vert m_i = 1)}{P(z_t \vert m_i = 0)}}_{l_{\text{meas}}} + l_{t-1}(m_i)$$

**Step 5 — Separate prior from measurement.**
Restoring the prior $l_0$ explicitly:
$$\boxed{\,l_t(m_i) \;=\; l_{t-1}(m_i) \;+\; l_{\text{meas}} \;-\; l_0\,}$$

This is the **canonical KTU log-odds update rule** — additive, fast, and trivially invertible.

**Step 6 — Saturation / clamping.**
To prevent runaway confidence from many consistent observations:
$$l_t(m_i) = \max\!\left(l_{\min},\ \min\!\left(l_{\max},\ l_t(m_i)\right)\right)$$
Typical values: $l_{\min} = -2$, $l_{\max} = +2$ (i.e., $p \in [0.12, 0.88]$).

**Step 7 — Recover probability for display / planning.**
$$p(m_i = 1) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l_t(m_i)}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Memory Complexity of a 2D Grid vs. an Octree

**Setup:** A 3D workspace of side $L$ discretized at resolution $\Delta$.

**Step 1 — Number of cells in a uniform voxel grid.**
$$N_{\text{grid}} = \left(\frac{L}{\Delta}\right)^3 = \frac{L^3}{\Delta^3}$$

**Step 2 — Number of cells actually occupied in a sparse scene.**
For an environment with obstacle density $\rho \ll 1$:
$$N_{\text{occ}} = \rho \cdot N_{\text{grid}}$$

**Step 3 — Octree compression.**
A fully-populated octree of depth $k$ has node count:
$$N_{\text{oct,full}} = \sum_{d=0}^{k-1} 8^d = \frac{8^k - 1}{7}$$

**Step 4 — Sparse octree node count (key KTU result).**
A sparse octree stores one node *per occupied leaf* plus one internal node per non-uniform subdivision. For low $\rho$:
$$N_{\text{oct,sparse}} \;\approx\; N_{\text{occ}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1}{7}\right) \;=\; \frac{8}{7} N_{\text{occ}}$$

**Step 5 — Memory compression ratio.**
$$R \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{grid}}}{M_{\text{oct}}} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{grid}}}{N_{\text{oct,sparse}}} \;\approx\; \frac{L^3 / \Delta^3}{(8/7)\rho L^3/\Delta^3} \;=\; \frac{7}{8\rho}$$

For $\rho = 0.01$ (1% occupied): $R \approx 87.5\times$ compression. **This is the headline benefit KTU examiners test.**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (3-way)

| Criterion | Occupancy Grid | Octree | Topological Graph |
|-----------|---------------|--------|-------------------|
| **Update** | $O(1)$ per cell | $O(\log n)$ per insert | $O(1)$ node add |
| **Nearest-query** | $O(N)$ linear | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\deg)$ BFS |
| **Memory (sparse)** | $O(N)$ worst | $O(N_{\text{occ}})$ | $O(N_L + E)$ |
| **Loop closure** | Easy (Bayesian) | Moderate | Hard (graph match) |
| **Best use** | Dense indoor, AMRs | 3D / outdoor | Floor-plan scale |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Map-Building Data-Structure Taxonomy

The following Mermaid block renders the complete hierarchical taxonomy of map data structures as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3). All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Map Representations] --> B[Metric Maps]
    A --> C[Feature Based Maps]
    A --> D[Topological Maps]
    A --> E[Hybrid Maps]

    B --> B1[2D Occupancy Grid]
    B --> B2[3D Voxel Grid]
    B --> B3[Distance Transform]
    B --> B4[Elevation Map]

    B1 --> B1a[Uniform Resolution]
    B1 --> B1b[Quadtree Hierarchical]
    B2 --> B2a[Uniform Voxel]
    B2 --> B2b[Octree Hierarchical]

    C --> C1[Point Landmarks]
    C --> C2[Line Landmarks]
    C --> C3[Plane Landmarks]
    C --> C4[Visual Keypoints ORB SIFT]

    D --> D1[Place Graph]
    D --> D2[Voronoi Graph]
    D --> D3[Roadmap PRM]

    E --> E1[Metric Topological Hybrid]
    E --> E2[Semantic Metric Hybrid]

    B1b --> Q1[Quadtree Node Internal]
    B1b --> Q2[Quadtree Leaf Occupied]
    B1b --> Q3[Quadtree Leaf Free]
    B2b --> O1[Octree Root]
    O1 --> O2[Child Octant 0 to 7]

    classDef primary fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef hybrid fill:#86efac,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    class A,B,C,D,E primary
    class B1a,B1b,B2a,B2b,C1,C2,C3,C4,D1,D2,D3 leaf
    class E1,E2 hybrid
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Grid-Based SLAM Map-Builder

This block diagram shows the **data flow from raw sensor to updated grid**, which is the most common KTU Part B question pattern.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Raw Lidar Scan] --> S2[Sensor Preprocessing]
    S2 --> S3[Pose Estimation from Odometry]
    S3 --> S4[Coordinate Transform World Frame]
    S4 --> S5[Ray Casting Algorithm]
    S5 --> S6[Log Odds Update per Cell]
    S6 --> S7[Clamping and Saturation]
    S7 --> S8[Occupancy Grid Memory]
    S8 --> S9[Path Planner Query]
    S8 --> S10[Localization Query]
    S8 --> S11[Visualization Output]

    classDef sensor fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#000
    classDef process fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#000
    classDef memory fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000
    classDef output fill:#86efac,stroke:#000
    class S1 sensor
    class S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7 process
    class S8 memory
    class S9,S10,S11 output
```

### 4.3 Quadtree Subdivision Schematic (2D Spatial Hierarchy)

A quadtree recursively subdivides a 2D region into 4 equal quadrants when the contained data is non-uniform. This is the **standard textbook figure** the KTU 2024 examiner references.

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Root Region] --> R1[Quadrant NE]
    R --> R2[Quadrant NW]
    R --> R3[Quadrant SE]
    R --> R4[Quadrant SW]

    R1 --> R1a[Free Leaf]
    R1 --> R1b[Free Leaf]
    R1 --> R1c[Free Leaf]
    R1 --> R1d[Occupied Leaf]

    R2 --> R2a[Free Leaf]

    R3 --> R3a[NE Sub]
    R3 --> R3b[NW Sub]
    R3 --> R3c[SE Sub]
    R3 --> R3d[SW Sub]

    R3b --> R3b1[Occupied Leaf]
    R3b --> R3b2[Occupied Leaf]
    R3b --> R3b3[Free Leaf]
    R3b --> R3b4[Free Leaf]

    R4 --> R4a[Free Leaf]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef empty fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000
    classDef occ fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#000
    class R root
    class R1a,R1b,R1c,R2a,R3a,R3b1,R3b2,R3b3,R3b4,R3c,R3d,R4a empty
    class R1d,R3b occ
```

### 4.4 Octree 3D Subdivision Logic

The 3D analogue: each cubic node splits into **8 octants** when refinement is required. The same recursion pattern as the quadtree, but in three dimensions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    O0[Root Cube] --> OO[Octant 0 Front Bottom Left]
    O0 --> O1[Octant 1 Front Bottom Right]
    O0 --> O2[Octant 2 Front Top Left]
    O0 --> O3[Octant 3 Front Top Right]
    O0 --> O4[Octant 4 Back Bottom Left]
    O0 --> O5[Octant 5 Back Bottom Right]
    O0 --> O6[Octant 6 Back Top Left]
    O0 --> O7[Octant 7 Back Top Right]

    OO --> OOa[Uniform Free Leaf]
    O1 --> O1a[Uniform Free Leaf]
    O2 --> O2a[Subdivide Triggered]
    O2 --> O2b[Uniform Free Leaf]
    O3 --> O3a[Occupied Leaf]
    O4 --> O4a[Uniform Free Leaf]
    O5 --> O5a[Uniform Free Leaf]
    O6 --> O6a[Uniform Free Leaf]
    O7 --> O7a[Uniform Free Leaf]

    O2a --> O2a0[Child Octant 0]
    O2a --> O2a1[Child Octant 1]
    O2a --> O2a2[Child Octant 2]
    O2a --> O2a3[Child Octant 3]
    O2a --> O2a4[Child Octant 4]
    O2a --> O2a5[Child Octant 5]
    O2a --> O2a6[Child Octant 6]
    O2a --> O2a7[Child Octant 7]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef free fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000
    classDef occ fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#000
    classDef subdiv fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000
    class O0 root
    class OOa,O1a,O2b,O4a,O5a,O6a,O7a free
    class O3a occ
    class O2a,O2a0,O2a1,O2a2,O2a3,O2a4,O2a5,O2a6,O2a7 subdiv
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define an occupancy grid map. State the Bayesian update rule used to fuse a new sensor measurement into the grid.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **occupancy grid map** is a discrete, fine-grained 2D (or 3D) representation of the environment in which the workspace is divided into a regular array of cells, and each cell stores the *probability* $p(m_i)$ that the corresponding spatial region is occupied by an obstacle.

The Bayesian update rule (log-odds form) is:

$$l_t(m_i) \;=\; l_{t-1}(m_i) \;+\; l_{\text{meas}} \;-\; l_0$$

where $l_t = \log\!\left(\dfrac{p_t}{1 - p_t}\right)$ is the log-odds of cell $i$ at time $t$, $l_{\text{meas}}$ is the contribution of the current measurement, and $l_0$ is the prior log-odds.

**Mark split:** 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark variable explanation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*List any **three** advantages of an octree representation over a uniform voxel grid for a 3D map.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Memory efficiency** — An octree stores only occupied regions, yielding up to $7/(8\rho)\times$ compression in sparse scenes.
2. **Multi-resolution queries** — Different parts of the map can be accessed at different detail levels, supporting efficient planning.
3. **Adaptive refinement** — Areas with obstacles can be refined to a finer depth, while empty space remains at coarse depth.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Uniform Grid + Log-Odds**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, ESE Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **2D occupancy grid map-building system** for a mobile robot equipped with a planar laser rangefinder. Describe the role of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **log-odds Bayesian update rule** for fusing a new range measurement into a single cell of the occupancy grid. Show explicitly how the rule is *linear and additive* in log-odds, and explain why saturation (clamping) is necessary in practice. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Architecture (7 marks)**

1. *Sensor acquisition* — The planar lidar returns a vector of $N$ range readings $z_t = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_N\}$ per scan, typically at 10–40 Hz.
2. *Pose estimation* — Wheel odometry / IMU provides a pose estimate $\xi_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$ of the robot.
3. *Coordinate transform* — Each endpoint of a laser ray is transformed from the sensor frame to the global frame using the pose $\xi_t$:
   $$\begin{aligned}
   x_{\text{end}}^{\text{world}} &= x_t + r_k \cos(\theta_t + \alpha_k) \\
   y_{\text{end}}^{\text{world}} &= y_t + r_k \sin(\theta_t + \alpha_k)
   \end{aligned}$$
   where $\alpha_k$ is the ray angle.
4. *Ray casting* — Bresenham's algorithm (or a digital differential analyzer) traverses the cells along each ray, marking them as *free*. The terminal cell is marked *occupied*.
5. *Log-odds update* — Each touched cell receives the additive log-odds update.
6. *Clamping* — Saturation limits prevent runaway confidence.
7. *Storage* — The grid is a 2D array of `int8` or `float32` log-odds values.
8. *Consumers* — The path planner ($A^*$ on free cells) and the localizer (scan-to-map match) read the grid.

*(Use the block diagram from Section 4.2 for the answer sheet.)*

**Mark split:** 2 marks diagram, 3 marks block description, 2 marks equation of coordinate transform.

---

**(b) — Derivation (7 marks)**

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. Required answer steps:

1. Start with Bayes' rule applied to occupancy $P(m_i \vert z_{1:t})$ — **1 mark**
2. Form the odds ratio $O(x) = p/(1-p)$ — **1 mark**
3. Take the natural log to obtain the linear additive form — **2 marks**
4. State the final boxed equation — **1 mark**
5. Explain clamping: with formula $l \in [l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$, justification "prevents over-confidence and ensures numerical stability when many consistent observations are fused" — **2 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Octree + Comparison**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, ESE Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **octree data structure** for 3D map building. With a labelled diagram, show how a single cubic node is subdivided into 8 octants and describe the algorithm for insertion of a point cloud. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **occupancy grid**, **feature-based map**, and **topological map** along the axes of (i) memory complexity, (ii) update complexity, (iii) loop-closure handling, and (iv) suitability for indoor vs. outdoor use. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Octree (7 marks)**

An **octree** is a hierarchical 3D spatial data structure in which each node represents a cubic volume of space. If a node is *non-uniform* (partially occupied), it is recursively subdivided into **8 equal octants**; otherwise it is stored as a **leaf**.

**Insertion algorithm (4 marks):**
1. Start at the **root** node representing the entire workspace.
2. Determine the octant of the point relative to the current node's center.
3. *If the child octant does not exist* → create it.
4. *If the child octant exists* → recurse into it.
5. Stop when the maximum depth $k$ is reached or the cell is uniformly occupied.
6. Mark the leaf as **occupied** or **free**.

Use the Mermaid diagram from **Section 4.4** as the labelled figure (replace with hand-drawn equivalent in exam). **3 marks** for explanation + 3 marks for the diagram + 1 mark for algorithmic step.

---

**(b) — Comparison Table (7 marks — distribute 1.5–2 per row, plus 0.5 for an introductory sentence)**

| Axis | Occupancy Grid | Feature Map | Topological Map |
|------|----------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **(i) Memory** | $O(N^2)$ in 2D — heavy for large areas | $O(N_L)$ — compact | $O(N_V + E)$ — very compact |
| **(ii) Update** | $O(1)$ per cell, $O(N_r)$ per scan (ray length) | $O(1)$ landmark add | $O(1)$ node/edge add |
| **(iii) Loop closure** | Direct Bayesian fusion | Data association required | Graph matching (e.g., SLAM back-end) |
| **(iv) Best domain** | Indoor, small-to-medium | Landmark-rich indoor/outdoor | Floor-plan scale, large outdoor |

**Concluding sentence (0.5 mark):**
Hybrid maps (e.g., *Atlas framework* by Bosse et al., or *Cartographer* by Google) combine all three — sub-maps as local grids linked in a pose graph — to leverage the strengths of each.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this question:**
> 1. **Forgetting the prior term $l_0$** in the log-odds equation — full marks only if the update is written as $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{\text{meas}} - l_0$, **not** $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{\text{meas}}$.
> 2. **Confusing *occupancy grid* with *topological graph*** — a grid stores spatial *occupancy*, not connectivity.
> 3. **Writing "octree stores 8 cells"** — wrong; it stores *one node that can branch into 8 children*. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this conceptual error.
> 4. **Skipping the units** in the formula sheet (e.g., resolution $r$ in **m/cell**) — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly checks for units in numerical answers.
> 5. **Not drawing the coordinate transform equation** in the architecture question — at least one equation is mandatory for full marks in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 6. **Forgetting the inverse-logistic $p = 1/(1+e^{-l})$** when asked to convert log-odds back to probability — examiners expect this final step.
> 7. **Calling "ray casting" by a different name** ("Bresenham", "DDA", "voxel traversal") — any of these is accepted, but "Bresenham" alone is *only* for 2D grids; in 3D the algorithm is the **Amanatides–Woo algorithm**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-yield for last-day exam prep):**

- [x] **Three families** of map representations: *metric (grid), feature-based, topological, hybrid.*
- [x] **Occupancy grid** stores $P(m_i = 1)$ per cell; uniform 2D array; **$O(1)$ update per cell.**
- [x] **Log-odds update**: $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{\text{meas}} - l_0$ — *additive*, invertible via $p = 1/(1 + e^{-l})$.
- [x] **Clamping** range: $l \in [-2, +2]$ nats, i.e., $p \in [0.12, 0.88]$.
- [x] **Quadtree** = 2D hierarchical; each non-uniform node splits into **4** children.
- [x] **Octree** = 3D hierarchical; each non-uniform node splits into **8** children.
- [x] **Octree compression ratio** $\approx 7 / (8\rho)$ for sparse scenes with obstacle density $\rho$.
- [x] **kd-tree** is used for **nearest-neighbor queries** on point clouds; $O(\log n)$ average.
- [x] **Voronoi diagram** is a *topological* map whose edges are equidistant from the two nearest obstacles — ideal for *roadmap* path planning.
- [x] **Feature maps** store extracted geometric primitives (points, lines, planes) with associated covariance matrices for EKF-SLAM.
- [x] **Hybrid maps** (e.g., Google's Cartographer) combine local occupancy grids with a global pose graph for loop closure.
- [x] **Resolution trade-off**: doubling resolution $\Delta \to \Delta/2$ **quadruples** 2D memory and **8×** 3D memory.
- [x] **Ray casting algorithms**: *Bresenham* (2D), *Amanatides–Woo* (3D) — name them correctly in the exam.
- [x] **Inverse sensor model** for occupancy: free along the ray, occupied at the endpoint, unknown beyond max range.
- [x] **Distance transform / ESDF** (Euclidean Signed Distance Field) is *derived from* the grid — not a separate representation — and is critical for gradient-based planning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explanation of Map Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT863 - COGNITIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Explanation of Map Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Map Building** is the computational process by which a mobile robot constructs an internal spatial representation (a *map*) of its environment using sensory observations, control actions, and (often uncertain) estimates of its own pose. In the KTU 2024 Cognitive Robotics syllabus, this falls under the broader probabilistic framework of **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**, where the robot simultaneously reasons about *where it is* and *what the world looks like*.

Mathematically, given a sequence of control actions $u_{1:t}$ and sensor observations $z_{1:t}$, the goal of map building is to compute the posterior probability distribution over the map $m$:

$$P(m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where $m$ represents the spatial model (metric grid, topological graph, or feature-based landmark list) and $t$ denotes discrete time steps.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blind Explorer

Imagine you wake up in a completely dark, unfamiliar house. You are holding a flashlight that illuminates only a small circular patch in front of you. As you walk, you:

1. **Sense** what is directly in front of you (the flashlight reveals walls, furniture, doorways).
2. **Move** through the space, remembering your steps (odometry).
3. **Sketch** what you have seen so far on a notepad.

The "notepad sketch" you are building in your mind **is the map**, the flashlight sweep **is the sensor model**, and your memory of footsteps **is the pose estimation**. Over time, your sketch becomes a consistent internal representation of the house — this is *exactly* what a robot does when it builds a map.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Map building is *not* a one-shot perception task. It is an **iterative, probabilistic accumulation of evidence** where every new sensor reading updates the robot's belief about every cell in the map.

---

## 1.3 Why Map Building is a *Cognitive* Robotics Problem

Map building qualifies as a **cognitive** robotics problem because it requires the robot to:

| Cognitive Capability | Role in Map Building |
|---|---|
| **Perception** | Interpreting raw sensor data (laser, sonar, vision) into spatial meaning |
| **Reasoning under uncertainty** | Handling noisy sensors and imperfect odometry via Bayes' rule |
| **Memory** | Maintaining a persistent world model across time steps |
| **Learning** | Updating beliefs as new evidence accumulates |
| **Decision making** | Choosing where to explore next (active mapping) |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Map Building

- **Map Resolution:** Typically **0.05 m to 0.5 m** per cell for occupancy grids.
- **Pose Uncertainty:** Often modeled as a 2D Gaussian with covariance $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ (x, y, $\theta$).
- **Occupancy Probability Range:** $P(m_i) \in [0, 1]$, often stored as **log-odds** in the range $[-100, 100]$ for numerical stability.
- **Sensor Range:** LiDAR up to **30–100 m**; sonar typically **0.2–5 m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The two most commonly tested sub-topics are (i) **Occupancy Grid Mapping** and (ii) the **Bayesian update rule** for grid cells. Memorize both rigorously.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Maps in Robotic Map Building

Robotic maps fall into three principal categories. Understanding the trade-offs is critical for KTU exams.

### A. Metric Maps
Store **geometric properties** of the environment at known coordinates.

- **Feature-based / Landmark maps:** Sparse list of distinguishable objects (corners, doors) with $(x, y)$ coordinates.
- **Occupancy Grid Maps:** Dense 2D raster of cells, each holding a probability of being *free*, *occupied*, or *unknown*.

**Advantage:** Precise spatial reasoning, path planning is straightforward.
**Disadvantage:** Memory-intensive; struggles with large unstructured spaces.

### B. Topological Maps
Store **connectivity relationships** between places (nodes = distinctive locations; edges = traversable paths).

**Advantage:** Compact, human-readable, scale well to large buildings.
**Disadvantage:** Hard to construct reliably without a good place-recognition system.

### C. Hybrid Maps
Combine the strengths of both: a **global topological graph** where each node contains a **local metric map**. This is the **state-of-the-art** approach used in modern autonomous systems (e.g., warehouse robots).

---

## 2.2 The Occupancy Grid Map — The KTU Favorite

An **occupancy grid** divides the environment into a uniform 2D lattice of cells $m_i$. Each cell $m_i$ is a binary random variable:

$$m_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if cell } i \text{ is occupied} \\ 0 & \text{if cell } i \text{ is free} \end{cases}$$

The robot maintains the **posterior belief** that cell $i$ is occupied given all observations:

$$P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

where $x_{1:t}$ is the robot's pose trajectory. The standard assumption is **independence between cells**, which makes the problem tractable:

$$P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i} P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the independence assumption?** Without it, the joint posterior over millions of cells is computationally intractable. The independence assumption is an approximation — cells *are* correlated in reality (e.g., a wall implies its neighbors are likely free in the same direction) — but it works remarkably well in practice and is the foundation of the **probabilistic robotics** textbook by Thrun, Burgard, and Fox.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following table summarizes every formula you are expected to recognize and apply for map building questions in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Posterior over map | Definition of the mapping problem |
| 2 | $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ | Posterior for cell $i$ | Occupancy grid update |
| 3 | $\prod_{i} P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ | Cell independence | Factored posterior |
| 4 | $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}$ | Bayes' recursive update | Cell-by-cell evidence fusion |
| 5 | $\ell_{t,i} = \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$ | Log-odds of occupancy | Numerically stable representation |
| 6 | $\ell_{t,i} = \ell_{t-1,i} + \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - \ell_0$ | Recursive log-odds update | Efficient grid update |
| 7 | $P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) = \prod_{k} P(z_t^{(k)} \mid m_i, x_t)$ | Independent beam model | Forward sensor model |
| 8 | $H(m) = -\sum_i P(m_i) \log P(m_i)$ | Map entropy | Information gain / exploration |
| 9 | $\Sigma_{x_t} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ | Pose covariance (x, y, $\theta$) | Uncertainty propagation |
| 10 | $\vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ | Manhattan distance (grid) | Neighbor cell iteration |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notational Trap:** In KTU answer scripts, always clearly distinguish the **map** $m$ (a set of cells) from the **measurement** $z_t$ (a single observation at time $t$). Examiners deduct marks for conflating these.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Map building is the bedrock of:
- **Autonomous vehicles** (Google/Waymo, Tesla FSD) — build HD maps of road networks.
- **Warehouse automation** (Amazon Kiva robots) — map fulfillment centers dynamically.
- **Domestic vacuum robots** (iRobot Roomba) — build occupancy grids to plan coverage paths.
- **Disaster response robots** — map rubble-strewn environments for first responders.
- **Planetary rovers** (NASA Perseverance, ESA Rosalind Franklin) — build terrain maps for safe navigation on Mars.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayesian Update Rule for Occupancy Grid Maps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** in the KTU Map Building module. Practice writing it from memory.

### Setup

Let $m_i \in \{0, 1\}$ denote the occupancy state of grid cell $i$. We seek the recursive Bayesian update for the posterior belief that cell $i$ is occupied.

### Step 1 — State the Desired Posterior

We wish to compute the probability that cell $i$ is occupied at time $t$, given all observations up to $t$ and the robot trajectory up to $t$:

$$P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

### Step 2 — Factor the Posterior Using the Markov Property

Because observations are conditionally independent given the map and pose, the posterior can be written as the prior (from time $t-1$) updated by the newest observation $z_t$:

$$P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}$$

**Explanation:** The numerator splits the new evidence $P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)$ from the accumulated prior $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$. The denominator $P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})$ is a normalizer ensuring the result is a valid probability.

### Step 3 — Express the Denominator via the Law of Total Probability

The normalizer is obtained by marginalizing over the two possible states of $m_i$:

$$P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid m_i = 1, x_t) P(m_i = 1 \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) + P(z_t \mid m_i = 0, x_t) P(m_i = 0 \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$$

**Explanation:** Cell $i$ is either occupied or free; both cases contribute to the total probability of observing $z_t$.

### Step 4 — Convert to Log-Odds for Numerical Stability

Direct multiplication of many small probabilities causes **numerical underflow** in floating-point hardware. Define the **log-odds** representation:

$$\ell_{t,i} \triangleq \log \frac{P(m_i = 1 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{P(m_i = 0 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$$

### Step 5 — Derive the Recursive Log-Odds Update

Starting from Step 2, applying $\log$ to both sides, and using the identity $\log(a/b) = \log a - \log b$, the update collapses to an elegant **additive** form:

$$\ell_{t,i} = \ell_{t-1,i} + \log \frac{P(m_i = 1 \mid z_t, x_t)}{P(m_i = 0 \mid z_t, x_t)} - \ell_0$$

where $\ell_0 = \log \frac{P(m_i = 1)}{P(m_i = 0)}$ is the **prior log-odds** (usually $\ell_0 = 0$, meaning a 50/50 prior).

**Explanation:** The new log-odds equals the previous log-odds **plus** the evidence contribution of the latest measurement **minus** the prior. Addition is numerically stable and computationally cheap.

### Step 6 — Recover Probability from Log-Odds

To visualize or act on the map, convert back to probability via the **logistic function**:

$$P(m_i = 1 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(\ell_{t,i})}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Model Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> This table is a frequent source of **Part B 14-mark** questions in KTU exams. Learn the distinctions cold.

| Property | Forward Sensor Model | Inverse Sensor Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | $P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)$ — probability of observing $z_t$ given cell state and robot pose | $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ — probability of cell state given observation and pose |
| **Role** | Generative; predicts what the sensor *would see* | Discriminative; infers what the world *must be* to produce $z_t$ |
| **Computational cost** | High (integrates over all cells in sensor cone) | Low (direct per-cell update) |
| **Theoretical rigor** | Fully Bayesian | Approximation (treats measurements as independent of map) |
| **Practical usage** | Localization, ray-casting simulation | Real-time occupancy grid update |
| **KTU exam tip** | Mention **beam model** and **endpoint model** | Mention **inverse measurement model** directly applied per cell |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Map Entropy as an Exploration Metric

A common KTU question asks how a robot decides *where to explore next*. The answer uses **information gain**, measured by the **map entropy**:

$$H(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = -\sum_{i} P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) \log_2 P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

**Explanation:** Entropy is maximized when $P(m_i) = 0.5$ (maximum uncertainty). As the robot gathers more evidence, $P(m_i)$ shifts toward 0 or 1, and entropy drops. The robot should steer toward poses that **maximize the expected reduction in entropy** — this is the principle behind **active mapping** and **next-best-view planning**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Map Building Pipeline — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the end-to-end functional flow of an occupancy grid map building system. This is the **architecture diagram** KTU examiners expect in 7-mark sub-questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Robot Initialization] --> poseNode[Pose Estimation via Odometry]
    poseNode --> sensorNode[Acquire Sensor Scan z_t]
    sensorNode --> rayNode[Ray Casting from Robot Position]
    rayNode --> freeNode[Mark Free Cells Along Ray Path]
    rayNode --> occNode[Mark Endpoint Cell as Occupied]
    freeNode --> bayesNode[Bayesian Update per Cell]
    occNode --> bayesNode
    bayesNode --> logoddsNode[Log-Odds Accumulation]
    logoddsNode --> mapNode[Update Global Occupancy Grid]
    mapNode --> entropyNode[Compute Map Entropy]
    entropyNode --> decisionNode{Exploration Complete?}
    decisionNode -->|No| exploreNode[Select Next Best View]
    exploreNode --> poseNode
    decisionNode -->|Yes| outputNode[Output Final Map]
```

**How to read this diagram for exam purposes:**

| Stage | Function | KTU Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| `startNode` → `poseNode` | Robot initializes and estimates its current $(x, y, \theta)$ | Odometry model |
| `sensorNode` | Reads laser / sonar / vision measurement $z_t$ | Sensor model |
| `rayNode` → `freeNode`, `occNode` | Splits the measurement into "free" cells along the beam and the "occupied" endpoint | Inverse sensor model |
| `bayesNode` | Applies Bayes' rule cell-by-cell | Core update equation (Section 3.1) |
| `logoddsNode` | Stores evidence additively | Log-odds formula |
| `mapNode` | Maintains the persistent world representation | Output $P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ |
| `entropyNode` → `decisionNode` | Decides whether to continue exploring | Information gain |
| `outputNode` | Final 2D map delivered to path planner / user | Final deliverable |

---

## 4.2 Cell State Transition Diagram

This diagram illustrates how a single cell's belief evolves as evidence accumulates over time. **Draw a version of this in your exam** when explaining Bayesian updating.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    priorNode[Prior P m_i equals 0.5] --> obs1Node[Observation 1]
    obs1Node --> upd1Node[Posterior 1 P1]
    upd1Node --> obs2Node[Observation 2]
    obs2Node --> upd2Node[Posterior 2 P2]
    upd2Node --> obs3Node[Observation N]
    obs3Node --> finalNode[Converged Belief near 0 or 1]
```

**Reading guide:** Each successive observation narrows the uncertainty around the true occupancy state. After $N$ observations, $P(m_i \mid z_{1:N}, x_{1:N}) \to \{0, 1\}$ asymptotically — the robot has high confidence about whether the cell is occupied.

---

## 4.3 Hybrid Map Architecture (Block Diagram)

For 14-mark questions on advanced mapping, you can use this block diagram to explain a **hybrid topological-metric map**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topoNode[Global Topological Graph] --> placeNode[Place Recognition Module]
    placeNode --> localMetricNode[Local Metric Map per Node]
    localMetricNode --> gridNode[Local Occupancy Grid]
    localMetricNode --> featureNode[Local Landmark List]
    gridNode --> plannerNode[Path Planner]
    featureNode --> plannerNode
    plannerNode --> actionNode[Robot Action]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part A carries 3 marks per question and tests direct conceptual recall. Answers should be **3–4 sentences** plus a labeled diagram where appropriate.

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define map building in the context of mobile robotics. Why is it considered a hard problem?**

**Model Answer:**

Map building is the process of constructing a spatial representation of an unknown environment using sensor data collected as a robot moves through it. Formally, it is the computation of the posterior $P(m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ over the map $m$ given observations and controls. It is considered hard because (i) the robot must localize itself while mapping (the *chicken-and-egg* problem of SLAM), (ii) sensors and actuators are noisy, and (iii) the state space grows combinatorially with environment size. Probabilistic methods like Bayesian occupancy grids are required to manage this uncertainty rigorously. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between metric and topological maps.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Metric Map | Topological Map |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | Continuous coordinates / grid cells | Graph of nodes and edges |
| Information stored | Geometry (distances, shapes) | Connectivity (adjacency of places) |
| Memory | High (dense) | Low (sparse) |
| Path planning | Direct (A*, RRT on grid) | Graph search (Dijkstra) |
| Example | Occupancy grid | Floor plan as graph |

A **hybrid map** combines both: a global topological graph whose nodes each contain a local metric map. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**. Provide full derivations, diagrams, and final boxed conclusions.

---

### **Question A [14 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of an occupancy grid map building system. Discuss the roles of the forward sensor model and the inverse sensor model.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the recursive Bayesian update equation for a single cell of an occupancy grid. Show how the log-odds formulation arises and explain its numerical advantage.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture of Occupancy Grid Mapping

**Step 1 — High-level description:**
An occupancy grid map building system consists of three interacting subsystems: **(i) perception**, **(ii) localization**, and **(iii) map update**. The robot moves through the environment, each time producing a sensor scan $z_t$ from a known (or estimated) pose $x_t$.

**Step 2 — Block diagram (reproduce from Section 4.1):**

```
[Pose Estimation] → [Sensor z_t] → [Inverse Sensor Model] → [Bayesian Update] → [Log-Odds Grid] → [Map m_t]
                                                                                       ↑
                                                                                  [Prior m_{t-1}]
```

**Step 3 — Role of the forward sensor model $P(z_t \mid m, x_t)$:**
This is a *generative* model. Given a hypothetical map and a robot pose, it predicts what the sensor would measure. It is used in **localization** (finding the pose that best explains the observations) and in **simulation** of robots. It decomposes into a beam model with four components: (1) measurement noise, (2) unexpected objects, (3) failures (max range, random), (4) random noise.

**Step 4 — Role of the inverse sensor model $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$:**
This is a *discriminative* model. Given an observation $z_t$ and robot pose $x_t$, it directly returns the probability that a specific cell $m_i$ is occupied. It marks **(i) cells along the beam as free** (with some probability) and **(ii) the endpoint cell as occupied**. It is computationally cheap and used for **real-time mapping**.

**Conclusion:** A practical system uses the inverse sensor model for efficient online updates and the forward sensor model for offline localization and evaluation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation of the Bayesian Update and Log-Odds

**Step 1 — Posterior statement:**
We seek $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ for each cell $i$.

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' rule:**

$$P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}$$

**Step 3 — Marginalize the denominator:**

$$P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t}) = \sum_{m_i \in \{0,1\}} P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 4 — Define log-odds $\ell_{t,i}$:**

$$\ell_{t,i} \triangleq \log \frac{P(m_i = 1 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{P(m_i = 0 \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}$$

**Step 5 — Take $\log$ of the update and simplify:**

$$\ell_{t,i} = \underbrace{\log \frac{P(m_i = 1 \mid z_{t-1}, x_{t-1})}{P(m_i = 0 \mid z_{t-1}, x_{t-1})}}_{\ell_{t-1,i}} + \underbrace{\log \frac{P(m_i = 1 \mid z_t, x_t)}{P(m_i = 0 \mid z_t, x_t)}}_{\text{inverse model term}} - \underbrace{\log \frac{P(m_i = 1)}{P(m_i = 0)}}_{\ell_0}$$

**Step 6 — State the numerical advantage:**
Direct multiplication of probabilities over thousands of cells causes floating-point **underflow** (values underflow toward zero). The log-odds form turns products into sums, which is (i) numerically stable, (ii) order-independent (commutative and associative), and (iii) trivially reversible via the logistic function $P = (1 + e^{-\ell})^{-1}$.

**Conclusion:** The recursive log-odds update is the workhorse algorithm behind every practical occupancy grid mapper, including the ROS `gmapping` and `cartographer` stacks. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B [14 Marks]** — *Alternative Choice*

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain the forward sensor model for a range-bearing sensor (e.g., laser scanner). Write the mathematical expression for $P(z_t \mid m, x_t)$ assuming independence between beams.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the major challenges in map building for mobile robots operating in real-world environments. How does the occupancy grid framework address each challenge?

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Forward Sensor Model

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The forward sensor model specifies the probability of obtaining measurement $z_t$ assuming the map $m$ and robot pose $x_t$ are known.

**Step 2 — Beam model decomposition:**

For a range finder returning $K$ beams, the joint measurement is $z_t = \{z_t^{(1)}, z_t^{(2)}, \ldots, z_t^{(K)}\}$. Assuming independence:

$$P(z_t \mid m, x_t) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} P(z_t^{(k)} \mid m, x_t)$$

**Step 3 — Four-component mixture per beam:**
Each single-beam likelihood $P(z_t^{(k)} \mid m, x_t)$ is a mixture of:

$$P(z_t^{(k)} \mid m, x_t) = \alpha_{\text{hit}} \cdot p_{\text{hit}} + \alpha_{\text{short}} \cdot p_{\text{short}} + \alpha_{\text{max}} \cdot p_{\text{max}} + \alpha_{\text{rand}} \cdot p_{\text{rand}}$$

where:

- $p_{\text{hit}}$ — Gaussian centered on the true range (detected object).
- $p_{\text{short}}$ — Exponential for unexpected obstacles blocking the beam.
- $p_{\text{max}}$ — Point mass at maximum sensor range.
- $p_{\text{rand}}$ — Uniform over the measurement interval.
- $\alpha_{\text{hit}} + \alpha_{\text{short}} + \alpha_{\text{max}} + \alpha_{\text{rand}} = 1$.

**Step 4 — Diagram:**
Draw a coordinate frame with the robot at origin, a beam of length $r$ terminating at an obstacle, with a small Gaussian blob centered on the true range and a uniform tail extending to $z_{\text{max}}$.

**Conclusion:** The forward model is essential for localization (matching predicted vs. observed beams) and for simulating realistic sensors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Challenges in Real-World Map Building

| Challenge | Description | How Occupancy Grids Address It |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor noise** | Every measurement has Gaussian + outlier noise | Probabilistic update fuses many noisy readings into a confident posterior |
| **Odometry drift** | Wheel slippage causes pose error | Map evidence eventually overpowers the drift if the robot revisits areas (loop closure) |
| **Dynamic objects** | People, doors, cars change between scans | Cells that flicker between occupied/free are flagged as dynamic or filtered |
| **Perceptual aliasing** | Different places look identical (corridors, rooms) | The grid carries geometric context; identical *local* views differ in *global* position via accumulated pose |
| **Computational cost** | Millions of cells × thousands of scans | Log-odds additivity makes updates $O(1)$ per cell; hierarchical or multi-resolution grids reduce memory |
| **Map inconsistency** | Loop closure creates discontinuities | Probabilistic SLAM frameworks (e.g., Graph-SLAM) optimize the entire trajectory jointly with the map |

**Conclusion:** While occupancy grids alone do not solve loop closure, they provide the **per-cell uncertainty representation** that feeds into higher-level SLAM optimizers. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on Map Building questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Conflating SLAM with pure mapping** — Pure map building *assumes* known poses; SLAM estimates poses and map *jointly*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if you do not distinguish them in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Skipping the independence assumption** — When writing the factored posterior $\prod_i P(m_i \mid \cdot)$, you MUST explicitly state *why* this assumption is made. Without it, you lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the normalizer in Bayes' rule** — Writing $P(m_i \mid z, x) = P(z \mid m_i, x) P(m_i)$ without dividing by $P(z \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})$ is a **critical error** worth 2 marks.
> 4. **Log-odds without final conversion** — Always end with $P = (1 + e^{-\ell})^{-1}$. Examiners look for this final "recovery" step.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** — A neat, labeled block diagram is worth at least 1.5 marks. Never skip it.
> 6. **Confusing forward and inverse sensor models** — Forward predicts *measurements*; inverse predicts *occupancy*. Mixing them up loses 2 marks in (a) parts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ Map building = computing $P(m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ over a spatial representation $m$.
- ✅ Three map types: **metric** (precise, costly), **topological** (compact, abstract), **hybrid** (best of both).
- ✅ **Occupancy grid** = uniform 2D lattice; each cell stores $P(m_i = \text{occupied} \mid \cdot)$.
- ✅ **Cell independence assumption** is what makes the problem tractable.
- ✅ **Bayes' update:** $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) \propto P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$.
- ✅ **Log-odds representation:** $\ell_{t,i} = \ell_{t-1,i} + \text{inverse model} - \ell_0$; convert back via $P = (1 + e^{-\ell})^{-1}$.
- ✅ **Forward sensor model** = $P(z \mid m, x)$, used for localization and simulation (4-component beam mixture).
- ✅ **Inverse sensor model** = $P(m_i \mid z, x)$, used for cheap online grid updates.
- ✅ **Map entropy** $H(m)$ measures uncertainty and drives **active exploration**.
- ✅ **Loop closure** and **dynamic environments** are the two biggest open challenges; pure occupancy grids do not solve them — Graph-SLAM and dynamic filtering do.
- ✅ Always include a **labeled block diagram** in 7+ mark answers.
- ✅ Numerical underflow is the reason log-odds exist — mention this in any derivation.
- ✅ Distinguish **pure mapping** (known poses) from **SLAM** (unknown poses).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Simulation and Robot Map Building Programming
Program for Sensor Reading Display
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT863 - COGNITIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program for Sensor Reading Display

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensor Reading Display Programming** in Cognitive Robotics refers to the systematic process of acquiring raw analog or digital data from environmental sensors (e.g., ultrasonic, infrared, temperature, IMU), performing signal conditioning, executing unit conversion and calibration, and rendering the processed values on a human-machine interface (HMI) such as an LCD, OLED, serial terminal, or graphical dashboard. It forms the **lowest tier of the Sense-Plan-Act (SPA) cognitive loop**, where perception is materialized into observable, quantifiable feedback.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, the program is evaluated against Course Outcomes pertaining to:
- **CO4:** Design and implement perception pipelines for cognitive robots.
- **CO5:** Develop sensor interfacing and data visualization modules.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of a robot's sensor program like a **doctor's diagnostic monitor in an ICU**. The patient (robot's environment) is constantly changing — heartbeat, blood pressure, oxygen levels. The sensors (electrodes, cuffs, pulse oximeters) gather raw electrical signals. The monitor (microcontroller + display) converts these signals into numbers (e.g., "72 bpm") and shows them on a screen so the doctor (user/operator) can make decisions. Without this translation layer, the data is just meaningless voltage fluctuations.

### Core Components in a Sensor Display Program

| Component | Role | Example |
|-----------|------|---------|
| **Sensor Transducer** | Converts physical phenomena to electrical signals | HC-SR04, DHT11, MPU6050 |
| **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** | Quantizes continuous signals | 10-bit ADC on Arduino Uno |
| **Driver/Firmware** | Communicates using protocols (I2C, SPI, UART) | `Wire.h`, `DHT.h` library |
| **Processing Logic** | Converts raw counts → engineering units | `cm = (duration/2) / 29.1` |
| **Display Interface** | Renders the final value to human | 16x2 LCD, Serial Monitor, TFT |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values (Bolded):** Typical ADC resolution = **10-bit (1024 levels)**, Reference Voltage $V_{ref}$ = **5.0 V**, UART baud rate = **9600 bps**, I2C clock = **100 kHz / 400 kHz**, LCD character count = **16 columns × 2 rows**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

1. **Sensor Initialization (CONFIG Phase)**
   - Set pin modes (`INPUT`, `OUTPUT`).
   - Initialize communication buses (UART, I2C, SPI).
   - Configure sensor-specific parameters (sampling rate, resolution).

2. **Signal Acquisition (READ Phase)**
   - Trigger the sensor (e.g., 10 µs pulse for HC-SR04).
   - Wait for echo/data-ready flag.
   - Capture raw digital value or ADC count.

3. **Signal Conditioning (PROCESS Phase)**
   - Apply calibration constants.
   - Filter noise (moving average, Kalman filter).
   - Map raw counts to physical units.

4. **Display Rendering (OUTPUT Phase)**
   - Format the numeric value with proper units.
   - Update cursor position on LCD or print to terminal.
   - Handle display refresh rate.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why initialize first?** Because uninitialized pins float randomly, causing false triggers.
- **Why condition the signal?** Raw ADC values are non-linear, temperature-dependent, and noisy.
- **Why format output?** Engineers and end-users need SI units, not raw binary counts.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these equations — they appear in **every KTU sensor question paper**.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{in} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | ADC count → Voltage conversion | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $d = \frac{t_{echo} \times v_{sound}}{2}$ | Ultrasonic distance (round trip divided by 2) | meters (m) |
| 3 | $v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times T$ | Speed of sound vs. temperature | m/s |
| 4 | $T_{C} = \frac{V_{out} - 0.5}{0.01}$ | LM35 temperature conversion | °C |
| 5 | $\text{value} = \text{map}(x, in_{min}, in_{max}, out_{min}, out_{max})$ | Arduino linear mapping function | unitless |
| 6 | $\text{Res.} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | ADC resolution (LSB weight) | V/count |
| 7 | $f_{sample} = \frac{1}{T_{period}}$ | Sampling frequency relation | Hz |
| 8 | $I = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{N}$ | Moving average (N samples) | same as $x_i$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas power real-world applications — autonomous vehicle LiDAR, medical ventilators (pressure sensors), industrial PLCs (4–20 mA current loops), and smartphone accelerometers. In cognitive robotics specifically, the distance formula $d = \frac{t_{echo} \times v_{sound}}{2}$ is the bedrock of obstacle avoidance behavior trees.

### Worked Numerical Example: Ultrasonic Distance

**Given:** $t_{echo} = 588\ \mu s$, $T = 25\ °C$.

**Step 1:** Compute speed of sound.
$$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times 25 = 331 + 15 = 346\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute distance.
$$d = \frac{588 \times 10^{-6} \times 346}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{0.203448}{2} = 0.1017\ \text{m} = 10.17\ \text{cm}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 3.1: ADC Count to Voltage Mapping

A **10-bit ADC** produces integer values from **0 to 1023**, where:

$$
0 \rightarrow 0\ \text{V}, \quad 1023 \rightarrow V_{ref}
$$

This is a **linear** mapping. Using the two-point form:

$$
\frac{V_{in} - 0}{ADC - 0} = \frac{V_{ref} - 0}{1023 - 0}
$$

$$
V_{in} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023}
$$

Substituting the **standard reference** $V_{ref} = 5.0\ \text{V}$:

$$
V_{in} = \frac{ADC \times 5.0}{1023} \approx \frac{ADC \times 5.0}{1024}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{in} = \frac{ADC}{204.6}}
$$

### Derivation 3.2: Resolution (LSB) Calculation

The smallest detectable voltage change (Least Significant Bit weight):

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}
$$

For $V_{ref} = 5.0\ \text{V}$ and $n = 10$:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} = 4.88\ \text{mV/count}
$$

### Derivation 3.3: LM35 Linear Temperature Sensor

The LM35 outputs **10 mV per °C** with a 0.5 V offset at 0 °C:

$$
T(°C) = \frac{V_{out} - 0.5}{0.01}
$$

If the ADC reads **204**:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{204 \times 5.0}{1024} = 0.996\ \text{V}
$$

$$
T = \frac{0.996 - 0.5}{0.01} = \frac{0.496}{0.01} = 49.6\ °C
$$

### Derivation 3.4: Ultrasonic Range Equation Derivation

The HC-SR04 emits a 40 kHz pulse, listens for the echo, and returns a HIGH pulse of duration $t_{echo}$. The wave travels distance $d$ to the object and **$d$ back** (round trip).

$$
\text{Distance traveled} = v_{sound} \times t_{echo} = 2d
$$

$$
d = \frac{v_{sound} \times t_{echo}}{2}
$$

For air at **20 °C**, $v_{sound} \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$. If we want $d$ in cm, multiply by 100:

$$
d(\text{cm}) = \frac{343 \times 100 \times t_{echo}(\text{s})}{2}
$$

$$
d(\text{cm}) = 17150 \times t_{echo}(\text{s})
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
d(\text{cm}) = \frac{t_{echo}(\mu s)}{58.2}
$$

### Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison Table

| Parameter | HC-SR04 (Ultrasonic) | DHT11 (Temp/Humid) | MPU6050 (IMU) | LM35 (Temp) |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Type** | Active | Passive | Active | Passive |
| **Range** | 2–400 cm | 0–50 °C | ±2000 °/s | −55 to +150 °C |
| **Protocol** | GPIO Trigger/Echo | Single-wire | I2C (0x68) | Analog |
| **Accuracy** | ±3 mm | ±2 °C | ±0.01 °/s | ±0.5 °C |
| **Update Rate** | 40 Hz | 1 Hz | 1 kHz | Continuous |
| **Supply** | 5 V | 3.3–5 V | 3.3 V | 4–30 V |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 4.1: Sensor Reading Display — Program Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Program] --> B[Initialize Serial 9600 bps]
    B --> C[Initialize LCD I2C Address 0x27]
    C --> D[Set Pin Modes Trigger OUTPUT Echo INPUT]
    D --> E[Loop Begin]
    E --> F[Send 10 us Pulse to Trigger Pin]
    F --> G[Measure Echo Pulse Duration]
    G --> H{Duration > 0?}
    H -- No --> I[Display OUT OF RANGE]
    H -- Yes --> J[Compute distance cm equals duration divided by 58.2]
    J --> K[Format String Distance value cm]
    K --> L[Print to Serial Monitor]
    L --> M[Update LCD Row 1 Col 0]
    M --> N[Delay 100 ms]
    N --> E
    I --> N
```

### Diagram 4.2: Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSE_LAYER
        S1[HC-SR04 Ultrasonic]
        S2[DHT11 Temp Sensor]
        S3[LM35 Analog Temp]
    end

    subgraph INTERFACE_LAYER
        ADC[10 bit ADC]
        DIG[Digital GPIO]
        I2C_BUS[I2C Bus 100 kHz]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS_LAYER
        MCU[Microcontroller Unit]
        CALIB[Calibration Constants]
        FILTER[Moving Average Filter]
    end

    subgraph DISPLAY_LAYER
        LCD[16x2 LCD Display]
        SER[Serial Terminal]
        GUI[Python GUI Dashboard]
    end

    S1 --> DIG
    S2 --> DIG
    S3 --> ADC
    ADC --> MCU
    DIG --> MCU
    I2C_BUS --> LCD
    MCU --> CALIB
    CALIB --> FILTER
    FILTER --> LCD
    FILTER --> SER
    SER --> GUI
```

### Diagram 4.3: Sense-Plan-Act Cognitive Loop with Display

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[Environment] -->|Stimuli| SENS[Sensors]
    SENS -->|Raw Data| PROC[Perception Processor]
    PROC -->|Filtered Values| DISP[Display Module]
    DISP -->|Visual Feedback| OP[Human Operator]
    OP -->|Decision| PLAN[Planner]
    PLAN -->|Action| ACT[Actuators]
    ACT -->|Effect| ENV
    PROC -->|Filtered Values| PLAN
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **display module** is the critical bridge between machine cognition and human oversight. Without it, the robot operates as a **"black box"** — a violation of KTU's CO4 (transparency in cognitive systems).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the function of an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) in a sensor reading display program. State the resolution of a 10-bit ADC with a 5 V reference.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **ADC** converts continuous analog sensor voltages into discrete digital counts that a microcontroller can process. For a 10-bit ADC:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} = 4.88\ \text{mV per count}
$$

It uses **successive approximation** to map $0\ \text{V} \rightarrow 0$ and $5\ \text{V} \rightarrow 1023$. The digital value is then converted to engineering units using linear scaling formulas. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Write the formula to compute distance using the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor. Why do we divide the travel time by 2?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
d = \frac{v_{sound} \times t_{echo}}{2}
$$

We divide by **2** because the ultrasonic pulse travels from the sensor **to the object AND back** to the sensor (round trip). The total distance covered is $2d$, so the actual one-way distance is half the measured path. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Ultrasonic Distance Measurement with LCD Display*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Draw and explain the program flow to interface an **HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor** with an Arduino and display distance on a 16x2 LCD.

**Model Solution:**

**Hardware Connections:**

- VCC → 5V
- GND → GND
- TRIG → Pin 9
- ECHO → Pin 10
- LCD SDA → A4, SCL → A5 (I2C)

**Working Logic:**

1. Set TRIG as OUTPUT, ECHO as INPUT.
2. Send a 10 µs HIGH pulse to TRIG.
3. Measure ECHO pulse width using `pulseIn()`.
4. Compute: $d = t_{echo} / 58.2$ (in cm).
5. Display: `lcd.print(d); lcd.print(" cm");`

**Reference Program:**

```c
#include <Wire.h>
#include <LiquidCrystal_I2C.h>

LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 16, 2);
const int trigPin = 9;
const int echoPin = 10;

void setup() {
  lcd.init();
  lcd.backlight();
  pinMode(trigPin, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(echoPin, INPUT);
  Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW);
  delayMicroseconds(2);
  digitalWrite(trigPin, HIGH);
  delayMicroseconds(10);
  digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW);

  long duration = pulseIn(echoPin, HIGH, 30000);
  float distance = duration * 0.0343 / 2.0;

  lcd.setCursor(0, 0);
  lcd.print("Dist: ");
  lcd.print(distance);
  lcd.print(" cm   ");

  Serial.print("Distance: ");
  Serial.print(distance);
  Serial.println(" cm");

  delay(100);
}
```

**Explanation:** `0.0343 cm/µs` is the speed of sound in air at 20 °C. Dividing by 2 corrects for round-trip travel. `pulseIn()` returns the echo pulse width in microseconds; a 30 ms timeout prevents infinite blocking. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss two **common errors** in sensor reading programs and how to mitigate them.

**Model Solution:**

**Error 1 — Floating Pin / No Echo Received:**
- **Symptom:** LCD displays "0 cm" or "Out of range" continuously.
- **Cause:** Object beyond 4 m range, or sensor misalignment.
- **Mitigation:** Use a timeout in `pulseIn()`, add a validity check `if (duration == 0) display("No Echo");`.

**Error 2 — Noisy ADC Readings:**
- **Symptom:** Temperature flickers between 24.3 °C and 26.1 °C.
- **Cause:** Electrical noise, poor grounding, sensor nonlinearity.
- **Mitigation:** Apply a **moving average filter** over N=10 samples:
$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i
$$
Or use a **median filter** to reject outlier spikes. Also, add a 100 nF decoupling capacitor near the sensor VCC pin.

**Additional mitigations:** Use shielded cables for long sensor wiring, average multiple readings before display, and implement hysteresis to prevent display flicker at threshold values. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Temperature Monitoring with LM35 and Serial Plotter*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Design a program to read **LM35 temperature sensor** on Arduino analog pin A0 and display the value in °C on the **Serial Monitor** every 500 ms. Include the derivation of the conversion formula.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**
LM35 outputs 10 mV/°C linearly. With $V_{ref} = 5\ \text{V}$ and 10-bit ADC:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{ADC \times 5.0}{1024}
$$

$$
T(°C) = \frac{V_{out}}{0.01} = \frac{ADC \times 5.0}{1024 \times 0.01} = \frac{ADC \times 5.0}{10.24}
$$

$$
T(°C) = \frac{ADC}{2.048}
$$

**Program:**

```c
const int lm35Pin = A0;
float voltage, tempC;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  Serial.println("Time(ms)\tTemperature(C)");
}

void loop() {
  int adcValue = analogRead(lm35Pin);
  voltage = adcValue * (5.0 / 1024.0);
  tempC = voltage / 0.01;

  Serial.print(millis());
  Serial.print("\t");
  Serial.println(tempC);

  delay(500);
}
```

**Explanation:** The tab `\t` separator enables use with **Arduino Serial Plotter** for real-time visualization. `millis()` returns elapsed time in ms, providing the x-axis. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **DHT11** and **LM35** temperature sensors. Justify which is more suitable for a **cognitive robot operating in a humid greenhouse environment**.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | DHT11 | LM35 |
|-----------|-------|------|
| **Humidity Sensing** | Yes (20–90% RH) | No |
| **Interface** | Single-wire digital | Analog |
| **Library Required** | `DHT.h` | None |
| **Accuracy** | ±2 °C | ±0.5 °C |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low |

**Justification for Greenhouse:**
A **DHT11** is more suitable because:
1. Greenhouses have **high humidity (60–90% RH)**, which directly affects plant transpiration and robot navigation (slippery surfaces). The DHT11 provides both **temperature and humidity** in a single package.
2. Cognitive robots in greenhouses need **multi-modal perception** — humidity influences watering decisions and path planning.
3. The ±2 °C accuracy is acceptable for environmental monitoring, while LM35's lack of humidity sensing is a critical limitation.

However, if the application requires **precision temperature control** (e.g., a fermentation chamber), the LM35 is preferable. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the division by 2** in the ultrasonic distance formula — costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Not converting ADC value to voltage first** before applying the LM35 formula — full step-marks lost.
> 3. **Missing `pinMode()` setup** in program — examiners deduct **1 mark** for incomplete initialization.
> 4. **No timeout in `pulseIn()`** — causes the program to hang, but students often ignore this in code.
> 5. **Displaying float without decimals** — use `lcd.print(value, 1)` to show 1 decimal place, else marks deducted for poor UX.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **ADC Resolution Formula:** $\text{Res.} = V_{ref} / 2^n$ — for 10-bit, 5V → **4.88 mV/count**.
- **Ultrasonic Distance:** $d = t_{echo} \times 0.0343 / 2$ (cm), or shortcut $d = t_{echo} / 58.2$.
- **LM35:** $T = V_{out} / 0.01$, with $V_{out} = ADC \times 5.0 / 1024$.
- **I2C LCD Address:** commonly **0x27** or **0x3F** — always check with I2C scanner.
- **Serial.begin(9600)** must match the Serial Monitor baud rate.
- **Moving Average Filter** is the simplest noise-reduction technique for ADC.
- **`pulseIn()` timeout** prevents infinite blocking when no echo returns.
- **Floating-point math** on 8-bit Arduino is slow — avoid `float` in tight loops if performance is critical.
- **Sampling rate** must be ≥ 2× the signal frequency (**Nyquist criterion**).
- **Display refresh** should be throttled (≥ 100 ms) to avoid LCD ghosting and serial buffer overflow.
- **Sense-Plan-Act (SPA) loop** requires the display module to close the human-robot feedback loop.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program for Avoid-Obstacles Behavior
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT863 - COGNITIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program for Avoid-Obstacles Behavior

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid-Obstacles Behavior** is a fundamental **reactive behavior** in cognitive and behavior-based robotics wherein a robot continuously monitors its environment through onboard sensors and dynamically alters its trajectory (speed, direction, or both) to prevent collision with static or dynamic obstacles. It is the cornerstone of **autonomous navigation** in unknown environments.

In the context of **Behavior-Based Robotics (BBR)** and the **Subsumption Architecture** (Rodney Brooks, 1986), avoid-obstacles is typically implemented as the **lowest-priority layer (Layer 0)** of a layered control system. It operates on a tight **Sense → Think → Act** loop without requiring an explicit world model.

**KTU Syllabus Keywords (Match These Exactly):**
- Reactive Behavior / Reflexive Behavior
- Sensor Subsumption
- Threshold-Based Decision Making
- Stimulus-Response Mapping
- Finite State Machine (FSM) for Behavior Arbitration

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Walking in a Dark Room with a Flashlight:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded in a room filled with furniture. You hold a flashlight in one hand and slowly sweep it left-right. The instant the light beam detects an object closer than arm's length, you instinctively (1) **stop moving forward**, (2) **turn your body** in the direction with the most clearance, and (3) **resume forward motion** once the path is clear. You do **not** maintain a mental map of every chair and table — you simply react to the *closest* thing.
>
> A robot with avoid-obstacles behavior does the **exact same thing** with **ultrasonic / IR / LiDAR sensors** replacing the flashlight and **motor commands** replacing your legs.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the **industry-standard measurement ranges** used in KTU lab evaluations:

| Sensor Type | Typical Range | Resolution | Update Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HC-SR04 Ultrasonic** | 2 cm – 400 cm | 0.3 cm | 40 Hz |
| **Sharp IR (GP2Y0A21)** | 10 cm – 80 cm | 0.5 cm | 26 Hz |
| **Bumper / Tactile** | 0 cm (contact) | Binary | 1000 Hz |
| **LiDAR (2D scan)** | 0.1 m – 12 m | ±1 cm | 5–20 Hz |

> [!TIP]
> **Threshold Constant (D_th):** The most critical tuning parameter. For indoor KTU lab robots, a safe value is **D_th = 20 cm**. Below this distance, the "avoid" reflex overrides the "wander" reflex.

### 1.4 Behavior Classification in Cognitive Robotics

- **Reactive (Reflexive):** No memory, no map. Purely stimulus-response (used in this topic).
- **Deliberative:** Builds a map, plans paths using A*/Dijkstra.
- **Hybrid:** Combines reactive layer (avoid) with deliberative layer (goal-seek) — *most modern systems*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Avoid-Obstacle Triad — Sense, Decide, Act

The behavior is decomposed into three concurrent processes. KTU examiners love asking you to "list and explain the three phases."

**① Sense (Perception Layer)**
- Read distance `d_i` from each sensor `i ∈ {1, 2, …, n}`.
- For a 2-wheel differential robot, typically **n = 3 sensors**: Left, Front, Right.
- Apply noise filtering: *exponential moving average* or *median filter*.

**② Decide (Arbiter / Behavior Layer)**
- Compute minimum distance: $d_{min} = \min(d_{Left}, d_{Front}, d_{Right})$
- Compare $d_{min}$ against the **safe threshold** $D_{th}$.
- If $d_{min} < D_{th}$ → **AVOID mode active**, else → **WANDER mode active**.

**③ Act (Actuation Layer)**
- Translate decision into motor PWM / voltage signals.
- Avoid mode: turn the wheel on the side closest to the obstacle (or reverse + turn).
- Wander mode: drive both wheels forward at cruise speed.

### 2.2 The Stimulus-Response Mapping Function

The behavior can be formally expressed as a mathematical function $B$ mapping a sensor vector to actuator outputs:

$$B: \mathbb{R}^{n} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m}$$

where $n$ is the number of sensors and $m$ is the number of actuators (typically 2 for differential drive). The canonical form is:

$$v_L = f_L(d_{Left}, d_{Front}, d_{Right})$$

$$v_R = f_R(d_{Left}, d_{Front}, d_{Right})$$

Here, $v_L$ and $v_R$ are the **left and right wheel velocities** in cm/s or PWM duty cycle (%).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table for the exam. These equations appear almost every year.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{pulse} = \frac{d \times 2}{v_{sound}}$ | Ultrasonic time-of-flight | seconds |
| 2 | $v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | Speed of sound at temperature T | m/s |
| 3 | $d_{min} = \min(d_i)$ for all i | Smallest sensed distance | cm |
| 4 | $\Delta v = v_L - v_R$ | Differential velocity (turning rate proxy) | cm/s |
| 5 | $d_{th} = 20$ cm (lab default) | Safety threshold | cm |
| 6 | $v_{cruise} = 50$ (0–255 PWM) | Forward wander speed | unitless |
| 7 | $v_{turn} = 30$ (0–255 PWM) | Avoidance turn speed | unitless |
| 8 | $\omega = \frac{\Delta v}{W}$ | Angular velocity (W = wheelbase) | rad/s |
| 9 | $R_{turn} = \frac{W}{2} \cdot \frac{v_L + v_R}{v_L - v_R}$ | Instantaneous turn radius | cm |
| 10 | $T_{obstacle} = 1 - \frac{d_{min}}{d_{th}}$ | Obstacle threat level (0 to 1) | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Avoid-obstacle behavior is the **backbone of every autonomous system** you interact with:
> - **Roomba / robotic vacuum cleaners** (iRobot)
> - **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in Amazon warehouses
> - **Self-driving cars** (Tesla FSD, Waymo) — *they use the exact same Sense-Decide-Act loop, just with much better sensors*
> - **Mars rovers** (NASA Curiosity, Perseverance) — *their entire "AutoNav" subsystem is built on layered avoid behaviors*
> - **Drone collision avoidance** (DJI, Skydio)

### 2.5 Subsumption Architecture Layering (Brooks)

In KTU 2024 scheme, you may be asked how avoid-obstacles fits into the **Subsumption Architecture**:

| Layer | Behavior | Can Suppress |
|---|---|---|
| 3 (Top) | Explore / Map Building | Layer 0, 1, 2 |
| 2 | Wander (Goal-seeking) | Layer 0, 1 |
| 1 | Avoid-Obstacles (this topic) | Layer 0 |
| 0 (Bottom) | Reflexive Stop (bumper hit) | None |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Decision Rule

We derive the formal condition under which the robot transitions from *Wander* to *Avoid*.

**Step 1 — Define the sensor vector.**
At time step $k$, the robot obtains:

$$S(k) = [d_L(k), \quad d_F(k), \quad d_R(k)]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Define the safety predicate.**
A distance is "safe" if and only if it exceeds the threshold:

$$safe(d_i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } d_i \geq D_{th} \\ 0 & \text{if } d_i < D_{th} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the global safety.**

$$Safe_{global}(k) = safe(d_L(k)) \land safe(d_F(k)) \land safe(d_R(k))$$

**Step 4 — Formulate the behavior decision.**

$$\text{Mode}(k) = \begin{cases} \text{WANDER} & \text{if } Safe_{global}(k) = 1 \\ \text{AVOID} & \text{if } Safe_{global}(k) = 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the avoid-action selection rule.**
When in AVOID mode, the robot must pick the side with the *maximum clearance*:

$$\text{TurnDirection} = \arg\max_i (d_i) \quad \text{subject to } i \in \{L, R\}$$

This is the *core heuristic* of the avoid-obstacles algorithm.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Sensor Reading Interpretation

**Problem:** A KTU lab robot reads the following values: $d_L = 35$ cm, $d_F = 12$ cm, $d_R = 28$ cm. Threshold $D_{th} = 20$ cm. Determine the robot's mode and action.

**Solution (step-by-step as expected in the exam):**

- Step 1: Compare each $d_i$ with $D_{th} = 20$.
  - $d_L = 35 \geq 20$ → safe
  - $d_F = 12 < 20$ → **UNSAFE**
  - $d_R = 28 \geq 20$ → safe

- Step 2: Since the front sensor is unsafe, $Safe_{global} = 0$.

- Step 3: Mode = **AVOID**.

- Step 4: Compare left vs. right (since front is blocked):

$$\max(d_L, d_R) = \max(35, 28) = 35$$

- Step 5: Turn **LEFT** (left is clearer).

- Step 6: Actuator output — execute `TurnLeft()` function: set $v_L < v_R$ (or $v_L$ negative for in-place rotation).

### 3.3 The Reference Program (Pseudo-code for KTU Exam)

> [!NOTE]
> The following program is the **canonical answer** for the KTU question: *"Write a program to implement the avoid-obstacles behavior of a mobile robot."* Memorize the structure.

```
PROGRAM: AvoidObstaclesBehavior
  // ---------- CONFIGURATION ----------
  CONSTANT  D_th        = 20      // cm - safety threshold
  CONSTANT  V_CRUISE    = 50      // forward speed (PWM 0-255)
  CONSTANT  V_TURN      = 30      // turning speed
  CONSTANT  TURN_DELAY  = 500     // ms

  // ---------- MAIN LOOP (runs forever) ----------
  BEGIN MAIN
    InitializeSensors()
    InitializeMotors()
    CalibrateSensors()
    
    WHILE (TRUE) DO
        
        // [STEP 1] SENSE - read 3 distance sensors
        d_L  = ReadUltrasonic(SENSOR_LEFT)
        d_F  = ReadUltrasonic(SENSOR_FRONT)
        d_R  = ReadUltrasonic(SENSOR_RIGHT)
        
        // [STEP 2] DECIDE - find minimum and check threshold
        d_min = MIN(d_L, d_F, d_R)
        
        IF (d_min < D_th) THEN
            // [STEP 3a] AVOID MODE
            IF (d_L < d_R) THEN
                TurnRight(V_TURN, TURN_DELAY)
            ELSE
                TurnLeft(V_TURN, TURN_DELAY)
            END IF
        ELSE
            // [STEP 3b] WANDER MODE
            MoveForward(V_CRUISE)
        END IF
        
    END WHILE
  END MAIN
END PROGRAM
```

### 3.4 Enhanced Version — With Threat-Level Modulation

This advanced version uses the **threat level** $T_{obstacle}$ from the formula sheet to *gradually* slow down rather than abruptly stopping — biologically inspired by how humans brake:

```
PROGRAM: AvoidObstaclesProportional
  CONSTANT  D_th  = 20
  CONSTANT  D_min = 5      // absolute minimum - hard stop
  
  WHILE (TRUE) DO
    d_min = GetMinimumDistance()
    
    IF (d_min <= D_min) THEN
        StopMotors()                    // hard emergency stop
    
    ELSE IF (d_min < D_th) THEN
        T_obstacle = 1 - (d_min / D_th) // threat in [0,1]
        v_reduced  = V_CRUISE * (1 - T_obstacle)
        TurnAwayFromObstacle(v_reduced)
    
    ELSE
        MoveForward(V_CRUISE)          // safe - full speed
    END IF
  END WHILE
END PROGRAM
```

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Behavior Comparison

| Behavior Type | Memory? | Map? | Response Time | Computation | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Avoid-Obstacles (this topic)** | No | No | < 50 ms | Very Low | Roomba, Mars rover reflex layer |
| Wall-Following | No | No | < 50 ms | Low | Maze-solving robots |
| Goal-Seeking | No | No | < 100 ms | Low | Line-follower with target |
| Path-Planning (A*) | Yes | Yes | 100 ms – 10 s | High | Self-driving cars |
| SLAM + Plan | Yes | Yes (live) | 1–30 s | Very High | Autonomous vehicles |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Sense-Decide-Act Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Robot Boot and Init"] --> B["Read Sensors: dL, dF, dR"]
    B --> C{"d_min less than D_th"}
    C -- "NO, path clear" --> D["Wander Mode: Move Forward"]
    C -- "YES, obstacle near" --> E{"dL less than dR"}
    E -- "YES, left blocked" --> F["Turn Right"]
    E -- "NO, right blocked" --> G["Turn Left"]
    D --> H["Delay 100 ms"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> B
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Sub-system View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERC["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        P1["Ultrasonic Sensor Left"]
        P2["Ultrasonic Sensor Front"]
        P3["Ultrasonic Sensor Right"]
        P4["Median Filter and Noise Reduction"]
    end

    subgraph COG["COGNITION LAYER"]
        C1["Min Distance Calculator"]
        C2["Threshold Comparator"]
        C3["Direction Arbiter"]
        C4["Threat Level Estimator"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        A1["Left Motor Driver"]
        A2["Right Motor Driver"]
        A3["PWM Generator"]
    end

    P1 --> P4
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C3
    C4 --> C3
    C3 --> A3
    A3 --> A1
    A3 --> A2
```

### 4.3 Subsumption Layering — Avoid-Obstacles as Layer 1

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L3["LAYER 3: Explore and Build Map (highest priority)"]
    L2["LAYER 2: Wander and Goal Seek"]
    L1["LAYER 1: AVOID OBSTACLES (this topic)"]
    L0["LAYER 0: Emergency Stop on Bumper Hit"]

    L3 -- "suppresses" --> L2
    L2 -- "suppresses" --> L1
    L1 -- "suppresses" --> L0
```

### 4.4 Finite State Machine Representation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> WANDER : "Sensors Calibrated"
    WANDER --> AVOID : "d_min less than D_th"
    AVOID --> WANDER : "Path Cleared and d_min greater than D_th"
    AVOID --> EMERGENCY : "d_min less than D_critical"
    EMERGENCY --> WANDER : "Manual Reset or Bumper Released"
    note right of AVOID : "Turn away from nearest obstacle side"
    note right of WANDER : "Cruise forward at V_CRUISE"
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT863]**
*Define reactive behavior in robotics. Explain how avoid-obstacles is classified as a reactive behavior.*
