**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Reactive behavior** is a control paradigm in robotics where the robot's actions are generated as a direct, immediate function of current sensor readings, **without using an internal world model or historical memory**.
>
> Avoid-obstacles qualifies as a reactive behavior because:
> 1. **No memory:** It does not store past sensor readings or remember previously seen obstacles.
> 2. **No explicit map:** It does not maintain a geometric representation of the environment.
> 3. **Tight loop:** Sensor read → threshold check → motor command happens within a few milliseconds.
> 4. **Stimulus-response:** The output is purely a function of the *current* stimulus (distance).
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for 4 correct justifications — examiner's key.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT863]**
*List the three sensors commonly used for obstacle detection in mobile robots and state one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Sensor | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ultrasonic (HC-SR04) | Inexpensive, works on most surfaces, range up to 4 m |
| 2 | Infrared (Sharp GP2Y0A21) | Fast response, low power, ideal for short range |
| 3 | Bumper / Tactile switch | 100% reliable at contact, zero false negatives |

*(1 mark per correct row.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PERUT863]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw and explain the three-layer Sense-Decide-Act architecture for implementing the avoid-obstacles behavior of a differential-drive mobile robot. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete program (pseudo-code or C/Python for Arduino) for the avoid-obstacles behavior. Assume the robot has 3 ultrasonic sensors (Left, Front, Right) connected to digital pins. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sense-Decide-Act Architecture — 7 marks**

| Layer | Function | Components | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sense (Perception)** | Acquire environmental data | 3 × HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensors, median filter, ADC/time-of-flight conversion | 2 |
| **Decide (Cognition)** | Compare distances to threshold, select turn direction | `min()` function, threshold comparator, argmax arbiter | 3 |
| **Act (Actuation)** | Convert decisions into motor commands | L298N motor driver, PWM generator, H-bridge | 2 |

The Sense-Decide-Act loop is executed at approximately **10 Hz** (every 100 ms) on a typical Arduino Uno. The Sense layer produces the vector $S(k) = [d_L, d_F, d_R]^T$, the Decide layer computes $d_{min}$ and the turn direction, and the Act layer outputs PWM signals to the left and right motors.

**(b) Program — 7 marks** (Complete, exam-ready Arduino code)

```c
// ===== AVOID-OBSTACLES BEHAVIOR - ARDUINO =====
// Course: PERUT863 - Cognitive Robotics, KTU 2024

const int trigL = 2, echoL = 3;
const int trigF = 4, echoF = 5;
const int trigR = 6, echoR = 7;
const int enA   = 9, enB   = 10;   // PWM enable pins
const int in1   = 8, in2   = 11;   // Left motor direction
const int in3   = 12, in4 = 13;    // Right motor direction

const int D_TH        = 20;        // cm threshold
const int V_CRUISE    = 150;       // forward PWM
const int V_TURN      = 100;       // turning PWM

// ---------- Function: read distance in cm ----------
long readDistance(int trigPin, int echoPin) {
    digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW);  delayMicroseconds(2);
    digitalWrite(trigPin, HIGH); delayMicroseconds(10);
    digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW);
    long duration = pulseIn(echoPin, HIGH, 30000);
    return duration * 0.034 / 2;
}

// ---------- Function: move forward ----------
void moveForward(int speed) {
    analogWrite(enA, speed);
    analogWrite(enB, speed);
    digitalWrite(in1, HIGH); digitalWrite(in2, LOW);
    digitalWrite(in3, HIGH); digitalWrite(in4, LOW);
}

// ---------- Function: turn left (right wheel faster) ----------
void turnLeft(int speed) {
    analogWrite(enA, speed / 2);    // left slow
    analogWrite(enB, speed);        // right fast
    digitalWrite(in1, HIGH); digitalWrite(in2, LOW);
    digitalWrite(in3, HIGH); digitalWrite(in4, LOW);
}

// ---------- Function: turn right (left wheel faster) ----------
void turnRight(int speed) {
    analogWrite(enA, speed);        // left fast
    analogWrite(enB, speed / 2);    // right slow
    digitalWrite(in1, HIGH); digitalWrite(in2, LOW);
    digitalWrite(in3, HIGH); digitalWrite(in4, LOW);
}

// ---------- Function: stop ----------
void stopMotors() {
    analogWrite(enA, 0);
    analogWrite(enB, 0);
}

// =================== MAIN LOOP ===================
void setup() {
    pinMode(trigL, OUTPUT); pinMode(echoL, INPUT);
    pinMode(trigF, OUTPUT); pinMode(echoF, INPUT);
    pinMode(trigR, OUTPUT); pinMode(echoR, INPUT);
    pinMode(enA, OUTPUT);   pinMode(enB, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(in1, OUTPUT);   pinMode(in2, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(in3, OUTPUT);   pinMode(in4, OUTPUT);
    Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
    // STEP 1: SENSE
    long dL = readDistance(trigL, echoL);
    long dF = readDistance(trigF, echoF);
    long dR = readDistance(trigR, echoR);
    long d_min = min(dL, min(dF, dR));

    // STEP 2 & 3: DECIDE and ACT
    if (d_min < D_TH) {
        // AVOID mode
        if (dL < dR) turnRight(V_TURN);
        else         turnLeft(V_TURN);
    } else {
        // WANDER mode
        moveForward(V_CRUISE);
    }

    delay(100);   // 10 Hz control loop
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `pulseIn(..., 30000)` timeout** → sensor hangs at 30 ms, robot freezes. (-1 mark)
> 2. **No `delay(100)` at loop end** → CPU runs at 100% and ultrasonic sensors cross-talk. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Wrong turn logic:** Student turns *toward* the obstacle (dL < dR → turnLeft). This is the #1 mistake. Always turn **AWAY**: dL < dR means *left is closer* → turn **RIGHT**. (-2 marks)
> 4. **Missing pinMode() calls** in `setup()`. (-0.5 mark)
> 5. **Using `delay()` inside the sense function** instead of `delayMicroseconds()`. (-0.5 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Subsumption Architecture** of Rodney Brooks. With a neat diagram, show how avoid-obstacles fits as a layer in this architecture. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between **reactive**, **deliberative**, and **hybrid** control architectures. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (Concise Key Points)

**(a) Subsumption Architecture — 7 marks:**
- Proposed by **Rodney Brooks (1986)** at MIT.
- Decomposes robot control into **layers of competence**, each implementing a complete behavior.
- Layers run **concurrently**; higher layers can **suppress (subsume)** the outputs of lower layers.
- Avoid-obstacles is **Layer 1** (above the reflexive stop, below goal-seeking).
- Communication is via **inhibitory/suppressive messages**, not via shared memory.
- *(Full mark requires: definition + 3 layers named + suppression mechanism + diagram.)*

**(b) Reactive vs Deliberative vs Hybrid — 7 marks:**

| Architecture | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Reactive** (e.g., avoid-obstacles) | Extremely fast, robust to sensor noise, no map needed | Cannot plan long-horizon goals, gets stuck in local minima |
| **Deliberative** (e.g., A* planner) | Globally optimal paths, handles complex missions | Slow, brittle to map errors, computationally expensive |
| **Hybrid** (e.g., layered) | Combines speed of reactive with planning of deliberative | Complex arbitration, difficult to debug, layer conflicts |

*(1 mark for each correct row × 3 architectures + 1 mark for stating that avoid-obstacles is the reactive component of a hybrid system = 4 marks for the table + 3 marks for the explanation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Avoid-Obstacles Specific:**
> 1. Do NOT confuse **avoid-obstacles** with **path-planning**. The former is reactive (no map); the latter is deliberative. (-2 marks if mixed)
> 2. In your diagrams, **label all three sensor variables** $d_L$, $d_F$, $d_R$ — examiners check for this. (-1 mark)
> 3. Always state the **units** of threshold (cm) and **update rate** (Hz). (-0.5 mark each omission)
> 4. In code, the line `if (d_min < D_TH)` is the **most-graded single line** in this module — get it right.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ **Avoid-obstacles is a REACTIVE behavior** — no memory, no map, no planning.
- ☐ The three phases are: **Sense → Decide → Act**, executed in a tight loop (typically 10 Hz).
- ☐ Default lab threshold: **D_th = 20 cm**. Default speeds: cruise = 50 PWM, turn = 30 PWM.
- ☐ Turn **AWAY** from the closer side: if `dL < dR`, turn **RIGHT** (most common bug: turning toward the obstacle).
- ☐ In **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986)**, avoid-obstacles is **Layer 1**, above the bumper-stop (Layer 0) and below goal-seeking (Layer 2).
- ☐ The three sensors are typically **Left, Front, Right** — total 3 HC-SR04 ultrasonic modules.
- ☐ Ultrasonic distance formula: $d = \frac{t_{echo} \times v_{sound}}{2}$ where $v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s at 20°C.
- ☐ **Threat level** (proportional avoidance): $T_{obstacle} = 1 - \dfrac{d_{min}}{D_{th}}$, ranges from 0 (safe) to 1 (collision imminent).
- ☐ `pulseIn(echoPin, HIGH, 30000)` — always include the 30 ms timeout to prevent blocking.
- ☐ Control loop delay: `delay(100)` → 10 Hz refresh rate, prevents ultrasonic cross-talk.
- ☐ Real-world applications: **Roomba, Mars rovers, AGVs, self-driving cars, drones**.
- ☐ Differentiate clearly: **Reactive** (fast, no map) vs **Deliberative** (slow, map-based) vs **Hybrid** (combines both).
- ☐ Emergency stop: if `d_min < D_critical` (typically 5 cm), call `stopMotors()` immediately.
- ☐ Exam mantra: *"Sense-Decide-Act, Threshold-Compare, Turn-Away-From-Obstacle."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program for Wandering Within the Workspace
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT863 - COGNITIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program for Wandering Within the Workspace

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Workspace Wandering** is a fundamental behavior in cognitive robotics defined as the autonomous, non-repeating exploration of a robot's operational environment (the *workspace*) through reactive control strategies, where the robot continuously senses its surroundings and generates motion commands to traverse free space while avoiding collisions with obstacles and workspace boundaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** In cognitive robotics, wandering is implemented as a *sensorimotor loop* — a closed feedback cycle where proximity/infrared/ultrasonic sensor readings are mapped in real time to wheel velocities, producing emergent exploratory trajectories without requiring an explicit world map.

Mathematically, the wandering behavior can be expressed as a discrete-time control law:

$$
\mathbf{v}(t+1) = f\bigl(\mathbf{S}(t),\, \mathbf{v}(t)\bigr)
$$

where $\mathbf{S}(t)$ is the sensor vector at time $t$, $\mathbf{v}(t)$ is the wheel-velocity vector, and $f(\cdot)$ is the reactive coupling function.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Blind Person in an Unfamiliar Room:** Imagine you are placed inside an unfamiliar living room with your eyes closed, holding a long stick in each hand. You walk forward by instinct. The moment your left stick touches the sofa, you instinctively veer right. If both sticks hit walls, you take a small step back and pivot. You never "remember" the room, yet you successfully explore every corner. **This is exactly how a wandering robot behaves** — it has no internal map; it reacts purely to what its sensors detect right now.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Workspace** | The bounded 2D (or 3D) region in which the robot is permitted to operate. | Defines the operational envelope. |
| **Obstacle** | Any object within the workspace that the robot must not collide with. | Triggers reactive avoidance behavior. |
| **Free Space** | Subset of the workspace unoccupied by obstacles — the valid motion region. | Robot's admissible trajectory domain. |
| **Sensor Vector $\mathbf{S}$** | Vector of $n$ proximity sensor readings sampled at time $t$. | Drives the reactive control law. |
| **Wheel Velocity $\mathbf{v}$** | Vector containing the angular velocities of left and right wheels. | The control output of the wandering algorithm. |
| **Braitenberg Coupling** | Direct excitatory/inhibitory wiring between sensors and motors. | Classical cognitive robotics paradigm for wandering. |

> [!TIP]
> **Cognitive Robotics Insight:** Wandering is considered *embodied cognition* — intelligence emerges from the body's interaction with the environment, not from internal symbolic reasoning. This is why no map or planner is needed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sensorimotor Loop Architecture

The wandering program is built on a four-stage reactive loop executed at every control cycle (typically every **20–100 ms**):

1. **Sense Stage** — Acquire readings from all proximity sensors into $\mathbf{S}(t)$.
2. **Compute Stage** — Apply the coupling function $f(\cdot)$ to determine desired wheel velocities.
3. **Act Stage** — Send PWM/motor commands to left and right wheel actuators.
4. **Wait Stage** — Sleep for the control period $\Delta t$, then return to Step 1.

## 2.2 Braitenberg Vehicle Formalism

Valentino Braitenberg's 1984 vehicles provide the mathematical backbone for wandering. For a two-wheeled differential-drive robot with two front sensors (left $S_L$, right $S_R$):

### Vehicle 2a — *Coward* (Fear Response)
The robot flees from obstacles. Stronger sensor reading → stronger wheel speed on the *same* side, but **inhibitory** (negative coupling) so the wheel spins faster *backwards* or slows.

$$
v_L = v_{\max} - \alpha \cdot S_L
$$
$$
v_R = v_{\max} - \alpha \cdot S_R
$$

### Vehicle 2b — *Aggressive/Love* Response (Approach)
Stronger sensor reading on the same side produces **excitatory** (positive) coupling, accelerating toward the source.

$$
v_L = \alpha \cdot S_L
$$
$$
v_R = \alpha \cdot S_R
$$

### Vehicle 3 — *Explorer* (Asymmetric Coupling — Used for Wandering)
Cross-coupling: left sensor excites the *right* wheel, and right sensor excites the *left* wheel. The robot approaches obstacles but turns smoothly before collision — producing the characteristic wandering trajectory.

$$
v_L = v_{\text{base}} + \alpha \cdot S_R
$$
$$
v_R = v_{\text{base}} + \alpha \cdot S_L
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Vehicle 3 wanders:** Because both wheels always move forward ($v_{\text{base}} > 0$), the robot never stops. The cross-coupling creates a smooth turn toward open space whenever an obstacle is detected on one side, yielding a continuous, space-filling trajectory.

## 2.3 Obstacle Avoidance Geometry

When a sensor reading $S_i$ exceeds a **threshold** $\tau$, a turn is triggered. The minimum safe turning radius is:

$$
r_{\min} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_{\max} + v_{\min}}{v_{\max} - v_{\min}}
$$

where $L$ is the wheelbase and $v_{\max}$, $v_{\min}$ are the maximum and minimum wheel velocities.

The instantaneous angular velocity of the robot is:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}
$$

## 2.4 Coverage Performance Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| **Area Coverage $C_A$** | $C_A = \dfrac{A_{\text{covered}}}{A_{\text{workspace}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of free space visited. |
| **Path Efficiency $\eta$** | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{\text{covered}}}{L_{\text{path}} \cdot w_{\text{robot}}}$ | Useful area per unit path length. |
| **Mean Revisit Time $T_{\text{rev}}$** | $T_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{revisits}}}$ | Average time between successive visits to the same cell. |
| **Collision Count $N_c$** | Direct count over run time $T$ | Safety/reliability indicator. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous Vacuum Cleaners** (Roomba) — Reactive wandering with spiral and wall-following modes.
- **Warehouse AMRs** (Autonomous Mobile Robots) — Wander-to-find-charging-station fallback.
- **Planetary Rovers** — Opportunistic science wandering between planned waypoints.
- **Underwater ROVs** — Survey-mode exploration of coral reefs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Program Logic & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Program Structure (Algorithmic Pseudocode)

The wandering program is structured as an **infinite reactive loop**. Below is the complete algorithm explicitly written for KTU board reference:

```
ALGORITHM: Wandering_Within_Workspace
─────────────────────────────────────────
CONSTANTS
    V_MAX      ← 0.5        // max wheel speed (m/s)
    V_MIN      ← 0.0        // min wheel speed
    ALPHA      ← 0.3        // coupling gain
    V_BASE     ← 0.2        // baseline forward velocity
    THRESHOLD  ← 0.4        // obstacle detection threshold (m)
    DT         ← 0.1        // control loop period (s)

VARIABLES
    S_L, S_R   : REAL       // left and right sensor readings
    V_L, V_R   : REAL       // left and right wheel velocities
    t          : REAL       // elapsed time
BEGIN
    INITIALIZE robot_pose, sensors, motors
    t ← 0
    WHILE (simulation_active = TRUE) DO
        
        // STEP 1: SENSE
        S_L ← read_proximity_sensor(LEFT)
        S_R ← read_proximity_sensor(RIGHT)
        
        // STEP 2: COMPUTE  (Braitenberg Vehicle 3 cross-coupling)
        IF (S_L > THRESHOLD) OR (S_R > THRESHOLD) THEN
            // Obstacle detected — apply avoidance
            V_L ← V_BASE + ALPHA × S_R
            V_R ← V_BASE + ALPHA × S_L
        ELSE
            // Free space — straight-line wandering
            V_L ← V_BASE
            V_R ← V_BASE
        END_IF
        
        // STEP 3: CLAMP to physical limits
        V_L ← CLAMP(V_L, V_MIN, V_MAX)
        V_R ← CLAMP(V_R, V_MIN, V_MAX)
        
        // STEP 4: ACT
        SET_MOTOR_SPEED(LEFT_WHEEL,  V_L)
        SET_MOTOR_SPEED(RIGHT_WHEEL, V_R)
        
        // STEP 5: WAIT and update state
        SLEEP(DT)
        t ← t + DT
        LOG(S_L, S_R, V_L, V_R, t)
        
    END_WHILE
END
```

## 3.2 Line-by-Line Analytical Walkthrough

### Step 1 — Sense Phase
The robot polls its two front-facing infrared/ultrasonic sensors. Output is normalized distance in metres.

$$
S_L \in [0,\, S_{\max}], \qquad S_R \in [0,\, S_{\max}]
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Phase (The Core of Wandering)

When **no** obstacle is detected (the *ELSE* branch):

$$
V_L = V_R = V_{\text{base}} = 0.2 \text{ m/s}
$$

This produces straight-line motion — the robot "wanders forward."

When **an** obstacle is detected (the *IF* branch), the cross-coupling activates:

$$
V_L = V_{\text{base}} + \alpha \cdot S_R
$$
$$
V_R = V_{\text{base}} + \alpha \cdot S_L
$$

**Numerical Example.** Suppose $S_L = 0.7$ m, $S_R = 0.1$ m, $\alpha = 0.3$, $V_{\text{base}} = 0.2$ m/s:

$$
V_L = 0.2 + 0.3 \times 0.1 = 0.23 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
V_R = 0.2 + 0.3 \times 0.7 = 0.41 \text{ m/s}
$$

Because $V_R > V_L$, the robot **turns left**, away from the obstacle detected on its left side. This is the hallmark of wandering — *the obstacle itself steers the robot.*

### Step 3 — Clamping (Saturation)
Physical motors cannot exceed $V_{\max}$ or fall below $V_{\min}$:

$$
V_L \leftarrow \max\!\bigl(V_{\min},\ \min(V_L,\, V_{\max})\bigr)
$$
$$
V_R \leftarrow \max\!\bigl(V_{\min},\ \min(V_R,\, V_{\max})\bigr)
$$

### Step 4 — Actuation
The clamped velocities are dispatched to the motor drivers.

### Step 5 — Time Update & Logging
After sleeping $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, the loop iterates. Logging is mandatory for post-hoc coverage analysis.

## 3.3 State Machine Equivalent

The wandering algorithm is equivalently described as a **3-state finite state machine**:

| State | Entry Condition | Action | Exit Condition |
|-------|-----------------|--------|----------------|
| **FORWARD** | $S_L < \tau$ AND $S_R < \tau$ | Both wheels at $V_{\text{base}}$ | Either sensor $\geq \tau$ |
| **TURN** | $S_L \geq \tau$ OR $S_R \geq \tau$ | Cross-couple to produce angular velocity $\omega$ | Both sensors $<\tau$ |
| **STOP** | Emergency flag or goal reached | $V_L = V_R = 0$ | Reset |

The angular velocity during the TURN state is:

$$
\omega = \frac{V_R - V_L}{L} = \frac{\alpha\,(S_L - S_R)}{L}
$$

When $S_L > S_R$ (obstacle on the left), $\omega > 0$ → counter-clockwise rotation → robot turns **right**, away from the threat.

## 3.4 Parameter Tuning Sensitivity

For guaranteed wandering (no robot stuck in loops), the coupling gain must satisfy:

$$
\alpha \cdot S_{\max} < V_{\max} - V_{\text{base}}
$$

Otherwise the right wheel saturates and the robot loses turning authority — a common KTU practical exam pitfall.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — Wandering Behavior

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FORWARD
    FORWARD --> TURN : S_L >= tau OR S_R >= tau
    TURN --> FORWARD : S_L < tau AND S_R < tau
    FORWARD --> STOP : emergency OR goal_reached
    TURN --> STOP : emergency OR goal_reached
    STOP --> FORWARD : reset_command
    STOP --> [*]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Chart — Program Execution Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Simulation] --> B[Initialize Sensors and Motors]
    B --> C[Read Left Sensor S_L]
    C --> D[Read Right Sensor S_R]
    D --> E{Obstacle Detected?}
    E -- No --> F[V_L = V_base; V_R = V_base]
    E -- Yes --> G[V_L = V_base + alpha x S_R]
    G --> H[V_R = V_base + alpha x S_L]
    F --> I[Clamp Velocities]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Send to Motors]
    J --> K[Sleep DT Seconds]
    K --> L{Simulation Active?}
    L -- Yes --> C
    L -- No --> M[Stop and Log Results]
    M --> N[End]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sensorimotor Coupling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Environment [Workspace Environment]
        OBJ1[Obstacle Left]
        OBJ2[Obstacle Right]
        FREES[Free Space Ahead]
    end

    subgraph Sensing [Sensing Layer]
        SL[Left Proximity Sensor]
        SR[Right Proximity Sensor]
    end

    subgraph Cognition [Reactive Controller Braitenberg Vehicle 3]
        CL[Coupling Matrix f]
    end

    subgraph Action [Actuation Layer]
        ML[Left Motor]
        MR[Right Motor]
    end

    OBJ1 --> SL
    OBJ2 --> SR
    SL --> CL
    SR --> CL
    CL --> ML
    CL --> MR
    ML --> FREES
    MR --> FREES
    FREES -.feedback.-> SL
    FREES -.feedback.-> SR
```

## 4.4 Physical Robot Schematic (Adapted as Block Architecture)

For the KTU lab-rat wheeled robot (commonly used in PERUT863 practicals), the wandering program interacts with the following hardware modules:

| Hardware Module | Function | Program Interface |
|-----------------|----------|-------------------|
| **IR Sensor Array (x5)** | Detect obstacles in a 180° arc | `read_ir(sensor_id)` |
| **DC Geared Motors (x2)** | Differential drive | `set_motor(motor_id, speed)` |
| **Wheel Encoders (x2)** | Odometry for pose estimation | `get_encoder(motor_id)` |
| **Microcontroller (Arduino/STM32)** | Hosts the wandering loop | Main `loop()` function |
| **Power Supply (Li-ion 11.1 V)** | Energy source | Hardware-only |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define "wandering behavior" in the context of cognitive robotics. How does it differ from planned path execution?**

**Model Answer:**
Wandering behavior is a reactive, sensor-driven exploration strategy in which a robot autonomously traverses its workspace without a pre-computed trajectory, instead generating motion commands in real time from proximity sensor readings. Unlike planned path execution — which relies on a known map, global path planner (e.g., A*), and goal specification — wandering requires no internal map, no goal, and produces emergent trajectories through Braitenberg-style sensorimotor coupling. It is the canonical embodiment of *embodied cognition*, where intelligent-looking behavior arises from the body's direct interaction with the environment.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for contrast with planning, 1 for embodied-cognition insight)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the role of Braitenberg Vehicle 3 in workspace wandering. Why is cross-coupling essential?**

**Model Answer:**
Braitenberg Vehicle 3 implements wandering through asymmetric cross-coupling between sensors and motors: the left sensor drives the *right* wheel and vice versa. When an obstacle appears on the left, the left sensor reading rises, which *increases* the right wheel speed, causing the robot to turn smoothly *away* from the threat. Cross-coupling is essential because symmetric (same-side) coupling produces either aggressive approach or fearful retreat — not the smooth, continuous, space-filling trajectory characteristic of wandering. The asymmetry creates a balanced avoidance that perpetually turns the robot toward open space without halting forward motion.

*(3 marks: 1 for Vehicle 3 description, 1 for cross-coupling equation, 1 for behavioral justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Design the complete pseudocode for a wandering program on a two-wheeled differential-drive robot equipped with two front infrared sensors. Specify all constants, the reactive loop, and the obstacle-avoidance logic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot uses Vehicle 3 wandering with $V_{\text{base}} = 0.2$ m/s, $\alpha = 0.4$, and sensor range $S_{\max} = 1.0$ m. At an instant, $S_L = 0.8$ m and $S_R = 0.3$ m. Compute the resulting wheel velocities, the angular velocity $\omega$, and the turning radius. Take wheelbase $L = 0.3$ m. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pseudocode Design (7 marks)**

```text
PROGRAM: Wandering_Within_Workspace
─────────────────────────────────────
1.  Define constants:
        V_MAX = 0.5, V_MIN = 0.0
        V_BASE = 0.2, ALPHA = 0.4
        TAU = 0.5, DT = 0.1 s

2.  Initialize motors, sensors, timer t = 0.

3.  REPEAT every DT seconds:
        3.1  S_L ← ReadLeftIRSensor()
        3.2  S_R ← ReadRightIRSensor()
        3.3  IF (S_L > TAU) OR (S_R > TAU):
                 V_L ← V_BASE + ALPHA × S_R
                 V_R ← V_BASE + ALPHA × S_L
             ELSE:
                 V_L ← V_BASE
                 V_R ← V_BASE
        3.4  V_L ← CLAMP(V_L, V_MIN, V_MAX)
             V_R ← CLAMP(V_R, V_MIN, V_MAX)
        3.5  SetLeftMotor(V_L)
             SetRightMotor(V_R)
        3.6  t ← t + DT
        3.7  Log(S_L, S_R, V_L, V_R, t)

4.  UNTIL simulation ends.
```

*(Mark split: 2 for constants/init, 3 for loop with branching logic, 2 for clamping & logging.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $V_{\text{base}} = 0.2$ m/s, $\alpha = 0.4$, $S_L = 0.8$ m, $S_R = 0.3$ m, $L = 0.3$ m.

**Step 1 — Wheel Velocities (Vehicle 3 cross-coupling):**

$$
V_L = V_{\text{base}} + \alpha \cdot S_R = 0.2 + 0.4 \times 0.3
$$
$$
V_L = 0.2 + 0.12 = 0.32 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_R = V_{\text{base}} + \alpha \cdot S_L = 0.2 + 0.4 \times 0.8
$$
$$
V_R = 0.2 + 0.32 = 0.52 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular Velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{V_R - V_L}{L} = \frac{0.52 - 0.32}{0.3}
$$
$$
\omega = \frac{0.20}{0.3} = 0.667 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Turning Radius (Instantaneous Centre of Rotation):**

$$
r = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{V_R + V_L}{V_R - V_L} = \frac{0.3}{2} \cdot \frac{0.52 + 0.32}{0.52 - 0.32}
$$
$$
r = 0.15 \times \frac{0.84}{0.20} = 0.15 \times 4.2
$$
$$
\boxed{r = 0.63 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification of Clamping:**
Since $V_R = 0.52 > V_{\max} = 0.5$, we **must clamp**:

$$
V_R \leftarrow 0.5 \text{ m/s}
$$

Re-computing with clamped value:

$$
\omega_{\text{clamped}} = \frac{0.50 - 0.32}{0.3} = 0.60 \text{ rad/s}
$$
$$
r_{\text{clamped}} = 0.15 \times \frac{0.82}{0.18} \approx 0.683 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion:** The robot will turn **right** (away from the larger left-side reading) with a turning radius of approximately **0.683 m**.

*(Mark split: 2 for velocities, 2 for angular velocity, 2 for radius, 1 for clamping correction.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Compare and contrast Braitenberg Vehicles 2a, 2b, and 3 with respect to their sensorimotor wiring, resulting motion behavior, and suitability for workspace wandering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a 5-minute wandering trial, a robot covers 38 m² of a 50 m² workspace. It logs 412 sensor events and triggers 23 avoidance maneuvers, with 2 collisions recorded. Compute (i) area coverage $C_A$, (ii) mean revisit density $\rho_{\text{rev}}$ if each cell is visited on average 4.2 times, and (iii) the collision rate per minute. Comment on the wandering quality. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Property | Vehicle 2a (Coward) | Vehicle 2b (Love/Aggressive) | Vehicle 3 (Explorer) |
|----------|---------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Wiring** | Same-side inhibitory ($-$) | Same-side excitatory ($+$) | Cross-side excitatory ($+$) |
| **Sensor–Motor Map** | $v_L = v_{\max} - \alpha S_L$, $v_R = v_{\max} - \alpha S_R$ | $v_L = \alpha S_L$, $v_R = \alpha S_R$ | $v_L = v_{\text{base}} + \alpha S_R$, $v_R = v_{\text{base}} + \alpha S_L$ |
| **Behaviour** | Flees from obstacles | Approaches obstacles | Wanders smoothly |
| **Stops?** | Yes (at far obstacle) | Yes (at obstacle) | No — always moves forward |
| **Suitability for Wandering** | Poor — robot stagnates | Poor — collides | **Excellent** — canonical wandering |

*(Mark split: 2 for wiring diagrams, 3 for behaviour comparison, 2 for suitability judgement.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $A_{\text{covered}} = 38$ m², $A_{\text{workspace}} = 50$ m², $T = 5$ min, $N_{\text{events}} = 412$, $N_{\text{avoid}} = 23$, $N_{\text{collisions}} = 2$, mean revisits per cell = 4.2.

**(i) Area Coverage:**

$$
C_A = \frac{A_{\text{covered}}}{A_{\text{workspace}}} \times 100\% = \frac{38}{50} \times 100\%
$$
$$
\boxed{C_A = 76\%}
$$

**(ii) Mean Revisit Density:**
Let $N_{\text{cells}} = A_{\text{covered}} / a_{\text{cell}}$. With $a_{\text{cell}} = 0.25$ m² (assumed 0.5 m × 0.5 m grid):

$$
N_{\text{cells}} = \frac{38}{0.25} = 152 \text{ cells}
$$

Total revisits:

$$
N_{\text{total visits}} = 152 \times 4.2 = 638.4
$$

Revisit density per m² per minute:

$$
\rho_{\text{rev}} = \frac{N_{\text{total visits}}}{A_{\text{workspace}} \times T} = \frac{638.4}{50 \times 5}
$$
$$
\boxed{\rho_{\text{rev}} = 2.55 \text{ visits/m²/min}}
$$

**(iii) Collision Rate:**

$$
R_c = \frac{N_{\text{collisions}}}{T} = \frac{2}{5}
$$
$$
\boxed{R_c = 0.4 \text{ collisions/min}}
$$

**Quality Comment (1 mark):** The 76% area coverage with only 0.4 collisions/min indicates **good** wandering quality. A collision rate below 1/min is acceptable for reactive wandering. However, the high revisit density (2.55) suggests the robot is circling in some regions — tuning $\alpha$ upward or adding wall-following fallback would improve coverage uniformity.

*(Mark split: 2 for coverage, 2 for revisit density, 2 for collision rate, 1 for qualitative comment.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PERUT863 Wandering Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clamp velocities** — students compute $V_R > V_{\max}$ and proceed without saturation, losing 1–2 marks and producing physically impossible trajectories.
> 2. **Confusing Vehicle 2a (same-side inhibitory) with Vehicle 3 (cross-side excitatory)** — these are *opposite* wirings with opposite behaviors. Read the coupling equation carefully.
> 3. **Using absolute value of $\omega$ in radius formula** — the sign of $\omega$ indicates turn direction; the radius itself is positive, but direction (CW/CCW) must be stated.
> 4. **Omitting the baseline velocity $V_{\text{base}}$** in the cross-coupling equation. Vehicle 3 only "wander" when both wheels always move forward.
> 5. **Not specifying threshold units** — KTU examiners expect sensor thresholds in **metres**, not raw ADC values.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Wandering** = reactive sensor-driven exploration without a map.
- [x] Built on the **sensorimotor loop**: Sense → Compute → Act → Wait.
- [x] **Braitenberg Vehicle 3** with cross-coupling is the canonical implementation.
- [x] Coupling equations: $V_L = V_{\text{base}} + \alpha S_R$, $V_R = V_{\text{base}} + \alpha S_L$.
- [x] **Clamping** is mandatory to respect physical motor limits.
- [x] Turning radius: $r = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{V_R + V_L}{V_R - V_L}$.
- [x] Angular velocity: $\omega = \dfrac{V_R - V_L}{L}$.
- [x] Performance measured by **area coverage**, **collision rate**, and **revisit density**.
- [x] Tunable parameters: $V_{\text{base}}$, $\alpha$, $\tau$, $\Delta t$.
- [x] Embodied cognition principle: intelligent behavior emerges from body–environment coupling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program for Tele-operation. A Complete Program for Autonomous Mobile Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT863 - COGNITIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program for Tele-operation. A Complete Program for Autonomous Mobile Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tele-operation: Formal Academic Definition

**Tele-operation** is a form of remote robot control in which a human operator issues motion and action commands to a robotic agent that is physically located at a distance, with continuous (or near-continuous) feedback exchanged over a communication channel. The robot is *semi-autonomous* — it does not plan its own high-level goals, but it may execute low-level stabilization, safety reflexes, and sensor pre-processing autonomously.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PERUT863), tele-operation is positioned on the **autonomy spectrum** between *tele-presence* (pure human-in-the-loop control) and *full autonomy* (no human input). The defining attributes are:

- A **human operator** as the cognitive decision-maker.
- A **master console** that encodes operator intent into commands.
- A **slave robot** that physically executes the task.
- A **feedback channel** carrying sensory information back to the operator (video, force, audio).
- A **latency-prone communication link** (often wireless, sometimes satellite).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PERUT863, Module 4):** Tele-operation is the *bridge technology* between human cognition and remote actuation. It is critical when full autonomy is infeasible (e.g., nuclear cleanup, deep-sea, planetary rovers) or unsafe for the human (EOD — Explosive Ordnance Disposal).

## 1.2 Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR): Formal Definition

An **Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR)** is a robotic platform capable of:

1. **Perceiving** its environment via onboard sensors (LIDAR, cameras, IMU, ultrasonic, wheel encoders).
2. **Localizing** itself within a known or unknown map.
3. **Planning** a trajectory from its current pose to a goal pose.
4. **Executing** motion while dynamically avoiding obstacles.
5. **Recovering** from faults and uncertainties — all without continuous human intervention.

The **"program"** for an AMR is therefore not a single linear script — it is a structured, layered software architecture combining perception, cognition, and actuation loops.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Remote-Control Car and the Self-Driving Taxi"

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Translation |
|---|---|---|
| **Tele-operation** | A child steering a toy car via a wireless remote; the child *sees* through an onboard camera | Operator + slave robot + feedback channel |
| **Full Autonomy** | A Waymo taxi navigating from airport to home with zero driver input | AMR with onboard cognition |
| **Hybrid (Shared Autonomy)** | A Tesla driver engaging Autopilot but keeping hands near the wheel | Adjustable autonomy level (LOA) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Intuition (Plain English):** Think of tele-operation as *playing a video game where your controller moves a real physical robot in a real room miles away*. You make every decision; the robot only obeys. An autonomous mobile robot, in contrast, is like giving the robot *its own brain, eyes, and GPS* — you only tell it *where to go*, and it figures out *how*.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of an AMR Program

Every KTU-aligned AMR software stack rests on three architectural pillars:

1. **Sense → Plan → Act (SPA) paradigm** — the classical AI-robotics loop.
2. **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986)** — layered reactive behaviors, where higher layers subsume lower ones.
3. **Hybrid Deliberative/Reactive** — combines slow high-level planning with fast low-level reflexes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** *Tele-operated robot* = human in the *control loop*. *Autonomous robot* = human in the *supervisory loop only*. *Semi-autonomous* = human intervenes on exception only.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Tele-operation Program

A tele-operation system is best modelled as a **closed-loop bilateral control system**. The canonical block diagram consists of:

1. **Operator (Human Model):** Generates motion commands based on task and feedback.
2. **Master Device (Haptic Joystick / Exoskeleton):** Encodes operator hand motion into position/velocity signals.
3. **Communication Channel:** Carries forward (command) and backward (feedback) packets.
4. **Slave Robot:** Receives commands, drives motors, and reads local sensors.
5. **Feedback Sensors:** Encoders, force/torque sensors, cameras, microphones.

The **transfer function** of a tele-operation channel must consider **time delay** ($\tau$) and **packet loss**. For a constant delay $\tau$, the round-trip behaviour approximates a **Padé approximant** of order $(1,1)$:

$$
G_{\text{delay}}(s) \;=\; e^{-\tau s} \;\approx\; \frac{1 - \tfrac{\tau}{2} s}{1 + \tfrac{\tau}{2} s}
$$

## 2.2 Stability of Bilateral Tele-operation

The classical result by **Anderson and Spong (1989)** shows that naive delayed force feedback destabilizes the closed loop. The fix is **passivity-based scattering**:

$$
V_{\text{out}}(t) \;=\; \frac{F(t) + \tau \dot{X}(t)}{\sqrt{2}}, \qquad
V_{\text{in}}(t) \;=\; \frac{-F(t - \tau) + \tau \dot{X}(t - \tau)}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

The **passivity condition** requires the net energy stored in the network to be non-increasing:

$$
E_{\text{stored}}(t) \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} P_{\text{in}}(\sigma)\, d\sigma \;\geq\; \int_{0}^{t} P_{\text{out}}(\sigma)\, d\sigma
$$

## 2.3 Tele-operation Control Architectures — Comparison Table

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table directly for KTU 2-mark / 3-mark comparison questions.

| Architecture | Forward Path | Return Path | Stability under Delay | Transparency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Open-Loop)** | Position command | None | Stable but unsafe | Poor (no force feel) |
| **Position-Position** | Position | Position | Unstable for $\tau > 0.5\,\text{s}$ | Medium |
| **Position-Force** | Position | Force | Marginally stable | High |
| **Force-Position** | Force | Position | Sensitive to delay | High |
| **Four-Channel** | Force + Position | Force + Position | Most stable | Highest |
| **Scattering / Wave-Variable** | Wave variables | Wave variables | Passivity-guaranteed | High |

## 2.4 Anatomy of a Complete AMR Program

A complete KTU-style AMR program is decomposed into **seven functional layers**, each with a clear input/output contract:

| Layer | Module Name | Inputs | Outputs | Rate (Hz) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L7 | Mission Manager | Goal string | High-level task list | 1 |
| L6 | Global Planner | Start, Goal, Map | Geometric path | 1–5 |
| L5 | Local Planner (DWA / TEB) | Path, Scan | Velocity commands | 10–20 |
| L4 | Behaviour Coordinator | Behaviors | Arbitration output | 20–50 |
| L3 | Motion Controller | $v$, $\omega$ setpoints | Wheel voltages | 50–200 |
| L2 | Sensor Driver | Raw sensor I/O | Filtered measurements | 50–1000 |
| L1 | Hardware Abstraction | Motor PWM, ADC | Actuator setpoints | 1000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Formulae and Constants**

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v$ | Linear velocity of robot base | $v = r \cdot \omega_{\text{wheel}}$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{v_{R} - v_{L}}{L}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| $r$ | Wheel radius | given parameter | $\text{m}$ |
| $L$ | Wheelbase | given parameter | $\text{m}$ |
| $\tau$ | Communication delay | $\tau = \tau_{f} + \tau_{b}$ | $\text{s}$ |
| $f_{\text{ctrl}}$ | Control loop frequency | $f_{\text{ctrl}} = \tfrac{1}{T_{s}}$ | $\text{Hz}$ |
| $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta$ | Pose update (odometry) | $\Delta x = v \cos\theta \cdot \Delta t$ | $\text{m, rad}$ |
| $\mathcal{R}$ | Rotation matrix 2D | $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$ | — |
| $Z$ | Battery level threshold | $Z_{\text{min}} = 0.20$ (typical) | fraction |
| $d_{\text{safe}}$ | Safe obstacle distance | $d_{\text{safe}} \geq 0.30$ | $\text{m}$ |
| $P_{\text{obs}}$ | Obstacle probability | $0 \leq P_{\text{obs}} \leq 1$ | dimensionless |
| $\lambda$ | Wave impedance (scattering) | $\lambda > 0$ | $\text{Ns/m}$ |
| $\rho$ | Path smoothness weight | tuned parameter | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap in Markdown:** In KTU exam scripts and answer sheets, write the determinant / absolute value as `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` — **never** the bare `|` character inside a table — because the pipe collides with LaTeX environments and the markdown table delimiter.

## 2.5 The Sense–Plan–Act Paradigm

The classical **SPA loop** is the foundation on which most AMR curricula, including KTU's, are based. Each iteration executes:

1. **Sense:** Read encoders, LIDAR, IMU, camera.
2. **Plan:** Update world model, recompute path, select behaviour.
3. **Act:** Send velocity commands to motor controller.

The loop period $T_{\text{SPA}}$ must satisfy the Nyquist criterion relative to the fastest dynamic:

$$
T_{\text{SPA}} \;\leq\; \frac{1}{2 f_{\text{max}}}
$$

## 2.6 Subsumption Architecture — Brooks' Layered Reactive Model

Rodney Brooks (1986) proposed that complex robot behaviour need not arise from complex central planners. Instead, **layers of competence** are stacked:

- **Layer 0:** Avoid contact with objects (reflexive).
- **Layer 1:** Wander without hitting obstacles.
- **Layer 2:** Explore by reaching "interesting" places.
- **Layer 3:** Build maps of the environment.
- **Layer 4:** Notice changes using perception.
- **Layer 5:** Categorize objects and reason about tasks.
- **Layer 6:** Plan and execute multi-step tasks.

Higher layers can **subsume** (override) the output of lower layers through *suppression* and *inhibition* signals.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Where These Systems Live

- **Industrial AGVs / AMRs (Amazon Kiva, Locus Robotics)** — warehouses, hospitals.
- **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance, ESA Rosalind Franklin)** — tele-operated when far, autonomous when near science targets.
- **Defence & EOD (TALON, PackBot)** — tele-operated bomb disposal.
- **Search and Rescue (Inuktun, Survivor Buddy)** — semi-autonomous rubble navigation.
- **Underwater ROVs (Jason, Hercules)** — tele-operated deep-sea archaeology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential-Drive Odometry Update

A differential-drive AMR computes its pose $(x, y, \theta)$ incrementally from wheel encoder readings. Let:

- $v_{L}, v_{R}$ = left and right wheel linear velocities (m/s).
- $r$ = wheel radius (m).
- $L$ = distance between wheel contact points (m).
- $\Delta t$ = sampling period (s).

**Step 1 — Linear and angular velocity of the robot base:**

$$
v \;=\; \frac{v_{R} + v_{L}}{2}
$$

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{v_{R} - v_{L}}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) geometry:**

For a non-zero $\omega$, the robot moves along an arc of radius:

$$
R_{\text{ICR}} \;=\; \frac{v}{\omega} \;=\; \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_{R} + v_{L}}{v_{R} - v_{L}}
$$

**Step 3 — Discretize the arc into a chord for small $\Delta t$:**

The arc length is $s = v \cdot \Delta t$, and the heading change is $\Delta \theta = \omega \cdot \Delta t$. Using the small-angle chord approximation:

$$
\Delta x \;\approx\; v \cos\theta \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
\Delta y \;\approx\; v \sin\theta \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
\Delta \theta \;\approx\; \omega \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 4 — Update the pose vector via Euler integration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_{k} + v \cos\theta_{k} \cdot \Delta t \\
y_{k+1} &= y_{k} + v \sin\theta_{k} \cdot \Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_{k} + \omega \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — The exact arc-displacement formula (preferred for KTU derivations):**

When $\omega \neq 0$, the exact centre-of-arc update is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_{k} + \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\sin(\theta_{k} + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta_{k}\bigr] \\
y_{k+1} &= y_{k} - \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\cos(\theta_{k} + \omega\Delta t) - \cos\theta_{k}\bigr] \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_{k} + \omega \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic — Why two forms?** The Euler form is *linear* and fast but introduces drift over long arcs. The exact form is *non-linear* in $\theta$ and is curvature-correct; it is the gold standard in ROS `amcl` and most AMR stacks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Wave-Variable Transformation (Stability Proof Sketch)

**Step 1 — Define wave variables** from force $F$ and velocity $\dot{X}$ at port $i$:

$$
u_{i}(t) \;=\; \frac{F_{i}(t) + \lambda \dot{X}_{i}(t)}{\sqrt{2\lambda}}, \qquad
v_{i}(t) \;=\; \frac{F_{i}(t) - \lambda \dot{X}_{i}(t)}{\sqrt{2\lambda}}
$$

Here $\lambda > 0$ is the **characteristic wave impedance**, in $\text{Ns/m}$.

**Step 2 — Energy transported by a wave packet:**

$$
P(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\bigl[u^{2}(t) - v^{2}(t)\bigr] \cdot \lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Scattering at the receiving end:**

$$
v_{\text{out}}(t) \;=\; u_{\text{in}}(t - \tau)
$$

**Step 4 — Total stored energy in the delay line:**

$$
E(t) \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{2} \int_{t-\tau}^{t} \bigl[u^{2}(\sigma) + v^{2}(\sigma)\bigr] \, d\sigma
$$

**Step 5 — Passivity condition:**

Since $E(t) \geq 0$ and $dE/dt \leq P_{\text{ext}}$, no energy is created by the network — hence the closed-loop bilateral system is **BIBO stable** for *any* $\tau$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour Arbitration Strategies

| Strategy | Mathematical Form | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Priority (Subsumption)** | $\text{out} = \arg\max_{i}\, w_{i} \cdot b_{i}(t)$ | Simple, deterministic | No fusion of behaviours |
| **Weighted Sum** | $\text{out} = \sum_{i} \alpha_{i} v_{i},\ \sum \alpha_{i}=1$ | Smooth transitions | Tuning is hard |
| **Vector-Field (Potential Field)** | $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} + \sum \mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}}$ | Mathematically clean | Local minima traps |
| **Fuzzy Logic** | $\text{out} = \text{defuzz}(\mu_{i}(s))$ | Handles uncertainty | Rule explosion |
| **DWA (Dynamic Window)** | $(v,\omega) = \arg\max G(v,\omega)$ | Dynamic constraints respected | Myopic horizon |
| **Decision Tree / FSM** | $\text{state} \to \text{action}$ | Auditable | Combinatorial |

## 3.4 The Complete AMR Program — Step-by-Step Functional Walkthrough

The "Complete Program for Autonomous Mobile Robots" required by KTU Module 4 is structured as a **finite-state supervisor** over cooperating behaviours. Below is the exhaustive pseudocode walkthrough — no syntax is skipped, every branch is explicit, and every variable carries a comment.

**Phase 1 — System Initialization**

1. Power-on self-test: battery voltage, IMU calibration, encoder zeroing.
2. Load map from disk or initialize empty occupancy grid $M \in \{0, 0.5, 1\}^{N \times N}$.
3. Initialize pose estimate $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$ from fiducial markers or GPS.
4. Set global state $\mathcal{S} \leftarrow \text{IDLE}$.
5. Spawn publishers/subscribers (command velocity topic, scan topic, odom topic).

**Phase 2 — Main Loop (executed at fixed rate $f_{\text{ctrl}}$)**

For each tick $k$:

- **SENSE stage** (Layer L2):
  - Read LIDAR scan $S_k = \{r_i, \phi_i\}_{i=1}^{N_b}$.
  - Read wheel odometry $(\Delta s_L, \Delta s_R)$.
  - Read IMU $(\dot{\theta}_{\text{imu}}, a_x, a_y)$.
- **LOCALIZE stage** (Layer L2→L3):
  - Fuse odometry + IMU using complementary filter.
  - Apply particle-filter correction against scan-match with map $M$.
- **PERCEIVE stage** (Layer L4):
  - Convert scan to local occupancy grid $M_{\text{loc}}$.
  - Extract obstacle clusters $\mathcal{O}_k = \{o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_m\}$.
  - Compute minimum obstacle distance $d_{\min} = \min_i \Vert o_i - p_k \Vert$.
- **PLAN stage** (Layer L5–L6):
  - If $\mathcal{S} = \text{NAVIGATE}$: invoke global planner $\text{A}^*$ from $p_k$ to $p_{\text{goal}}$.
  - Extract local sub-goal at look-ahead distance $d_{\text{la}}$ along the path.
- **ARBITRATE stage** (Layer L4):
  - Behaviour $b_{\text{avoid}}$ activates if $d_{\min} < d_{\text{safe}}$.
  - Behaviour $b_{\text{goal}}$ activates if $d_{\min} \geq d_{\text{safe}}$.
  - Behaviour $b_{\text{recover}}$ activates if pose uncertainty $\sigma_p > \sigma_{\text{thr}}$.
- **ACT stage** (Layer L3):
  - Compute $(v_k, \omega_k)$ from the dominant behaviour.
  - Saturate: $v_k \in [0, v_{\max}]$, $\omega_k \in [-\omega_{\max}, \omega_{\max}]$.
  - Convert to wheel commands:
    $$
    \omega_L = \frac{v_k - 0.5\,L\,\omega_k}{r}, \qquad
    \omega_R = \frac{v_k + 0.5\,L\,\omega_k}{r}
    $$
- **SUPERVISE stage** (Layer L7):
  - If battery $Z_k < Z_{\min}$: switch $\mathcal{S} \to \text{RETURN\_TO\_DOCK}$.
  - If goal reached ($\Vert p_k - p_{\text{goal}} \Vert < \epsilon$): switch $\mathcal{S} \to \text{IDLE}$.
  - Publish telemetry at $1\,\text{Hz}$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Odometry Computation

**Given:** Wheel encoder counts $\Delta s_L = 0.052\,\text{m}$, $\Delta s_R = 0.060\,\text{m}$ over $\Delta t = 0.1\,\text{s}$; $r = 0.05\,\text{m}$, $L = 0.30\,\text{m}$; previous pose $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k) = (1.00, 2.00, 0.000)\,\text{rad}$.

**Step 1 — Wheel velocities:**

$$
v_L = \frac{0.052}{0.1} = 0.520\,\text{m/s}, \qquad v_R = \frac{0.060}{0.1} = 0.600\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Base velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{0.520 + 0.600}{2} = 0.560\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{0.600 - 0.520}{0.30} = \frac{0.080}{0.30} = 0.2667\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Pose update (exact arc form):**

$$
\theta_{k+1} = 0.000 + 0.2667 \times 0.1 = 0.02667\,\text{rad}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= 1.00 + \frac{0.560}{0.2667}\bigl[\sin(0.02667) - \sin(0.000)\bigr] \\
&= 1.00 + 2.100 \times 0.02667 \\
&= 1.00 + 0.0560 = 1.0560\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{k+1} &= 2.00 - \frac{0.560}{0.2667}\bigl[\cos(0.02667) - \cos(0.000)\bigr] \\
&= 2.00 - 2.100 \times (0.99964 - 1.00000) \\
&= 2.00 - 2.100 \times (-0.00036) \\
&= 2.00 + 0.000745 = 2.000745\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (Euler approximation):**

$$
\Delta x \approx 0.560 \cdot \cos(0) \cdot 0.1 = 0.0560\,\text{m}, \quad \Delta y \approx 0
$$

The Euler and exact forms agree to within $7.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$ for this short interval.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tele-operation Block Diagram — Master-Slave with Delayed Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeOp[Human Operator]
    nodeMaster[Master Device Haptic Stick]
    nodeEncA[Forward Encoder Signal]
    nodeComm[Communication Channel Delay Tau]
    nodeSlave[Slave Robot Onboard CPU]
    nodeMotor[Motor Driver and Wheels]
    nodeEnv[Remote Environment]
    nodeSense[Force and Vision Sensors]
    nodeDec[Decoder and Reconstruction]
    nodeFB[Feedback Display Haptic and Video]

    nodeOp -->|intent| nodeMaster
    nodeMaster -->|position cmd| nodeEncA
    nodeEncA -->|packetized| nodeComm
    nodeComm -->|delayed| nodeSlave
    nodeSlave -->|low level ctrl| nodeMotor
    nodeMotor -->|physical motion| nodeEnv
    nodeEnv -->|reaction forces| nodeSense
    nodeSense -->|raw data| nodeDec
    nodeDec -->|tactile and visual| nodeFB
    nodeFB -->|perception| nodeOp
```

## 4.2 AMR Software Architecture — Layered Functional Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL7[Mission Manager Layer 7 Goal Queue]
    nodeL6[Global Planner Layer 6 A star on Grid]
    nodeL5[Local Planner Layer 5 DWA or TEB]
    nodeL4[Behavior Coordinator Layer 4 Arbitration]
    nodeL3[Motion Controller Layer 3 PID on v and omega]
    nodeL2[Sensor Fusion Layer 2 EKF or Particle Filter]
    nodeL1[Hardware Abstraction Layer 1 Drivers and PWM]

    nodeL7 -->|task| nodeL6
    nodeL6 -->|path| nodeL5
    nodeL5 -->|reference twist| nodeL4
    nodeL4 -->|selected twist| nodeL3
    nodeL3 -->|wheel PWM| nodeL1
    nodeL1 -->|encoder ticks| nodeL2
    nodeL2 -->|pose estimate| nodeL5
    nodeL2 -->|pose estimate| nodeL6
```

## 4.3 AMR Finite-State Supervisor — Top-Level Behaviour Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> nodeIDLE
    nodeIDLE --> nodeNAVIGATE : New goal received
    nodeNAVIGATE --> nodeAVOID : Obstacle within d safe
    nodeAVOID --> nodeNAVIGATE : Path clear
    nodeNAVIGATE --> nodeRECOVER : Localization lost sigma high
    nodeAVOID --> nodeRECOVER : Stuck timeout exceeded
    nodeRECOVER --> nodeIDLE : Recovery succeeded
    nodeNAVIGATE --> nodeDOCK : Battery below Z min
    nodeAVOID --> nodeDOCK : Battery below Z min
    nodeDOCK --> nodeIDLE : Charger contacted
    nodeNAVIGATE --> nodeIDLE : Goal reached within epsilon
    nodeIDLE --> [*] : Shutdown signal
```

## 4.4 Subsumption Architecture — Layered Competence Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL6[Layer 6 Plan and Reason]
    nodeL5[Layer 5 Recognize Objects]
    nodeL4[Layer 4 Detect Change in Map]
    nodeL3[Layer 3 Build and Maintain Map]
    nodeL2[Layer 2 Explore World]
    nodeL1[Layer 1 Wander Without Collision]
    nodeL0[Layer 0 Avoid Contact Reflex]

    nodeL6 -->|suppress| nodeL5
    nodeL5 -->|suppress| nodeL4
    nodeL4 -->|suppress| nodeL3
    nodeL3 -->|suppress| nodeL2
    nodeL2 -->|suppress| nodeL1
    nodeL1 -->|suppress| nodeL0

    nodeL0 -.-> nodeOut[Final Motor Output]
    nodeL1 -.-> nodeOut
    nodeL2 -.-> nodeOut
    nodeL3 -.-> nodeOut
    nodeL4 -.-> nodeOut
    nodeL5 -.-> nodeOut
    nodeL6 -.-> nodeOut
```

## 4.5 Sense–Plan–Act Loop — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSense1[LIDAR Scan]
    nodeSense2[Wheel Encoders]
    nodeSense3[IMU 9 axis]
    nodeFuse[Sensor Fusion EKF]
    nodeMap[Occupancy Grid Map]
    nodePlan[Global and Local Planner]
    nodeBeh[Behavior Arbitration]
    nodeCtrl[Motion Controller]
    nodeAct[Wheels and Motors]
    nodeEnv[Environment]
    nodeObs[Obstacles]

    nodeSense1 --> nodeFuse
    nodeSense2 --> nodeFuse
    nodeSense3 --> nodeFuse
    nodeFuse --> nodeMap
    nodeMap --> nodePlan
    nodePlan --> nodeBeh
    nodeBeh --> nodeCtrl
    nodeCtrl --> nodeAct
    nodeAct --> nodeEnv
    nodeEnv --> nodeObs
    nodeObs --> nodeSense1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PERUT863]**

### Question A1
**Explain the three components of a master-slave tele-operation system. Why is communication delay a critical issue, and how is it commonly handled?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A master-slave tele-operation system has three components: **(i)** the *master device* (haptic joystick, exoskeleton) which the human operator uses to issue commands; **(ii)** the *slave robot* which physically executes the task; and **(iii)** the *communication channel* that carries forward (command) and backward (sensor) packets between them.
Communication delay is critical because **delayed force feedback can destabilize the closed loop** — the operator feels an old force and over-corrects, causing oscillations. It is commonly handled using **wave-variable / scattering transformation** (Anderson & Spong, 1989) which guarantees **passivity** and hence stability for any delay $\tau$.

### Question A2
**List and briefly define the seven functional layers of a complete AMR software stack.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The seven layers are:
1. **Mission Manager (L7)** — accepts high-level goals, monitors completion.
2. **Global Planner (L6)** — computes a geometric path from current pose to goal using $\text{A}^*$ or Dijkstra on the map.
3. **Local Planner (L5)** — refines path into velocity commands avoiding dynamic obstacles (DWA, TEB).
4. **Behaviour Coordinator (L4)** — arbitrates between competing behaviours (avoid, seek, recover).
5. **Motion Controller (L3)** — low-level PID tracking of $(v, \omega)$.
6. **Sensor Fusion (L2)** — fuses LIDAR, encoder, IMU into pose estimate.
7. **Hardware Abstraction (L1)** — motor PWM drivers and ADC read-back.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A):** Students often write the layers in the wrong *order of execution* (downward) instead of *information flow* (sensory up, command down). Marks are lost. Memorize: **L7→L1 for commands, L1→L7 for feedback.**

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Model Solution — Question B-A

**(a) Tele-operation Architecture (7 marks):**

A **bilateral tele-operation system** is a closed-loop control system in which a human operator commands a remote robot through a master console, and receives sensory feedback (force, vision, audio) back through the same channel. The canonical block diagram is:

`Operator → Master Device → Encoder → Forward Channel (delay $\tau_f$) → Slave Robot → Environment → Sensors → Decoder → Feedback Channel (delay $\tau_b$) → Operator`

**Architecture comparison:**

| Architecture | Forward | Return | Transparency | Stability under delay |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Position-Position | Position | Position | Medium | Unstable for $\tau > 0.5\,\text{s}$ |
| Force-Position | Force | Position | High | Marginally stable |
| Four-Channel | Force + Position | Force + Position | Highest | Stable with proper tuning |

**Position-Position** sends master position $X_m$ to slave and returns slave position $X_s$ — operator feels the *position error*. It is transparent in free space but loses feel of contact forces.

**Force-Position** sends master *force* $F_m$ (admittance) and returns slave *position* $X_s$ — operator feels reaction forces directly but is sensitive to delay.

**Four-Channel** transmits both force and velocity in both directions using matched scaling factors $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$. It is the most general framework and can replicate any desired impedance — but requires precise modelling of operator and environment impedances. (Full 7 marks require the diagram, the table, and one sentence on each architecture.)

**(b) Wave-Variable Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1** — Define force $F$ and velocity $\dot{X}$ at port $i$. Construct wave variables:

$$
u_{i}(t) = \frac{F_{i}(t)}{\sqrt{2\lambda}} + \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2}}\,\dot{X}_{i}(t)
$$

$$
v_{i}(t) = \frac{F_{i}(t)}{\sqrt{2\lambda}} - \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2}}\,\dot{X}_{i}(t)
$$

**Step 2** — Invert to obtain force and velocity from waves:

$$
F_{i}(t) = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2}}\bigl[u_{i}(t) + v_{i}(t)\bigr]
$$

$$
\dot{X}_{i}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\lambda}}\bigl[u_{i}(t) - v_{i}(t)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3** — Compute the instantaneous power at the port:

$$
P_{i}(t) = F_{i}(t)\dot{X}_{i}(t) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[u_{i}^{2}(t) - v_{i}^{2}(t)\bigr]
$$

**Step 4** — Apply scattering across the delay line:

$$
u_{\text{slave}}(t) = v_{\text{master}}(t - \tau), \qquad u_{\text{master}}(t) = v_{\text{slave}}(t - \tau)
$$

**Step 5** — Energy stored in the delay line at time $t$:

$$
E(t) = \frac{1}{2}\int_{t-\tau}^{t}\bigl[u_{\text{master}}^{2}(\sigma) + v_{\text{master}}^{2}(\sigma)\bigr]\, d\sigma
$$

Since the integrand is a sum of squares, $E(t) \geq 0$ for all $t$.

**Step 6 — Passivity argument:** The rate of change of stored energy equals power input minus power output. Because waves only *travel* through the delay line (they are not amplified), the input power at one port equals the output power at the other. Therefore $dE/dt \leq P_{\text{ext}}(t)$, which is the **passivity condition**. A passive closed-loop system is BIBO stable for any delay $\tau$.

**(Award 7 marks:** 1 mark per step, with full credit when each step explicitly states the physical meaning.)

### Model Solution — Question B-B

**(a) Layered Architecture of AMR (7 marks):**

Draw the seven-layer diagram (L1 to L7) showing arrows as in **Section 4.2** above. Provide a one-sentence description of each layer's role and its inputs/outputs:

- **L7 Mission Manager** — Input: goal string. Output: task list. Role: high-level mission FSM.
- **L6 Global Planner** — Input: start, goal, static map. Output: geometric path. Role: $\text{A}^*$ / Dijkstra over grid.
- **L5 Local Planner** — Input: path + scan. Output: $(v, \omega)$ reference. Role: dynamic obstacle avoidance.
- **L4 Behaviour Coordinator** — Input: behaviour activations. Output: arbitrated twist. Role: subsumption or weighted sum.
- **L3 Motion Controller** — Input: $(v, \omega)$. Output: wheel voltages. Role: PID tracking with saturation.
- **L2 Sensor Fusion** — Input: raw sensor streams. Output: pose estimate + map. Role: EKF / particle filter.
- **L1 Hardware Abstraction** — Input: motor setpoints. Output: PWM, ADC. Role: device drivers.

**(b) Numerical Odometry (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r = 0.05\,\text{m}$, $L = 0.30\,\text{m}$, $\Delta t = 0.1\,\text{s}$, $\Delta s_L = 0.052\,\text{m}$, $\Delta s_R = 0.060\,\text{m}$, $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k) = (1.00, 2.00, 0.000)\,\text{rad}$.

**Step 1 — Wheel velocities:**

$$
v_L = \frac{\Delta s_L}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.052}{0.1} = 0.520\,\text{m/s}
$$

$$
v_R = \frac{\Delta s_R}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.060}{0.1} = 0.600\,\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Robot base linear and angular velocity:**

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.600 + 0.520}{2} = 0.560\,\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.080}{0.30} = 0.2667\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Heading update:**

$$
\theta_{k+1} = 0.000 + 0.2667 \times 0.1 = 0.02667\,\text{rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Position update using exact arc-displacement formula:**

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\sin(\theta_{k+1}) - \sin(\theta_k)\bigr]
$$

$$
x_{k+1} = 1.00 + \frac{0.560}{0.2667}\bigl[\sin(0.02667) - \sin(0.000)\bigr]
$$

$$
x_{k+1} = 1.00 + 2.100 \times (0.026660 - 0) = 1.00 + 0.0560 = 1.0560\,\text{m}
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = y_k - \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\cos(\theta_{k+1}) - \cos(\theta_k)\bigr]
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = 2.00 - 2.100 \times (0.99964 - 1.00000)
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = 2.00 - 2.100 \times (-0.00036) = 2.00 + 0.000745 = 2.000745\,\text{m}
$$

**Final answer:** $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}, \theta_{k+1}) = (1.0560\,\text{m},\ 2.0007\,\text{m},\ 0.02667\,\text{rad})$.

**(Award full 7 marks:** 2 marks for $v$ and $\omega$, 1 mark for $\theta$ update, 3 marks for $x$ and $y$ updates, 1 mark for the final boxed answer with units.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B — Numerical):**
> - **Most common error:** Forgetting that $\omega$ appears in the denominator of the arc-radius factor $\tfrac{v}{\omega}$. If $\omega = 0$, the formula degenerates and the student must use the **linear-motion limit** $\Delta x = v\cos\theta \Delta t$, $\Delta y = v\sin\theta \Delta t$. Failing to mention this loses 1–2 marks.
> - **Unit trap:** $\theta$ must be in **radians** when passed to $\sin$ and $\cos$. Converting to degrees silently yields wrong answers.
> - **Sign convention:** The minus sign in front of the cosine difference is critical; flipping it gives a mirrored trajectory. KTU examiners specifically test this sign.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Print this section before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **Tele-operation** = human in the *control loop*. **Autonomy** = human in the *supervisory loop only*.
- [x] The **master-slave** architecture has three blocks: master, communication channel, slave.
- [x] **Communication delay** destabilizes force feedback; fix is **wave-variable / scattering** transformation (Anderson & Spong, 1989).
- [x] **Passivity** is the energy-based stability guarantee — $E(t) \geq 0$ for all $t$.
- [x] **Four-channel bilateral control** is the most transparent and stable architecture.
- [x] An AMR program is structured as **7 layers**: Mission (L7) → Global Plan (L6) → Local Plan (L5) → Behaviour (L4) → Motion Ctrl (L3) → Sensor Fusion (L2) → HAL (L1).
- [x] **SPA loop** = Sense → Plan → Act. Must respect Nyquist relative to fastest dynamic.
- [x] **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks)** stacks layered competence; higher layers *suppress* lower ones.
- [x] Differential-drive **odometry**: $v = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_R + v_L)$, $\omega = \tfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$.
- [x] Exact arc-displacement formula is preferred over Euler linearization to avoid drift.
- [x] **Wheel command conversion:** $\omega_L = \tfrac{v - 0.5 L \omega}{r}$, $\omega_R = \tfrac{v + 0.5 L \omega}{r}$.
- [x] **Behaviour arbitration** strategies: priority (subsumption), weighted sum, potential field, fuzzy, DWA, FSM.
- [x] **Critical safety thresholds:** $d_{\text{safe}} \geq 0.30\,\text{m}$, $Z_{\min} = 0.20$, $\sigma_p < \sigma_{\text{thr}}$.
- [x] **Hybrid deliberative/reactive** AMR = slow planner + fast reactive layer = best engineering practice.
- [x] **Real-world examples:** NASA Perseverance (tele-op when far), Amazon Kiva (autonomous), TALON (EOD tele-op).
- [x] **Sign and unit traps** are the most common KTU valuation deductions — always state units and check $\theta$ in radians.
